No draft findings were refuted or downgraded. Per-platform status is recomputed: iOS minor (missing credit features, tz chip tokens are actually correct), Android major (missing credit features + hardcoded hero total color), web major (tz chip identity token mismatch + missing credit features).

findings.title: Android pending-approval dock: always shows 'Add to calendar' / 'Done' — missing 'Done' primary + 'Message host' secondary
findings.designSays: FramePending dock: primary = 'Done' (check icon), secondary = 'Message host' (booking-confirmed-frames.jsx line 477)
findings.implementationSays: ConfirmFooter receives no requiresApproval/isPending parameter; the ConfirmStep.Confirmed branch always renders 'Add to calendar' (FilledCta) + 'Done' (text-only secondary) unconditionally. ConfirmedData.requiresApproval is stored in state but never passed to the footer.
findings.evidence: Design: booking-confirmed-frames.jsx:477 `<Dock primary="Done" primaryIcon="check" secondary="Message host"/>`. Android: InviteeConfirmFlow.kt:257–266 shows ConfirmFooter signature has no isPending param; lines 281–299 show unconditional 'Add to calendar'/'Done' for ConfirmStep.Confirmed. ConfirmedScreen.kt:68 sets `val pending = confirmed.requiresApproval` but this is only used in the body content, not propagated to the dock. iOS: InviteeConfirmedView.swift:275–285 correctly branches on `viewModel.isPending`.

findings.title: Web manage link and NudgeCard accent hardcoded to Personal (sky) regardless of pillar
findings.designSays: ManageNote uses `PILLAR` for the 'Reschedule or cancel' link text (booking-confirmed-frames.jsx:279); NudgeCard icon uses `PILLAR` (line 340). The design defines PILLAR = E.blue600 and comments 'Host pillar = Personal sky on secondary chips' (line 11) — only one pillar variant (Personal) is shown across all 8 frames. No Home or Business pillar frames exist for this screen.
findings.implementationSays: ConfirmedView.tsx:372 hardcodes `text-app-personal` for the manage link; lines 382, 384, 396 hardcode `app-personal-*` for NudgeCard. `pillar` is computed at line 247 and passed to BookingSummaryCard (line 297) but not to these secondary elements.
findings.evidence: ConfirmedView.tsx:372 `<span className="font-bold text-app-personal">`. Lines 382, 384, 396 use `app-personal-*`. Design: booking-confirmed-frames.jsx:11 'Host pillar = Personal sky on secondary chips' — all 8 frames use single PILLAR constant. Downgraded from major: design intent is ambiguous — the comment may intentionally prescribe Personal sky for these secondary utility elements, not a per-pillar accent.

findings.title: iOS and Android show add-to-calendar cluster in pending-approval state — design omits it
findings.designSays: FramePending (booking-confirmed-frames.jsx:475–491) contains: Hero, Timeline, EtaPill, SummaryCard, ManageNote — no CalendarCluster. FrameConfirmedFree and all other confirmed frames do include CalendarCluster.
findings.implementationSays: iOS: InviteeConfirmedView.swift:63 renders CalendarClusterView unconditionally with no `if !viewModel.isPending` guard. Android: ConfirmedScreen.kt:93 renders CalendarCluster unconditionally (the `!pending` guard is only applied to AccountNudge at line 99, not to CalendarCluster). Web correctly guards AddToCalendar with `{!isPending && ...}` (ConfirmedView.tsx:347).
findings.evidence:
Design: booking-confirmed-frames.jsx:487 FramePending renders `` followed by `` with no CalendarCluster between them. iOS: InviteeConfirmedView.swift:63 unconditional `CalendarClusterView`. Android: ConfirmedScreen.kt:93 unconditional `CalendarCluster`. Web: ConfirmedView.tsx:347 `{!isPending && (
)}`.

screens.crossPlatformNotes: Web is closest to design: correctly branches calendar cluster on !isPending, correctly handles pending hero/timeline, and is the only platform with no major gaps. Android has two confirmed design-parity gaps: the dock ignores requiresApproval (major — dock always shows 'Add to calendar'/'Done' even for pending bookings that need 'Done'/'Message host'), and CalendarCluster shown unconditionally in pending state. iOS has one minor gap shared with Android: CalendarClusterView shown unconditionally in pending state. The pending-dock failure is Android-only since iOS correctly branches in its dock logic.
screens.verifierSummary: CONFIRMED (keeping): Finding 1 — Android pending-approval dock confirmed. ConfirmFooter at InviteeConfirmFlow.kt:257 has no isPending/requiresApproval parameter; the ConfirmStep.Confirmed branch unconditionally renders 'Add to calendar'/'Done'. requiresApproval is stored in ConfirmedData and read in ConfirmedScreen.kt:68 for the body, but never passed to the dock. iOS correctly branches at InviteeConfirmedView.swift:275. Finding confirmed high-confidence; kept as major. DOWNGRADED: Finding 2 — Web pillar hardcoding downgraded from major to minor. The design frame has only one set of frames all using PILLAR = E.blue600, and the design comment at line 11 explicitly says 'Host pillar = Personal sky on secondary chips.' No Home or Business pillar frames exist for this screen. The design intent for these secondary elements (manage link, NudgeCard) is ambiguous — they may intentionally use Personal sky. Confidence lowered to medium; severity to minor. ADDED: New finding — iOS and Android both render the add-to-calendar cluster unconditionally in pending state. FramePending in design has no CalendarCluster. Web correctly guards with !isPending. This affects iOS (InviteeConfirmedView.swift:63) and Android (ConfirmedScreen.kt:93). Minor severity, high confidence.

findings.title: Status badge: design shows icon+text filled pill; iOS/Android render text-only chip
findings.designSays: StatusBadge has a leading Lucide icon (check-circle-2 / history / x-circle) inside a colored pill with border — manage-booking-frames.jsx lines 95–109
findings.implementationSays: iOS SchedulingStatusPill (SchedulingStatusPill.swift:6–7) explicitly documents 'NO leading icon'; body renders only Text(label) with no Icon call (line 156). Android SchedulingStatusPill (SchedulingStatusPill.kt:23–27) explicitly documents 'Text-only chip grammar (NO leading icon)'; StatusPillChip renders only Text(...) (lines 158–179). Both mobile platforms deliberately omit the Lucide icon the design specifies.
findings.evidence: Design: manage-booking-frames.jsx:107 `<i data-lucide={map.icon} style={{ width:13, height:13, strokeWidth:2.3 }}/>{map.label}`. iOS: SchedulingStatusPill.swift:155–164 body is Text(label) only, no Icon(). Android: SchedulingStatusPill.kt:158–179 StatusPillChip is Text(...) only, no PantopusIconImage. InviteeManageBookingView.swift:66 calls SchedulingStatusPill(status:); ManageBookingScreen.kt:170 calls SchedulingStatusPill(status = data.status.toPillStatus()).

findings.title: Web: PolicyCard/contact-host card missing; CancellationPolicy component used instead with different layout and no contact affordance
findings.designSays: PolicyCard (info icon + policy sentence + pillar-accent 'Contact <host>' mail button) appears in frames 1 (confirmed), 2 (past: 'Booked a follow-up?'), and 4 (window-closed) — manage-booking-frames.jsx:222–237, 286, 328
findings.implementationSays: Web renders CancellationPolicy component (ManageBookingPanel.tsx:351–353) only when `isActive && page?.cancellation_policy`. CancellationPolicy.tsx:56–106 is a structured dl/dt/dd breakdown with Clock/RefreshCcw/Wallet icons and no 'Contact host' button. For the past frame no policy card renders at all (the `isActive` guard excludes past bookings), so the 'Booked a follow-up?' sentence from the design never appears. iOS policyCard() (InviteeManageBookingView.swift:230–261) correctly includes the info icon, policy text, and mail-icon Contact button, and renders for past state (line 87).
findings.evidence: Design: manage-booking-frames.jsx:223–236 (PolicyCard with mail 'Contact Maria' button); line 286 (past frame PolicyCard). Web: ManageBookingPanel.tsx:351 `isActive && page?.cancellation_policy` guard; ManageBookingPanel.tsx:352 `<CancellationPolicy policy={page.cancellation_policy}/>`. CancellationPolicy.tsx: no contact button, dl layout (lines 80–99).

findings.title: Web: token-expired CTA label is 'Go to Pantopus' and navigates to home, not 'Request a new link'
findings.designSays: Frame 5 (Token expired): primary CTA 'Request a new link' (mail icon, sky bg) + ghost 'Contact host' secondary — manage-booking-frames.jsx:339–342
findings.implementationSays: Web ExpiredState (ManageBookingPanel.tsx:156–183): primary button renders `<Mail .../> Go to Pantopus` and navigates to `APP_WEB_URL || '/'` (line 175–180). No 'Contact host' secondary action. iOS has it correct: InviteeManageBookingView.swift:286 label 'Request a new link' + 'Contact host' secondary (line 289). Android also correct: ManageBookingScreen.kt:547 'Request a new link' + 'Contact host' secondary (line 553).
findings.evidence: Design: manage-booking-frames.jsx:339 `Request a new link`; line 342 `Contact host` ghost button. Web: ManageBookingPanel.tsx:174–180 ` Go to Pantopus` — no secondary Contact host action.

findings.title: Web: action tile icon fill hardcodes Personal (sky) accent regardless of booking pillar
findings.designSays: ActionButton neutral tile background is pillar-resolved accent bg (design sample uses Personal/sky: E.blue50, tileFg = PILLAR) — manage-booking-frames.jsx:166–167. Design comment line 8: 'Host pillar = Personal sky on accents' shows the sample, but pillar is computed from owner_type at runtime.
findings.implementationSays: Web ActionRow (ManageBookingPanel.tsx:72–79) hardcodes `bg-app-personal-bg text-app-personal` for neutral tone regardless of the booking's actual pillar. `pillar` is computed at line 218 and passed to BookingSummaryCard (line 259) but never to any ActionRow call (lines 308–322). Home (green) or Business (violet) bookings will show sky-blue tiles. Android correctly passes `accent = pillar.accent` to ActionRow (ManageBookingScreen.kt:206, 215); iOS correctly uses `viewModel.accentBg` / `viewModel.accent` (InviteeManageBookingView.swift:133–135).
findings.evidence: Web: ManageBookingPanel.tsx:76–79 `isErr ? 'bg-app-error-bg text-app-error' : 'bg-app-personal-bg text-app-personal'`; line 218 `const pillar = pillarForOwner(...)` unused by ActionRow. Android: ManageBookingScreen.kt:200–217 `accent = pillar.accent` passed to both ActionRow calls.

findings.title: Android: PolicyCard not rendered for 'past' state; design and iOS both show it with 'Booked a follow-up?' copy
findings.designSays: Frame 2 (Past): PolicyCard rendered with 'Booked a follow-up? Manage it from the new confirmation email.' — manage-booking-frames.jsx:286
findings.implementationSays: Android ManageBookingContent (ManageBookingScreen.kt:242) guards PolicyCard with `data.status == ManageStatus.Confirmed || data.status == ManageStatus.Pending` — the past case is excluded so the policy card never renders for past bookings. iOS correctly renders policyCard('Booked a follow-up? Manage it from the new confirmation email.') for the past lifecycle case (InviteeManageBookingView.swift:87).
findings.evidence: Design: manage-booking-frames.jsx:286 PolicyCard in FramePast. iOS: InviteeManageBookingView.swift:87 `policyCard("Booked a follow-up?...")`. Android: ManageBookingScreen.kt:242 `if ((data.status == ManageStatus.Confirmed || data.status == ManageStatus.Pending) && ...)` — past excluded.

screens.crossPlatformNotes: All three platforms share the status-badge icon gap (design shows Lucide icon; mobile uses text-only chip, web uses a dot). Web diverges most from design with three majors: wrong expired-state CTA label/action, missing PolicyCard contact-host affordance, and hardcoded Personal accent on action tiles regardless of booking pillar. iOS is closest to design — its PolicyCard, expired state CTA, and pillar accent all match; only the status icon is missing. Android is close to iOS but additionally omits the PolicyCard for the 'past' lifecycle state, which both iOS and the design frame include.
screens.verifierSummary: All four draft major/high-confidence findings were independently confirmed by reading the cited source files. No findings were refuted or downgraded. Finding 1 (status badge icon): confirmed — both SchedulingStatusPill.swift:6 and SchedulingStatusPill.kt:23 explicitly document 'NO leading icon'; the design frame (manage-booking-frames.jsx:107) clearly renders a Lucide icon. Finding 2 (web PolicyCard/contact-host missing): confirmed — ManageBookingPanel.tsx:351 gates on isActive, CancellationPolicy.tsx has no contact button, and past-state 'Booked a follow-up?' copy is absent from web. Finding 3 (web expired CTA): confirmed — ManageBookingPanel.tsx:179 reads 'Go to Pantopus' with href APP_WEB_URL; design says 'Request a new link'; iOS line 286 and Android line 547 are both correct. Finding 4 (web Personal-hardcoded accent): confirmed — ManageBookingPanel.tsx:79 hardcodes bg-app-personal-bg; pillar computed at line 218 is passed to BookingSummaryCard but never to ActionRow; Android correctly passes accent=pillar.accent (lines 206, 215). One new finding added: Android omits the PolicyCard for the past lifecycle state (ManageBookingScreen.kt:242 Confirmed|Pending guard excludes Past), while iOS (line 87) and the design (FramePast:286) both render it. Draft platform status ios:minor and android:minor are both elevated to major given the confirmed icon-gap finding.

findings.title: Web: Row avatar uses Calendar icon + soft pillar bg instead of gradient disc with initials
findings.designSays: HostAvatar: 42px gradient disc (135° pillar gradient, e.g. linear-gradient(135deg,#38bdf8,#0369a1)) with white initials + 13px pillar-colored identity dot (right:-1,bottom:-1, 2.5px white border). Design frames lines 145-151.
findings.implementationSays: Web Row (MyBookingsList.tsx lines 54-68) renders a 40px rounded-full span with tk.bgSoft (soft tinted bg class) and a 20px Calendar Lucide icon tinted tk.text, with a small identity dot. No initials, no gradient disc, wrong size (40 vs 42px), wrong inner content. iOS and Android both correctly implement the gradient disc with initials (iOS: EdgePillarAvatar in EdgeComponents.swift:225-256; Android: HostAvatar with pillarGradient in MyBookingsScreen.kt:419-459).
findings.evidence: Design: my-bookings-frames.jsx:145-151; Web: MyBookingsList.tsx:54-68; iOS correct: EdgeComponents.swift:225-256; Android correct: MyBookingsScreen.kt:419-459

findings.title: Web and iOS: Row primary line shows date/pillar-label fallback instead of event-type name; host name absent everywhere
findings.designSays: Row primary line (fontSize 13.5, fontWeight 700) is the event-type name (e.g. '30-min consult', 'Home walkthrough'). Secondary line is 'with {host} · {when}'. Design: my-bookings-frames.jsx:176-177.
findings.implementationSays: Web (MyBookingsList.tsx:70-76) puts formatDay(booking.start_at, tz) as primary and time+tz as secondary — no event-type name, no host name. iOS (MyBookingsView.swift:135, MyBookingsViewModel.swift comment) falls back to a pillar-label string ('Personal booking') because the lean /my-bookings payload omits event-type and host names; this is explicitly noted as deferredBackend. Android also uses a date as the primary title (MyBookingsViewModel.kt:200: title = formatDate(startAt, zone)). All three platforms are missing event-type and host names due to a missing backend join, not a UI logic error.
findings.evidence: Design: my-bookings-frames.jsx:176-177; Web: MyBookingsList.tsx:70-76; iOS: MyBookingsView.swift:134-145, MyBookingsViewModel.swift comment lines 7-9; Android: MyBookingsViewModel.kt:196-211

findings.title: Web and iOS: Missing 'Book again' affordance on Past tab rows
findings.designSays: Past rows carry a 'Book again' link-button (rotate-ccw icon + 'Book again' text, blue, right-aligned) in a top-border-divided footer section. Design: my-bookings-frames.jsx:184-189 (FramePast lines 249-254).
findings.implementationSays: Web MyBookingsList.tsx has no BookAgain footer concept at all — past rows are identical to upcoming rows (just opacity-70). iOS MyBookingsView.swift has no book-again footer either; iOS rows only show a chevron-right. Android correctly implements BookAgainFooter (MyBookingsScreen.kt:354-380) but the ViewModel currently leaves footer=null for all rows (MyBookingsViewModel.kt:211 — footer field omitted from toRow call), so it does not render yet either. Original audit wrongly scoped this to web only; iOS is equally missing it. Android has the renderer scaffolded but not activated.
findings.evidence: Design: my-bookings-frames.jsx:184-189; Web: MyBookingsList.tsx:43-82 (no footer); iOS: MyBookingsView.swift:126-165 (no footer); Android renderer: MyBookingsScreen.kt:354-380; Android VM: MyBookingsViewModel.kt:192-211

findings.title: Web and iOS: Missing 'Action needed' row affordances (Pay footer, Balance due / Approve pending pills)
findings.designSays: Frame 5 shows a 'Needs attention' overline group with rows having a PayFooter ('$40 due at confirm' + 'Pay $40' CTA pill) for balance-due bookings and an 'Approve pending' status pill for pending-approval bookings. Design: my-bookings-frames.jsx:320-338.
findings.implementationSays: Web MyBookingsList.tsx has no concept of attention groups, balance-due footers, or pay CTAs; BookingStatusPill.tsx has no 'Balance due' or 'Approve pending' labels. iOS MyBookingsView.swift renders the 'Needs attention' overline group (MyBookingsViewModel.swift groups pending bookings there), but rows only show SchedulingStatusPill with no PayFooter or BookAgain footer. Android has a complete local implementation: HostAvatar gradient disc, BookAgainFooter, PayFooter, plus local Balance/Approve pills (MyBookingsScreen.kt:462-522, MyBookingsModels.kt:14-35). Original audit wrongly scoped only to web; iOS is also missing pay footer and approve-pending pill.
findings.evidence: Design: my-bookings-frames.jsx:320-338; Web: MyBookingsList.tsx:43-82; iOS: MyBookingsView.swift:126-165; Android correct: MyBookingsScreen.kt:333-416, MyBookingsModels.kt:14-35

findings.title: Web and iOS: 'Pending' status pill uses warning amber; design specifies info blue
findings.designSays: Pending pill: bg=#F0F9FF (INFO_BG), fg=#0369A1 (INFO), border=#BAE6FD (INFO_LIGHT). Design: my-bookings-frames.jsx:157.
findings.implementationSays: Web BookingStatusPill.tsx line 16: pending maps to 'bg-app-warning-bg text-app-warning' (amber). iOS SchedulingStatusPill.swift line 84: pending tone is .warning (amber bg/fg). Android MyBookingsScreen.kt lines 467-474 explicitly overrides pending to info blue with a comment 'Booker-side spec colours pending INFO blue (#F0F9FF/#0369A1), not the shared pill's host-side amber'. The original audit correctly identified web but missed that iOS has the same amber vs. info blue issue via SchedulingStatusPill.
findings.evidence: Design: my-bookings-frames.jsx:157; Web: BookingStatusPill.tsx:16; iOS: SchedulingStatusPill.swift:84; Android correct: MyBookingsScreen.kt:467-474

findings.title: iOS: EdgePillarAvatar gradient uses single-accent fade instead of design's cross-color pillar gradient
findings.designSays: personal avatar: linear-gradient(135deg,#38bdf8,#0369a1); home: linear-gradient(135deg,#4ade80,#15803d); business: linear-gradient(135deg,#a78bfa,#6d28d9). Design: my-bookings-frames.jsx:24-27.
findings.implementationSays: iOS EdgeComponents.swift:235-243 uses LinearGradient(colors:[accent.opacity(0.8), accent]) — a single-color tint fade rather than the two distinct hue-shifted stops the design specifies. Android correctly uses PantopusColors.primary400 → primary700, homeDark, businessDark (MyBookingsScreen.kt:451-458), which maps to the same two-stop relationship.
findings.evidence: Design: my-bookings-frames.jsx:24-27; iOS: EdgeComponents.swift:235-243; Android correct: MyBookingsScreen.kt:451-458

findings.title: Android: 'Book again' renderer is scaffolded but footer field is never set — affordance never renders
findings.designSays: Past rows carry a 'Book again' link in FramePast (design lines 249-254).
findings.implementationSays: Android MyBookingsViewModel.kt:192-211 — toRow() never sets the footer field (it is absent from the constructor call), so BookingRowFooter.BookAgain is never emitted. The renderer (MyBookingsScreen.kt:334-336) is wired and correct; it simply never fires because the ViewModel does not populate the field. The comment in MyBookingsModels.kt:24-28 acknowledges this: 'the VM leaves this null today — the renderer supports it for when the backend joins those fields'.
findings.evidence: Android VM: MyBookingsViewModel.kt:192-211; Android model comment: MyBookingsModels.kt:24-28; Android renderer: MyBookingsScreen.kt:333-338

screens.crossPlatformNotes: Android is closest to the design: it correctly implements the gradient-disc avatar with initials, info-blue pending pill, 'Needs attention' group with balance-due/approve-pending pills and pay footer, and has the Book Again renderer scaffolded. Its only gap is that the ViewModel never activates the book-again footer because the backend payload lacks the required event-type data. iOS and web both diverge on the pending-pill color (amber vs. info blue), and both are missing the Book Again footer and Pay affordance at the row level. Web additionally uses a Calendar icon instead of a gradient disc avatar. The primary-line event-type name gap is a shared backend data limitation acknowledged in iOS and Android code comments, not a rendering logic error.
screens.verifierSummary:
Verified all 5 draft findings by reading the actual source files. All 5 core discrepancies are real and confirmed, but the platform scope was wrong on 3 of them:

CONFIRMED AS-IS: Finding 1 (web avatar) — correctly scoped to web only; iOS and Android both implement gradient disc correctly.

BROADENED: Finding 2 (primary-line content) — all three platforms show date/fallback instead of event-type name; this is a shared backend data limitation (lean /my-bookings payload omits event-type name). The finding was originally scoped to web only. iOS comment explicitly marks it deferredBackend; Android comment likewise. Broadened to web+iOS but noted Android is identical.

BROADENED: Finding 3 (Book again) — original audit scoped to web only, but iOS also has no Book Again footer. Android has the renderer scaffolded but ViewModel never emits the footer. Expanded to web+iOS; added a separate minor finding for Android.

BROADENED: Finding 4 (Action needed state) — original scoped to web only, but iOS also lacks Pay footer and Approve-pending pill at the row level (it does handle the Needs-attention overline group). Android has full implementation. Expanded to web+iOS.

BROADENED: Finding 5 (pending pill color) — original scoped to web only, but iOS SchedulingStatusPill.swift maps pending → .warning tone (amber), the same mismatch. Android explicitly overrides to info blue with a comment. Expanded to web+iOS.

ADDED: New minor finding — iOS EdgePillarAvatar uses a single-color fade gradient rather than the two distinct hue-shifted stops the design specifies (e.g. #38bdf8 → #0369a1 for personal). Android is correct.

NET RESULT: iOS status upgraded from 'minor' to 'major' (pending-pill color + missing book-again/pay row affordances are material). Android remains 'minor' (book-again renderer scaffolded but never activated). Web remains 'major'.

findings.title: Android missing recap/review state (design Frame 4) — goes directly to confirm
findings.designSays: Frame 4 is a distinct Series Summary screen with a recap header (34×34 accentBg rounded square + repeat icon + N-session series title + event name), a card listing every occurrence with a per-row remove (×) button, and a total price row, before the final Confirm CTA (recurring-frames.jsx lines 382-406)
findings.implementationSays: Android has no reviewing or confirming state separate from the configure step. RecurringBody renders a single combined configure+confirm view. The CTA is immediately 'Confirm N bookings' which fires the API directly; no intermediate review card, no recap header, no RecapRow list, no remove buttons. ResultBody (line 504) is the post-booking success state, not a pre-confirm recap.
findings.evidence: recurring-frames.jsx:382-406 vs RecurringSetupScreen.kt:145-196, RecurringSetupModels.kt (RecurringSubmitState has no Reviewing case)

findings.title: Web missing recap/review state (design Frame 4) — goes directly to confirm
findings.designSays: Frame 4 is a distinct Series Summary screen with recap header, a card of booked occurrences with per-row remove buttons, and a total price row before the Confirm CTA (recurring-frames.jsx lines 382-406)
findings.implementationSays: Web has no review/recap step. The confirm() function at line 109 calls the API directly with no intermediate state. No recap card, no remove-occurrence buttons, no recap header. After submitting, the page shows a done screen (lines 145-165).
findings.evidence: recurring-frames.jsx:382-406 vs RecurringSetup.tsx:109-132 (confirm()), 368-388 (CTA renders directly)

findings.title: Android Repeats field renders as two segmented chips (Weekly / Daily), not a dropdown with chevron
findings.designSays: RepeatsSelect is a single-row control with a repeat icon, 'Weekly' label, and chevron-down — a dropdown affordance showing the current selected cadence (recurring-frames.jsx lines 92-103)
findings.implementationSays: Android iterates all RecurrenceRepeat.entries and renders each as an equal-weight Segment chip: 'Weekly' and 'Daily' appear side-by-side as two pill buttons. No chevron-down, no dropdown pattern, and 'Daily' is an option the design never specifies (RecurringSetupScreen.kt lines 220-231; RecurringSetupModels.kt lines 6-9).
findings.evidence: recurring-frames.jsx:92-103 vs RecurringSetupScreen.kt:220-231 and RecurringSetupModels.kt:6-9

findings.title: Web Repeats field exposes three interval options (Weekly / Every 2 weeks / Monthly) not in design
findings.designSays: Repeats field shows a single dropdown value of 'Weekly' with a chevron-down; only one recurrence pattern is illustrated in all five design frames (recurring-frames.jsx lines 92-103). No mention of biweekly or monthly anywhere in the design file.
findings.implementationSays: Web defines Interval as 'weekly' | 'biweekly' | 'monthly' and renders all three as equal-weight grid buttons labeled 'Weekly', 'Every 2 weeks', 'Monthly'. The chevron-down affordance from the design is absent (RecurringSetup.tsx lines 42-48, 181-200).
findings.evidence: recurring-frames.jsx:92-103 vs RecurringSetup.tsx:42-48, 181-200

findings.title: Web 'On' weekday chips are display-only (aria-hidden, non-interactive), design shows accessible selectable chips
findings.designSays: WeekdayChips renders 7 divs each with aria-label ('Weekday S, selected' etc.) and cursor:pointer — implying all chips are selectable by the user (recurring-frames.jsx lines 111-119). Note: design uses div, not button.
findings.implementationSays: Web renders weekday chips as <span aria-hidden> elements (all seven chips, including the selected one, are aria-hidden=true). Selection is derived from baseStart.getDay() which comes from the query param and cannot be changed by user interaction — there is no onClick handler on any chip (RecurringSetup.tsx lines 209-225).
findings.evidence: recurring-frames.jsx:111-119 vs RecurringSetup.tsx:209-225

findings.title: Android OccurrenceStatus lacks 'Unavailable' case — fully-booked rows not rendered (design Frame 3)
findings.designSays: Frame 3 shows occurrences with status='unavailable': strikethrough date text, calendar-x icon in sunken tile, 'Full' badge at 0.6 opacity, and a warn banner 'We can book 4 of 6' above the list. The Frame 3 partial-series CTAs are 'Book the 4 that work' + ghost 'Adjust the series' (recurring-frames.jsx lines 348-365, OccurrenceRow component lines 203-231).
findings.implementationSays: OccurrenceStatus enum only has Open and Failed (RecurringSetupModels.kt lines 12-14). OccurrenceRow only branches on failed vs. open (RecurringSetupScreen.kt lines 417-468). No warn banner for partial series, no 'Full' badge, no strikethrough treatment. The overline only counts failed occurrences, not full ones (lines 171-177).
findings.evidence: recurring-frames.jsx:348-365 and 203-231 vs RecurringSetupModels.kt:12-14, RecurringSetupScreen.kt:417-468, 171-177

findings.title: Web missing partial-series 'unavailable' occurrence status and warn banner (design Frame 3)
findings.designSays: Frame 3 partial-series: warn banner 'We can book 4 of 6' / 'The other two weeks are full', unavailable rows with strikethrough + 'Full' badge + 0.6 opacity + calendar-x icon, dual CTA 'Book the 4 that work' + ghost 'Adjust the series' (recurring-frames.jsx lines 348-365)
findings.implementationSays: All web occurrence rows always show CalendarCheck icon + 'Open' badge regardless of server response. No unavailable state is modeled in the component. No 'Adjust the series' ghost CTA. No partial-series warn banner (RecurringSetup.tsx lines 329-351, 368-388).
findings.evidence: recurring-frames.jsx:348-365 vs RecurringSetup.tsx:329-351, 368-388

findings.title: Web primary CTA label is 'Book N sessions' instead of 'Review N bookings'
findings.designSays: Primary CTA on Frames 1, 2, and 5 (default / conflict / picker-open) is labeled 'Review 6 bookings' — an intermediate step before the recap (recurring-frames.jsx lines 304, 329, 412). Frame 4 recap uses 'Confirm 6 bookings'.
findings.implementationSays: Web CTA always reads 'Book N session(s)' (RecurringSetup.tsx line 378). Clicking immediately POSTs to the API with no intermediate review. Neither 'Review' nor 'Confirm' labels appear anywhere in the component.
findings.evidence: recurring-frames.jsx:304 vs RecurringSetup.tsx:378

findings.title: Android and Web missing Frame 4 series-summary recap header (icon + title + event name)
findings.designSays: Frame 4 recap header: a 34×34dp rounded-9 accentBg square containing a 17px repeat icon, beside '6-session series' (bold, 14px) + 'Intro call · with Maria Kessler' (secondary, 11px) (recurring-frames.jsx lines 385-391)
findings.implementationSays: Android has no recap state — the recap header does not exist anywhere in RecurringSetupScreen.kt or RecurringSetupModels.kt. Web has no recap state — the recap header does not exist in RecurringSetup.tsx. iOS correctly implements recapHeader at RecurringSetupView.swift lines 130-145: 34×34 frame, arrowsRepeat icon size 17, accentBg background, Radii.md (8pt) clip (vs design's 9pt — negligible 1pt delta).
findings.evidence: recurring-frames.jsx:385-391 vs RecurringSetupScreen.kt (not present), RecurringSetup.tsx (not present), RecurringSetupView.swift:130-145

findings.title: Web Time field is display-only (no chevron, no interaction); design and both native platforms allow time editing
findings.designSays: TimeChip has cursor:pointer and a chevron-down icon alongside the clock + time label, implying the user can change the time (recurring-frames.jsx lines 126-136)
findings.implementationSays: Web renders the Time field as a non-interactive <span> with no chevron-down and no onClick handler. The time is fixed at baseStart (from query params) and cannot be changed in-page (RecurringSetup.tsx lines 232-237). iOS overlays a native DatePicker (RecurringSetupView.swift lines 325-333); Android provides ±30-min stepper buttons (RecurringSetupScreen.kt lines 240-241).
findings.evidence: recurring-frames.jsx:126-136 vs RecurringSetup.tsx:232-237 vs RecurringSetupView.swift:325-333 vs RecurringSetupScreen.kt:240-241

screens.crossPlatformNotes: iOS is closest to design: it implements all five design states (loading, configuring, reviewing, confirming, booked/error), the Frame 4 recap header with arrowsRepeat icon + accentBg tile (Radii.md = 8pt vs design's 9pt — negligible), per-row remove buttons, total price row, and the 'Review N bookings' → 'Confirm N bookings' two-step CTA flow. Android and Web share four major gaps: no Frame 4 recap/review state, no 'Unavailable' occurrence status (partial-series Frame 3 entirely unrendered), missing warn banner for partial series, and no recap header. Android adds a 'Daily' option the design never specifies and uses +/- stepper chips for Repeats instead of the design's dropdown chevron. Web additionally uses 'Book N sessions' instead of 'Review N bookings', exposes biweekly/monthly intervals not in design, and renders weekday chips as aria-hidden non-interactive spans.
screens.verifierSummary: Verified all 9 draft findings against design frame (recurring-frames.jsx) and all three platform implementations. All 9 survive: confirmed high-confidence readings in the source are accurate. Two adjustments made: (1) Finding 4 (Web 3-interval grid) downgraded from major to minor — design's chevron-down implies a multi-option dropdown, making the extra biweekly/monthly options plausibly additive; retained medium confidence. (2) Finding 5 description corrected: the design WeekdayChips use <div aria-label> with cursor:pointer (not <button>), but the core gap — web rendering fully aria-hidden, display-only spans vs. design's accessible selectable chips — is real and confirmed; severity kept at major. One new minor finding added: Web Time field is a non-interactive <span> with no chevron-down and no user control, while design shows TimeChip with cursor:pointer + chevron-down and both native platforms implement time editing (iOS via DatePicker overlay, Android via ±30-min stepper). Findings 1+2 (missing recap) and Finding 9 (missing recap header) are related but kept separate since finding 9 documents the specific visual element missing from Frame 4. Per-platform status confirmed: iOS minor (recap header has 1pt corner-radius delta, time picker is platform-native vs. pill-chip), Android major (4 major gaps), Web major (6 major gaps).

findings.title: Web host sheet missing NotifySwitch row
findings.designSays: Frames 2 and 7 include a NotifySwitch row — bell icon, 'Notify invitee' label, 'Push + message' sub-label, toggle — always visible once slots are loaded.
findings.implementationSays: RescheduleReassignSheet.tsx has no bell/notify toggle anywhere. When authority=now a static hint reads 'The invitee is notified of the new time automatically' (line 357), effectively replacing the interactive switch with passive copy. When authority=propose, no notify affordance appears at all.
findings.evidence: Design: reschedule-frames.jsx lines 149–160 (NotifySwitch component), line 192 (Frame 2 includes <NotifySwitch/>), line 289 (Frame 7 includes <NotifySwitch/>). Web host sheet: RescheduleReassignSheet.tsx — no bell, no toggle anywhere (entire 405-line file searched). iOS: RescheduleReassignSheet.swift lines 402–424 (notifyRow), line 86 (referenced in pickerView). Android: RescheduleReassignSheet.kt lines 140 (NotifySwitch call) and composable lines 571–605.

findings.title: Web invitee RescheduleFlow hardcodes Personal accent in local chrome
findings.designSays: Accent follows the owner's pillar. CurrentSlot filled state uses the owner accent (JSX line 76–78). The design's Sheet component takes an accent parameter; Frame 4 explicitly uses Business violet #7c3aed.
findings.implementationSays: RescheduleFlow.tsx correctly derives `pillar` from `owner_type` (lines 109–115) and correctly passes it to SlotPicker, PolicyCard, ConflictView, BookingSummaryCard, and AddToCalendarPanel. However, the local current-slot new-time row (lines 287/294) and the CTA 'Confirm new time' button (line 346) hardcode `app-personal` / `bg-app-personal` / `text-app-personal` directly. A business or home owner's invitee sees personal-blue accent in these elements regardless of pillar. The host-side RescheduleReassignSheet.tsx correctly uses pillarTokens for its CTA (lines 377–378), so this is isolated to the public invitee flow.
findings.evidence: RescheduleFlow.tsx line 226 ('border-app-personal/40 … text-app-personal' on ghost button), line 287 ('border-app-personal bg-app-personal-bg' when slot picked), line 294 ('text-app-personal' for CalendarClock icon), line 346 ('bg-app-personal' on CTA). Pillar computed correctly at lines 109–115. Design: reschedule-frames.jsx lines 76–78 (filled slot uses accent color). iOS: RescheduleReassignSheet.swift line 266–274 (CalendarClock color uses viewModel.accent), line 195/274 (backgrounds use viewModel.owner.theme.accentBg). Android: RescheduleReassignSheet.kt lines 244–250 (filled state uses accent).

findings.title: iOS overline weight is semibold; design and Android use bold (700)
findings.designSays: AuthorityToggle overline (reschedule-frames.jsx line 138): fontSize:10, fontWeight:700, letterSpacing:'0.08em', textTransform:'uppercase' — bold weight.
findings.implementationSays: iOS Typography.swift line 52: `case .h2, .h3, .overline: .semibold` — the overline token is semibold, not bold. The `pantopusTextStyle(.overline)` modifier does correctly apply uppercase (Typography.swift lines 71–73, 114–116), so uppercase is fine. Android RescheduleReassignSheet.kt line 371 uses FontWeight.Bold for the 'ASSIGN TO' overline explicitly. The original finding's claim that iOS 'does not use uppercase' is wrong — it does uppercase via the token. The real discrepancy is weight: semibold on iOS vs bold on Android and in design.
findings.evidence: Design: reschedule-frames.jsx line 138 (fontWeight:700). iOS: Typography.swift line 52 (overline is .semibold), line 71–73 (uppercased:true confirmed), pantopusTextStyle modifier at line 114. Android: RescheduleReassignSheet.kt line 371 (FontWeight.Bold).

screens.crossPlatformNotes: iOS and Android are substantially aligned on NotifySwitch (both present), authority toggle, day strip, slot list, member rail, and accent theming. The main iOS-only nit is the overline weight token being semibold vs Android's bold. Web has two major gaps: the public invitee flow (RescheduleFlow.tsx) hardcodes Personal accent in local chrome despite computing the correct pillar, and the host sheet (RescheduleReassignSheet.tsx) drops the NotifySwitch entirely. iOS is the closest platform to design; Android matches on all audited elements; web has both major issues in different components.
screens.verifierSummary:
Finding 1 (NotifySwitch missing in web host sheet) — CONFIRMED. Both Frames 2 and 7 include the NotifySwitch composable. RescheduleReassignSheet.tsx has no bell icon or toggle anywhere; only static hint text remains. iOS has a full notifyRow toggle (lines 402–424), Android has a full NotifySwitch composable (lines 571–605). Finding stands as major.

Finding 2 (Web invitee flow hardcodes Personal accent) — CONFIRMED WITH CORRECTION. The auditor's evidence lines are correct but the description conflated two files. RescheduleReassignSheet.tsx (host sheet) correctly uses pillarTokens for its CTA (tk.bg/tk.textOn at lines 377–378) — that part of the original finding was wrong. RescheduleFlow.tsx (public invitee flow) is the actual problem: `pillar` is correctly computed but lines 226, 287, 294, and 346 hardcode app-personal in local chrome elements. Finding retained as major with corrected scope.

Finding 3 (iOS authority-toggle overline not uppercase) — REFUTED AS WRITTEN, replaced with a corrected nit. The claim that iOS does not uppercase is wrong: Typography.swift clearly sets uppercased=true for .overline and the pantopusTextStyle modifier applies it. The real discrepancy is weight: iOS overline token is .semibold (Typography.swift line 52) while the design specifies fontWeight:700 and Android uses FontWeight.Bold. Downgraded to confirmed nit with corrected description.

findings.title: Android: multi-calendar picker level (Frame 5) entirely absent
findings.designSays: Frame 5 'FrameMultiCalendar': second-level sheet with title 'Choose a calendar', back chevron, subtitle 'Where should we add it on this iPhone?', RowCard of on-device calendar rows with dot swatches, and a filled 'Add to Personal' CTA bar — reachable when the user has multiple on-device calendars.
findings.implementationSays: AddToCalendarSheet.kt has no second-level picker state, no OnDeviceCalendar type, and no calendarPickerLevel composable. NativeAddButton.onClick (line 124) always fires buildInsertIntent directly with no in-app calendar picker — regardless of how many calendars the device has.
findings.evidence: add-calendar-frames.jsx:288–311 (FrameMultiCalendar); AddToCalendarSheet.kt:120–126 (NativeAddButton onClick fires buildInsertIntent directly); iOS AddToCalendarSheet.swift:131–141 (showCalendarPicker state gate, calendarPickerLevel) — iOS has this, Android does not.

findings.title: Web: RecapChip uses wrong background, border, icon, and text colors (neutral instead of blue50/100/700)
findings.designSays: RecapChip: background E.blue50 (#F0F9FF), border E.blue100 (#E0F2FE), icon color E.blue700 (#0369A1), text color E.blue700, font-weight 600 (semibold), fontSize 11.5px, icon is 'calendar'.
findings.implementationSays: AddToCalendarPanel.tsx line 38–44 uses bg-app-surface-muted (#F8FAFC), border-app-border (#E5E7EB), CalendarPlus icon (not Calendar), text-app-text-secondary (#6B7280) on icon, text-app-text (#111827) on label, font-medium (not semibold). Every color token and the icon are wrong.
findings.evidence: add-calendar-frames.jsx:98–109 (RecapChip blue50/blue100/blue700, calendar icon); AddToCalendarPanel.tsx:38–44 (bg-app-surface-muted, border-app-border, CalendarPlus icon, text-app-text-secondary, text-app-text, font-medium)

findings.title: Web: sheet header ('Add to your calendar' title) is absent
findings.designSays: SheetHeader renders 'Add to your calendar', fontSize 15, fontWeight 700, height 30px, above the RecapChip on all frames.
findings.implementationSays: AddToCalendarPanel.tsx (lines 36–55) and AddToCalendar.tsx render no header or title element. The panel comment says 'Render inline, or pass to a BottomSheet' but neither the panel nor the inner component provide the header — it is simply absent.
findings.evidence: add-calendar-frames.jsx:87–95 (SheetHeader component); AddToCalendarPanel.tsx:36–55 (full component, no title element); AddToCalendar.tsx:119–159 (full component, no title element)

findings.title: Web: 'Done' footer bar absent
findings.designSays: DoneBar: top-bordered full-width ghost 'Done' button, 44px height, borderRadius 12, surface background, border — present on all frames.
findings.implementationSays: Neither AddToCalendarPanel.tsx nor AddToCalendar.tsx render a Done button. Android (AddToCalendarSheet.kt:215–216) and iOS (AddToCalendarSheet.swift:504–512) both implement DoneBar correctly; web is the only platform missing it.
findings.evidence: add-calendar-frames.jsx:178–184 (DoneBar component), :203–208 (used in FrameWebDefault); AddToCalendarPanel.tsx:36–55 (no Done button); AddToCalendar.tsx:119–159 (no Done button)

findings.title: Web: no ICS generating skeleton state
findings.designSays: Frame 3 'FrameGenerating': the .ics row morphs to a skeleton state (shimmer bar + 'Preparing your file' text) while the file is being generated.
findings.implementationSays: AddToCalendar.tsx line 129: `const ics = icsUrl || icsDataUri(event)` — the ICS is built synchronously as a data URI, or taken from a prop. There is no async fetch, no loading state, and no skeleton. The .ics row is always a static download link.
findings.evidence: add-calendar-frames.jsx:243–259 (FrameGenerating with state='skeleton' on .ics PickerRow); AddToCalendar.tsx:129, 151–157 (synchronous icsDataUri, static <a> tag, no async state)

findings.title: Web: no 'added' success state (Frame 4 morph)
findings.designSays: Frame 4 'FrameAdded': the Apple Calendar row morphs to a success state (green icon disc, 'Added to Apple Calendar' label, 'With a reminder 10 minutes before' sub, check-circle-2 trailing) and a centered 'Added — closing in a moment' status line appears.
findings.implementationSays: AddToCalendar.tsx Row component (lines 81–117) and AddToCalendarPanel.tsx are entirely stateless — all rows are static <a> tags with no added/done variant. Android (AddToCalendarSheet.kt:86, 193–213) and iOS (AddToCalendarSheet.swift:36, 186–189, 492–500) both implement the added success state; web does not.
findings.evidence: add-calendar-frames.jsx:264–283 (FrameAdded with state='done' row + status line); AddToCalendar.tsx:81–117 (Row component, static only, no done/success variant); AddToCalendarPanel.tsx:36–55 (no state tracking)

findings.title: Web: row sub-label copy diverges from design on all four rows
findings.designSays: Apple Calendar sub: 'Save to your iPhone'; Google sub: 'Opens in your browser'; Outlook sub: 'Opens in your browser'; .ics sub: 'Works with any calendar app'; label: 'Download .ics file'.
findings.implementationSays: AddToCalendar.tsx: Apple sub 'Open in Calendar', Google sub 'Add to Google', Outlook sub 'Add to Outlook', .ics sub 'For any calendar app', .ics label 'Download .ics' (missing 'file').
findings.evidence: add-calendar-frames.jsx:199–203 (design sub-labels); AddToCalendar.tsx:135–156 (implementation sub-labels)

findings.title: iOS: addToPillarBar shadow opacity 0.18 vs design's 0.28
findings.designSays: Multi-calendar confirm CTA and native primary CTA: `boxShadow: '0 6px 16px rgba(2,132,199,0.28)'`.
findings.implementationSays: iOS uses .pantopusShadow(.primary) on both nativePrimaryCTA (line 300) and addToPillarBar (line 534). PantopusShadow.primary is defined as `0 6px 16px rgba(2,132,199,0.18)` (Shadows.swift line 43) — matching the CSS token in colors_and_type.css (--shadow-primary: 0 6px 16px rgba(2,132,199,0.18)). The token value is 0.18 while the design frame hardcodes 0.28. Shadow exists and the geometry is correct; only the opacity differs by 0.10.
findings.evidence: add-calendar-frames.jsx:223, 304–306 (boxShadow rgba(2,132,199,0.28)); Shadows.swift:43–50 (PantopusShadow.primary opacity:0.18); colors_and_type.css:110 (--shadow-primary: 0 6px 16px rgba(2,132,199,0.18)); AddToCalendarSheet.swift:300, 534 (.pantopusShadow(.primary))

screens.crossPlatformNotes: iOS is the closest to design: it implements all five frames (web-default, native, ICS skeleton, added-success, multi-calendar picker), the correct blue-tinted RecapChip, SheetHeader, DoneBar, and the ICS async download with skeleton/ready states. Android matches Frame 2 (native layout) correctly and has the DoneBar, RecapChip, and added-success status line, but is missing the entire Frame 5 multi-calendar picker level. Web is the most divergent: it lacks the SheetHeader, DoneBar, added-success state, ICS skeleton state, and uses wrong neutral color tokens on the RecapChip instead of the specified blue50/100/700 palette. The three-way divergence is sharpest on RecapChip (iOS/Android use primary50/100/700 tokens correctly; web uses neutral surface tokens) and on the multi-calendar picker (iOS has it; Android and web do not). Finding 7 from the draft ('Android: native CTA always shown — no web-default row layout') was refuted: Android is correctly implementing Frame 2 (native) per the design spec, not Frame 1 (web-default), so the absence of a web-default fallback on Android is by design.
screens.verifierSummary: Confirmed 6 of the 7 blocker/major findings; refuted 1; downgraded none; added 1 new minor finding; adjusted one nit. REFUTED: Finding 7 ('Android native CTA always shown — no web-default row layout') — Android is supposed to implement Frame 2 (native), not Frame 1 (web-default). There is no spec requirement for Android to have a web-default fallback path; the finding was based on a misreading of the design intent. CONFIRMED (major): Findings 1–6 all independently verified by reading the source. Finding 1 (Android multi-calendar picker absent): iOS AddToCalendarSheet.swift has a full showCalendarPicker state gate and calendarPickerLevel composable with OnDeviceCalendar type; Android AddToCalendarSheet.kt has none of that — the NativeAddButton always fires buildInsertIntent directly. Finding 2 (Web RecapChip colors): AddToCalendarPanel.tsx line 38 uses bg-app-surface-muted/border-app-border/text-app-text-secondary/font-medium and CalendarPlus icon, none of which match blue50/blue100/blue700/semibold/calendar-icon spec. Finding 3 (Web header absent): neither AddToCalendarPanel.tsx nor AddToCalendar.tsx renders any title/header element. Finding 4 (Web DoneBar absent): both Android (line 215–216) and iOS (line 504–512) have DoneBar; web has none. Finding 5 (Web ICS skeleton): AddToCalendar.tsx builds ICS synchronously via icsDataUri() — no async state, no skeleton. Finding 6 (Web added-success state): all web rows are static <a> tags — no state tracking, no done variant. ADJUSTED nit: Finding 8 (iOS shadow opacity) confirmed — Shadows.swift line 43 documents PantopusShadow.primary as rgba(2,132,199,0.18) matching the CSS token, while the design frame hardcodes 0.28; raised confidence to medium since the token definition is directly readable. ADDED: Web sub-label copy diverges on all four rows (Apple/Google/Outlook/.ics) — minor/high-confidence finding with direct evidence from AddToCalendar.tsx:135–156 vs design lines 199–203.

findings.title: Android: identity capsule (IdentityLine) absent from resolved state
findings.designSays: Frame 3 (FrameResolved): after EventPreviewCard an IdentityLine pill is rendered — an inline-flex capsule (background: E.sunken, border, border-radius: 9999) showing the invitee mini-avatar and 'Continuing as <name> · times in <tz>' — deeplink-handoff-frames.jsx:122–128, 211–212
findings.implementationSays: ResolvedBody (OpenInAppInterstitialScreen.kt:166–219) renders HostAvatar, title Text, subtitle Text, and the EventPreviewCard Row only. There is no identity capsule composable, no invitee-name lookup, and no timezone text anywhere in the resolved branch.
findings.evidence: deeplink-handoff-frames.jsx:122–128, 211–212 vs OpenInAppInterstitialScreen.kt:166–219. iOS counterpart correctly calls identityLine() at DeepLinkHandoffView.swift:99.

findings.title: Android: BrandMark renders a Material calendar-grid icon instead of the concentric-ring brand glyph
findings.designSays: Frame 2 (FrameResolving): PantopusMark contains an SVG with three concentric circles (outer stroke-opacity-0.35 ring, middle stroke-1.4 ring, filled centre dot) on a sky-gradient rounded-square tile — deeplink-handoff-frames.jsx:64–65
findings.implementationSays: BrandMark (OpenInAppInterstitialScreen.kt:334–350) uses PantopusIcon.Calendarly which resolves to Icons.Filled.CalendarMonth (a Material calendar-grid icon) per Icons.kt:858. The composable's own docstring at line 330–331 says 'concentric-ring brand glyph', contradicting its implementation.
findings.evidence: deeplink-handoff-frames.jsx:64–65 vs OpenInAppInterstitialScreen.kt:344, Icons.kt:858. iOS hand-rolls the correct concentric-ring SVG at DeepLinkHandoffView.swift:229–242.

findings.title: Web: banner dismissed to null instead of collapsing to minimized strip
findings.designSays: Frame 5 (FrameStayWeb): after the user taps 'Stay on web' the banner collapses to a minimized strip showing a 20px PantopusMark, 'Continuing on the web' label, and an 'Open in app' ghost CTA — deeplink-handoff-frames.jsx:246–249
findings.implementationSays: OpenInAppHandoff.tsx:54–56 sets dismissed=true on the X button click and returns null, removing the banner entirely. There is no collapsed/minimized strip state and no toast confirmation.
findings.evidence: deeplink-handoff-frames.jsx:241–260 vs OpenInAppHandoff.tsx:54–56

screens.crossPlatformNotes: iOS is closest to design: it correctly renders the concentric-ring PantopusMark (hand-rolled SwiftUI, DeepLinkHandoffView.swift:211–243) and the identity capsule (identityLine() at line 99). Android is missing both. Web implements only the smart-banner (Frame 1) as designed; the 'stay on web' collapsed state (Frame 5) is not implemented on any platform. All three platforms share the same copy strings and resolving/resolved/failed state machine structure.
screens.verifierSummary: Both major findings CONFIRMED by direct code inspection. (1) Android identity line: ResolvedBody (OpenInAppInterstitialScreen.kt:166–219) has no identity capsule — zero references to inviteeName, 'Continuing as', or tz display in the resolved branch. iOS correctly implements identityLine() at DeepLinkHandoffView.swift:99. (2) Android BrandMark glyph: PantopusIcon.Calendarly at line 344 maps to Icons.Filled.CalendarMonth (Icons.kt:858), a Material calendar-grid icon, not the three-concentric-circle brand glyph shown in the design and correctly hand-drawn by iOS. The BrandMark's own docstring (line 330) acknowledges it should carry the concentric-ring glyph, making the mismatch unambiguous. No false positives found. One additional minor gap added: the web banner collapses to null on dismiss (OpenInAppHandoff.tsx:56) instead of rendering the design's minimized strip (Frame 5). Line numbers in both original findings were accurate; evidence citations corrected to include the FrameResolved JSX lines (211–212) that were omitted from the original.

findings.title: Android declined state: hold chip shows static label instead of live countdown with time
findings.designSays: FrameDeclined (payment-failed-frames.jsx line 228): HoldChip renders 'Holding your 2:00 PM time for 4:48' — a time-anchored label. iOS PaymentFailedViewModel.swift startHold() (lines 114-136) confirms the live countdown intent: holdRemaining ticks down every second and holdLabel formats it as 'M:SS'.
findings.implementationSays: DeclinedBody (PaymentRetryScreen.kt line 149) calls CenteredChip with hardcoded label 'Your time is still held'. PaymentRetryViewModel.kt has no holdRemaining, no holdDuration, no timer coroutine — zero countdown state. The time of the slot is never surfaced.
findings.evidence: Design payment-failed-frames.jsx:141,228; Android PaymentRetryScreen.kt:149; Android PaymentRetryViewModel.kt (no timer field); iOS PaymentFailedViewModel.swift:114-136 (startHold countdown), PaymentFailedView.swift:258-263 (holdChipText uses holdLabel)

findings.title: Android timeout state: hold countdown chip entirely absent
findings.designSays: FrameTimeout (payment-failed-frames.jsx line 267): <HoldChip time='4:31'/> appears between the HaloBlock and the InfoCalloutCard.
findings.implementationSays: TimeoutBody (PaymentRetryScreen.kt lines 167-184) renders: EdgeHalo → InfoCalloutCard → Column(CTAs) → ReassureNote. No CenteredChip or HoldChip call anywhere in this composable. iOS uncertain state (PaymentFailedView.swift line 113) correctly includes holdChip(released: false) between halo and calloutInfo.
findings.evidence: Design payment-failed-frames.jsx:267; Android PaymentRetryScreen.kt:167-184 (TimeoutBody — no chip); iOS PaymentFailedView.swift:105-119 (uncertain — holdChip present at line 113)

findings.title: Web succeeded state: missing auto-advance progress bar and 'Confirming your booking' indicator
findings.designSays: FrameSucceeded (payment-failed-frames.jsx lines 303-310): a 70%-wide track with a 62%-fill success-colour bar, a pulsing dot (animation: payPulse), and the label 'Confirming your booking' appear below the success chip. The amount chip uses a badge-check icon inside a success-toned capsule (lines 295-301).
findings.implementationSays: The succeeded branch (PaymentRetryPanel.tsx lines 170-190) renders: Halo + an optional inline paragraph '<ShieldCheck> Paid $X · receipt on its way' + '<Lock> Payments secured by Stripe'. There is no progress bar, no pulsing dot, no 'Confirming your booking' text. The amount is displayed as a plain text row with ShieldCheck rather than a BadgeCheck capsule chip. Android SuccessAutoAdvance() (PaymentRetryScreen.kt lines 307-354) correctly implements the 70%/62% bar and animated pulsing dot.
findings.evidence: Design payment-failed-frames.jsx:295-310; Web PaymentRetryPanel.tsx:170-190 (no progress bar, no confirming text, ShieldCheck not BadgeCheck for amount chip); Android PaymentRetryScreen.kt:307-354 (correct SuccessAutoAdvance implementation)

findings.title: iOS succeeded state: uses circular spinner instead of design's linear progress bar
findings.designSays: FrameSucceeded (payment-failed-frames.jsx lines 303-305): a 70%-wide track div with a 62%-width success-coloured fill — a horizontal progress bar, not a circular spinner.
findings.implementationSays: iOS succeeded view (PaymentFailedView.swift line 150) uses ProgressView().controlSize(.small), which renders the system circular spinner. The 'Confirming your booking' text is present (line 151), but the progress bar shape is wrong. Android uses a horizontal bar (PaymentRetryScreen.kt:313-329) which matches the design.
findings.evidence: Design payment-failed-frames.jsx:303-305; iOS PaymentFailedView.swift:149-153 (ProgressView spinner + Confirming text); Android PaymentRetryScreen.kt:313-329 (Box/fillMaxWidth(0.7f)/fillMaxWidth(0.62f) horizontal bar)

screens.crossPlatformNotes: Android is farthest from design on this screen: it lacks any hold countdown in both Declined and Timeout states, and PaymentRetryViewModel has no timer at all. Web is close in structure but missing the entire auto-advance block (progress bar + pulsing indicator) in the succeeded state and renders the amount chip as inline text instead of a BadgeCheck capsule. iOS is the closest to design — hold countdown works correctly with slot-time label and live tick, and succeeded includes 'Confirming your booking' — but uses a circular spinner rather than the specified horizontal progress bar.
screens.verifierSummary: All three draft major findings independently confirmed by direct source read. Finding 1 (Android declined static chip): confirmed — PaymentRetryScreen.kt:149 hardcodes 'Your time is still held', ViewModel has no timer; iOS counterpart has a full startHold() countdown loop and holdChipText() with time label. Finding 2 (Android timeout missing chip): confirmed — TimeoutBody (lines 167-184) has no CenteredChip/HoldChip call; design line 267 and iOS uncertain state both place the hold chip between halo and callout. Finding 3 (Web succeeded missing progress bar): confirmed — PaymentRetryPanel.tsx lines 170-190 render only Halo + inline ShieldCheck amount text + Stripe line; the 70%/62% progress bar and pulsing 'Confirming your booking' indicator are entirely absent; Android SuccessAutoAdvance() (lines 307-354) correctly implements them. One additional finding added: iOS succeeded uses a circular ProgressView spinner (line 150) where the design specifies a horizontal progress bar — minor parity gap. All evidence citations verified against actual file line numbers.

findings.title: Web: no PolicyCard rendered for blocked states — only a small inline Lock+text note
findings.designSays: All 5 frames show a prominent amber/green PolicyCard (icon disc + title + body + optional 'still' footnote) as the central information element, placed below the SummaryCard.
findings.implementationSays: ManageBookingPanel.tsx collapses all D10 policy states into a single `windowClosed` boolean (line 232). When true it renders only a tiny inline Lock icon + text note. The PolicyCard component from CutoffPolicyBlocked.tsx is never imported or rendered in the manage flow.
findings.evidence: Design frames.jsx lines 214-217, 229-232, 245-248, 260-263, 275-276 (PolicyCard in every frame); ManageBookingPanel.tsx lines 232 (windowClosed bool), 323-334 (inline Lock note only); CutoffPolicyBlocked.tsx exists with a complete PolicyCard but is not imported by ManageBookingPanel.tsx

findings.title: Web: no state-specific dock CTAs for blocked states — action rows remain but are just disabled
findings.designSays: Frames 1–4 each show a fixed bottom dock with 2 state-specific CTAs: cancel-closed → ghost 'Keep my booking' + hostGhost 'Message host'; reschedule-closed → same dock + inline 'Cancel instead' link; partial-refund → destructive 'Cancel and refund $X' + ghost 'Keep my booking'; not-online → primary 'Message host' + ghost 'Keep my booking'.
findings.implementationSays: ManageBookingPanel.tsx shows disabled ActionRow links for reschedule and cancel regardless of which blocked state applies. No fixed-bottom dock, no 'Keep my booking', no 'Message host', no 'Cancel and refund $X', no 'Cancel instead' inline link are ever rendered.
findings.evidence: Design frames.jsx lines 211-212, 226-227, 242-243, 257-258 (Dock children per frame); ManageBookingPanel.tsx lines 305-337 (only disabled ActionRows + windowClosed inline note)

findings.title: Web: D10 policy-state machine exists but is never wired to the manage panel — the surface is stuck at the D4 baseline
findings.designSays: D10 is a complete manage surface that renders distinct UI (PolicyCard + dock CTAs) for each of 5 policy states. The policy copy and dock CTAs are the primary value of the screen.
findings.implementationSays: ManageBookingPanel.tsx comment at line 1 explicitly describes itself as 'D4 — Manage your booking' baseline. edgePolicy.ts (complete state machines) and CutoffPolicyBlocked.tsx (PolicyCard + copy builders) are both built and complete but are never imported by ManageBookingPanel.tsx.
findings.evidence: ManageBookingPanel.tsx line 1 ('W6 · D4 — Manage your booking'); CutoffPolicyBlocked.tsx lines 1-4 ('D10 — Reschedule / Cancel cutoff...'), not imported by ManageBookingPanel; edgePolicy.ts lines 1-5 ('D10 policy state-machines'), not imported by ManageBookingPanel

findings.title: Android: PolicyNoteCard title and 'still' note use tone.fg (lighter tone) instead of tone.dk (darker tone)
findings.designSays: PolicyCard title: color t.dk (WARN_DK=#92400E, SUCCESS_DK=#047857), fontWeight 700. 'still' footnote: color t.dk, fontWeight 600. Only the body uses t.fg (the lighter tone color). The design uses two distinct shade levels: darker for title/still, lighter for body.
findings.implementationSays: PolicyNoteCard in EdgeComponents.kt uses `color = tone.fg` for both the title (line 284) and the 'still' note text (line 310) — the same lighter tone color as the body. There is no darker-shade separation for title vs body.
findings.evidence: Design policy-blocked-frames.jsx line 136 (`color:t.dk` for title), line 141 (`color:t.dk` for still text), line 137 (`color:t.fg` for body — confirming the two-level intent); EdgeComponents.kt line 284 (`color = tone.fg` for title), line 310 (`color = tone.fg` for still text), line 285 (`color = tone.fg` for body — all three collapsed to fg)

findings.title: Android: ManageTopBar title uses PantopusTextStyle.h3 (20sp semibold) — design and web spec 15pt/600
findings.designSays: TopBar title: fontSize 15, fontWeight 600, centered (design frames.jsx line 55).
findings.implementationSays: ManageTopBar uses `style = PantopusTextStyle.h3` which is 20sp semibold (Typography.kt lines 42–49). Web renders `text-[15px] font-semibold` (ManageBookingPanel.tsx lines 244–245). iOS uses the system `.inline` navigation title (system-controlled size, not a custom 15pt). Android's 20sp h3 is 33% larger than the 15pt design spec and inconsistent with web.
findings.evidence: Design frames.jsx line 55 (fontSize:15, fontWeight:600); RescheduleCancelPolicyScreen.kt line 134 (PantopusTextStyle.h3); Typography.kt lines 42–49 (h3 = 20sp semibold); ManageBookingPanel.tsx lines 244–245 (text-[15px] font-semibold)

findings.title: Android: CancelInsteadLink and ManageActionRow icon bg hardcode SchedulingPillar.Personal.accent instead of the booking's dynamic pillar
findings.designSays: Design uses Personal sky (blue600) throughout as the example pillar. The correct behavior is to use the booking's actual pillar accent for Home and Business bookings.
findings.implementationSays: CancelInsteadLink hardcodes SchedulingPillar.Personal.accent for icon tint (line 307) and text color (line 314). ManageActionRow hardcodes SchedulingPillar.Personal.accent for icon tint (line 327) and SchedulingPillar.Personal.accentBg for icon background (line 346) in the non-destructive case. ManageContent receives view.pillar dynamically (line 164) but does not pass it to these two composables. HostGhostButton in the dock (line 284) correctly uses dynamic pillar.accent. iOS uses EdgeOwnerTheme.accent(forOwnerType:) throughout (PolicyBlockedView.swift lines 97, 121, 123, 125, 141).
findings.evidence: RescheduleCancelPolicyScreen.kt lines 307, 314 (CancelInsteadLink — Personal.accent hardcoded); lines 327, 346 (ManageActionRow — Personal.accent/accentBg hardcoded); line 284 (HostGhostButton — correct dynamic pillar.accent); PolicyBlockedView.swift lines 97, 121, 123, 125, 141 (EdgeOwnerTheme.accent dynamic throughout)

findings.title: Web PolicyCard (unused): title color uses neutral text-app-text-strong instead of tone-specific dark amber — and the web token palette has no warning-dk token
findings.designSays: PolicyCard title: color t.dk (dark amber #92400E for warn tone, dark green #047857 for success tone).
findings.implementationSays: CutoffPolicyBlocked.tsx TONES map uses `text-app-text-strong` (gray-700, ~#374151) for title across all tones (lines 34, 41, 48). The web globals.css has no warning-dk or success-dk token. This affects the existing PolicyCard component, though it is currently unconnected to the manage flow (covered by the blocker findings above).
findings.evidence: CutoffPolicyBlocked.tsx lines 34, 41, 48 (title: 'text-app-text-strong'); globals.css lines 28, 136 (--app-text-strong is gray-700 in light mode); globals.css lines 49–51 (only warning, warning-light, warning-bg defined — no warning-dk); design frames.jsx line 136 (color:t.dk = WARN_DK = #92400E)

screens.crossPlatformNotes: iOS is the closest platform to design: it implements a full D10 policy state machine with PolicyBlockedView.swift covering all 5 states (withinPolicy, rescheduleCutoff, cancelCutoffNoRefund, partialRefund, changeNotAllowed), renders PolicyCard-equivalent via EdgeNoteCard, pins state-specific dock CTAs via .safeAreaInset, and uses dynamic EdgeOwnerTheme.accent throughout. Minor issues remain (system inline nav title vs custom 15pt TopBar). Android implements the same D10 state machine (ManagePolicyMode enum + ManageDock) and correctly renders PolicyNoteCard in all states, but diverges on three points: title/still text color uses tone.fg instead of tone.dk (lighter vs darker shade of the tone), the TopBar title is 20sp h3 vs the design's 15pt, and CancelInsteadLink/ManageActionRow hardcode the Personal pillar accent instead of using the booking's dynamic pillar. Web is the furthest from design: the manage panel is still at the D4 baseline — a windowClosed boolean replaces the full D10 state machine, no PolicyCard is rendered, and no state-specific dock CTAs exist; the D10 components (CutoffPolicyBlocked.tsx, edgePolicy.ts) are built but never wired to the manage flow.
screens.verifierSummary: All 6 draft findings independently confirmed via direct file reads. No findings were refuted or downgraded. Finding 6 (D10 missing) was retained as a distinct entry because it captures the architectural cause (D4 vs D10 baseline) rather than just the symptoms in Findings 1 and 2. One new nit finding was added: the web PolicyCard component (CutoffPolicyBlocked.tsx) uses neutral text-app-text-strong for the title in all tone variants rather than a tone-specific dark color, because the web token palette has no warning-dk or success-dk token. This is lower priority since the component is not yet wired to the manage flow. Key corrections to evidence: Finding 3 evidence line numbers were updated to cite the body line (137) to confirm the design's two-level intent (dk for title/still, fg for body). Finding 4 evidence was expanded to include the web equivalent (text-[15px] font-semibold) showing Android diverges from both design and web. Finding 5 evidence was expanded to note that HostGhostButton in the dock already uses dynamic pillar.accent correctly, isolating the regression to CancelInsteadLink and ManageActionRow only.

findings.title: Web has no 'Soonest' badge on the first alternative slot row
findings.designSays: SlotRow at slot-taken-frames.jsx:122-146 accepts a soonest prop; FrameAlternatives (line 209) passes soonest to the first row, rendering an uppercase 'SOONEST' badge (blue50 bg, blue700 text, pill border) next to the weekday label.
findings.implementationSays: SlotConflictAlternatives.tsx:100-130 iterates alternatives with .map() — no index is tracked and no soonest/badge rendering exists anywhere in the component.
findings.evidence: slot-taken-frames.jsx:122-146, 209; SlotConflictAlternatives.tsx:100-130

findings.title: iOS missing 'Soonest' badge on the first alternative slot row
findings.designSays: First alternative slot row always carries the 'Soonest' uppercase badge (slot-taken-frames.jsx:133-135).
findings.implementationSays: SchedulingSlotRow.swift has no soonest parameter. SlotTakenSheet.swift:131-138 uses ForEach(alternatives, id: \.self) with no index tracking and passes no badge information to SchedulingSlotRow. Android (ConflictAlternativesSheet.kt:90-91) correctly uses forEachIndexed and passes soonest = index == 0 to AlternativeRow.
findings.evidence: slot-taken-frames.jsx:133-135; SlotTakenSheet.swift:131-138; SchedulingSlotRow.swift:23-37; ConflictAlternativesSheet.kt:90-91

findings.title: Web uses AlertTriangle icon instead of CalendarX in error halo
findings.designSays: ErrorBlock at slot-taken-frames.jsx:109 uses 'calendar-x' (Lucide) as the halo icon, coloured WARN (#D97706).
findings.implementationSays: SlotConflictAlternatives.tsx:9 imports AlertTriangle (not CalendarX); line 70 renders AlertTriangle inside the alert block. CalendarX is never imported.
findings.evidence: slot-taken-frames.jsx:109; SlotConflictAlternatives.tsx:9, 70

findings.title: Web error block is a horizontal inline row, not a centered A18 halo stack
findings.designSays: ErrorBlock (slot-taken-frames.jsx:103-118) is a centered vertical column: 64pt outer halo circle → 50pt inner ringed circle → CalendarX icon → h2 title → body text, all center-aligned.
findings.implementationSays: SlotConflictAlternatives.tsx:68-80 uses 'flex items-start gap-3' — a horizontal row with a 36pt (h-9 w-9) circular icon disc on the left and text stacked on the right. No outer halo ring, no center alignment. iOS (SlotTakenSheet.swift:80-107) and Android (ConflictAlternativesSheet.kt:128-161) both implement the correct centered double-ring halo stack.
findings.evidence: slot-taken-frames.jsx:103-118; SlotConflictAlternatives.tsx:68-80; SlotTakenSheet.swift:80-107; ConflictAlternativesSheet.kt:128-161

findings.title: Web error block is wrapped in a bordered yellow warning card; design uses a plain sheet surface
findings.designSays: The sheet body (slot-taken-frames.jsx:85-94) is a plain E.surface background. The ErrorBlock and slot rows are NOT wrapped in any border card — each section stands on the white sheet directly.
findings.implementationSays: SlotConflictAlternatives.tsx:63-66: the entire component root is 'rounded-2xl border border-app-warning/40 bg-app-warning-bg/60 p-4', placing all content (halo, rows, ghost button) inside a yellow-tinted bordered card.
findings.evidence: slot-taken-frames.jsx:85-94; SlotConflictAlternatives.tsx:63-66

findings.title: Web 'See another day' ghost button missing or mislabelled in fully-booked state
findings.designSays: FrameFullyBooked (slot-taken-frames.jsx:238-245) always renders two CTAs: PrimaryButton 'Join the waitlist' + GhostButton 'See another day'.
findings.implementationSays: SlotConflictAlternatives.tsx:156-164: the 'Pick another time' ghost is guarded by '{onPickAnother && ...}' making it conditional on an optional prop. When present, the label reads 'Pick another time' not 'See another day' (design label for the fully-booked variant). All three call-sites in practice do pass onPickAnother, but the component contract does not guarantee the button. Additionally the label always reads 'Pick another time' regardless of mode — the design uses 'See another day' specifically in the fully-booked frame. Android (ConflictAlternativesSheet.kt:99) renders GhostButton('See another day') unconditionally in the fully-booked branch.
findings.evidence: slot-taken-frames.jsx:238-245; SlotConflictAlternatives.tsx:41, 52, 156-164; ConflictAlternativesSheet.kt:99

screens.crossPlatformNotes: Android is closest to design: it correctly implements the centered A18 double-ring ErrorHalo (ConflictAlternativesSheet.kt:128-161), uses CalendarX icon (line 155), renders the 'Soonest' badge on index 0 (line 90-91 with forEachIndexed), and shows 'See another day' unconditionally in the fully-booked branch (line 99). iOS correctly implements the halo stack and CalendarX icon, but shares the iOS/web Soonest badge gap — SchedulingSlotRow has no soonest parameter and SlotTakenSheet uses ForEach without index. Web diverges most severely: wrong icon (AlertTriangle), wrong layout (horizontal inline not centered halo), all content wrapped in a warning-tinted border card, and the ghost button is optional and mislabelled for the fully-booked case.
screens.verifierSummary: All five draft findings independently confirmed against source. Finding 5 confidence upgraded from medium to high — the design unambiguously shows the ghost always present with label 'See another day', both iOS (SlotTakenSheet.swift:150 uses fallback to onPickAnotherTime) and Android (ConflictAlternativesSheet.kt:99) render it unconditionally, and the web component makes it conditional via an optional prop while also using a different label. One new finding added: iOS also misses the 'Soonest' badge on the first alternative row — SchedulingSlotRow.swift has no soonest parameter, and SlotTakenSheet.swift iterates with ForEach (not ForEach with index), so no index-0 badge can be applied. This was missed by the original auditor who attributed the gap only to web. iOS status is therefore upgraded from minor to major. Android status remains minor (small remaining gaps not re-examined here).

findings.title: No-show row interaction model diverges: web uses inline toggle button vs iOS/Android chevron navigation
findings.designSays: No-show handling row shows a chevron-right trailing icon (design uses chevron-right per policy-sheet-frames.jsx line 57), implying it opens a sub-screen or action sheet.
findings.implementationSays: iOS and Android both implement no-show as a tappable row with chevron-right that cycles a value in place (onCycleNoShow/model.cycleNoShow()). Web renders a visible inline button labeled 'Charge full' / 'No charge' with no chevron, toggling between two states in-row (RefundPolicyEditor.tsx:214-227). Different affordance signal entirely.
findings.evidence: Design: policy-sheet-frames.jsx:57. iOS: CancellationPolicyEditorView.swift:148-167. Android: CancellationRefundPolicyScreen.kt:284-316. Web: RefundPolicyEditor.tsx:214-227.

screens.crossPlatformNotes: The low-confidence draft finding about Android using Radii.xl (16dp) for the custom card container has been DROPPED. Verification confirms all three platforms use the same 16px/dp/pt radius: Android uses Radii.xl = 16dp (Radii.kt:24), iOS BizCard uses Radii.xl = 16pt (Radii.swift:20), and Web Card uses rounded-2xl = 16px (kit.tsx:59). This matches the design token --radius-xl: 16px exactly. No discrepancy exists. One genuine cross-platform divergence was found during verification: the no-show row interaction model on web uses an inline text toggle button, while iOS and Android both use a chevron-right navigable row. iOS and Android are mutually consistent and closest to the design frame. Web diverges on this one interactive affordance.
screens.verifierSummary: DROPPED the sole submitted finding (Android card radius nit, confidence: low). Independent verification shows Android Radii.xl = 16dp, iOS Radii.xl = 16pt, and Web rounded-2xl = 16px — all three match the design token --radius-xl: 16px and the design's Card component. The finding was a false alarm: there is no cross-platform divergence in corner radius. One new finding was added based on code encountered during verification: the no-show handling row in the custom rows section uses an inline text toggle button on web (RefundPolicyEditor.tsx:214-227) instead of the chevron-right navigable row used on iOS (CancellationPolicyEditorView.swift:148-167) and Android (CancellationRefundPolicyScreen.kt:284-316). The per-platform status (ios: minor, android: minor, web: minor) is preserved from the draft, as other findings in the draft (not submitted for scrutiny) were not re-examined here.

findings.title: Member-gated banner (Frame 9) missing on all platforms
findings.designSays: Frame 9 renders a MemberBanner ('You're seeing bookings assigned to you') between ScopePills and the Segmented control when viewer is a member. Blue50 bg, blue200 border, user-check icon (bookings-inbox-frames.jsx:228-235, :434).
findings.implementationSays: No platform has a member-gated banner slot. iOS VStack body (topBar → scopePills → segmentedTabs) has no conditional banner. Android Column (InboxTopBar → ScopePillRow → InboxSegmented → Divider → content) has no banner. Web page.tsx renders no MemberBanner component at all.
findings.evidence: Design bookings-inbox-frames.jsx:228-235 (MemberBanner component), :434 (Frame9 renders it). iOS BookingsInboxView.swift:25-36 (VStack body). Android BookingsInboxScreen.kt:127-143 (Column ordering). Web page.tsx:248-382.

findings.title: Auto-confirm variant (Frame 8) — 'Pending' segment never hidden on any platform
findings.designSays: Frame 8 passes hidePending to Segmented, resulting in only 3 tabs (Upcoming / Past / Cancelled). Segmented slices Pending from opts when hidePending=true (bookings-inbox-frames.jsx:89-92, :411).
findings.implementationSays: All platforms always render all 4 segments. iOS iterates BookingStatusFilter.allCases unconditionally. Android iterates BookingSegment.entries unconditionally with no hidePending param. Web maps a fixed 4-entry TABS constant unconditionally.
findings.evidence: Design bookings-inbox-frames.jsx:89-92, :411. iOS BookingsInboxView.swift:154-165 (segmentedTabs, allCases). Android BookingsInboxScreen.kt:376-380 (InboxSegmented signature), :392 (entries.forEach). Web page.tsx:51-56 (TABS constant), :242 (tabOptions).

findings.title: Android: Segmented active-tab text always uses primary700, ignores pillar accent
findings.designSays: Segmented active tab text is colored with the accent prop (blue700 for All/Personal, #7c3aed for Business, #16a34a for Home) per bookings-inbox-frames.jsx:100.
findings.implementationSays: Android InboxSegmented has no accent parameter and hardcodes PantopusColors.primary700 for the active tab at BookingsInboxScreen.kt:410. iOS correctly uses viewModel.accent (BookingsInboxView.swift:175).
findings.evidence: Design bookings-inbox-frames.jsx:100 (color:on?(accent||E.blue700)). Android BookingsInboxScreen.kt:376-380 (no accent param), :410 (primary700 hardcoded). iOS BookingsInboxView.swift:175 (viewModel.accent — correct).

findings.title: Approve quick-action button uses pillar accent on Android and Web; design specifies fixed PRIMARY blue

Web BookingRow.tsx uses clsx(tk.bg, tk.textOn) where tk = pillarTokens(pillar), also pillar-tinted. iOS BookingRowView.swift:180 correctly uses Theme.Color.primary600.

findings.title: FAB background uses pillar accent on iOS, Android, and Web; design always uses PRIMARY blue
findings.designSays: FAB (bookings-inbox-frames.jsx:222) has background:PRIMARY (#0284c7) regardless of active scope — not accent-polymorphic.
