findings.designSays: Frame 3 (No overlap): headline 'No time works for all 3' — count is dynamic based on number of selected people
findings.implementationSays: SuggestedSlotsScreen.kt:479: hardcoded 'No time works for everyone'. iOS: FindATimeSuggestedView.swift:319 uses 'No time works for all \(viewModel.members.count)' with dynamic count.
findings.evidence: Android: SuggestedSlotsScreen.kt:479 (NoOverlapBody). iOS: FindATimeSuggestedView.swift:319. Design: findtime-slots-frames.jsx:115.

screens.crossPlatformNotes: iOS is closest to design: implements shimmer skeleton composing state (not spinner), correct check icon and 'View responses' CTA in sent state, TopBar with 'Suggested times' title via .navigationTitle, and Edit button correctly opens a setup sheet. Android matches iOS on the composing shimmer cards and sent-state check icon, but diverges on the critical Edit button behavior (navigates back instead of opening edit sheet) and has two minor copy-level gaps (static composing subtitle, static no-overlap count). Web has the most divergences from design: spinner instead of shimmer in computing state, wrong icon and inverted CTA hierarchy in sent state, Edit button misplaced in footer instead of top bar, and 'Suggested times' title absent from results step entirely.
screens.verifierSummary: All 5 draft major findings were independently confirmed by direct source read. No findings were refuted or downgraded. Finding 1 (web spinner vs shimmer): confirmed — page.tsx:222-239 is Loader2 only, no SubHead, no shimmer cards. Finding 2 (web sent state): confirmed — Users icon at line 268, Open poll in primary slot at lines 289-298, Back to calendar demoted to secondary at lines 299-305, ShareLink widget added at lines 279-287, 'View responses' label absent. Finding 3 (web sticky footer): confirmed — SuggestedSlots.tsx:270-289 has a flex row with Edit + Send proposal side by side; design has only one full-width secondary button. Finding 4 (web missing top-bar): confirmed — SuggestedSlots.tsx has no TopBar element; page.tsx renders 'Find a time' h1 throughout all steps, 'Suggested times' never appears. Finding 5 (Android Edit navigates back): confirmed — SuggestedSlotsScreen.kt:121 passes onTrailing=onBack; iOS correctly opens a sheet at lines 55-64. Two additional nit-level cross-platform findings added: Android composing subtitle is static ('Overlaying everyone's availability') vs design's dynamic member names, and Android no-overlap headline is static 'No time works for everyone' vs design/iOS's dynamic count ('No time works for all N').

findings.title: Android: submitted/success state never rendered — screen stays on voting form after submit
findings.designSays: Frame 2 (Answered) leads to a completed state after submit; iOS renders a full submittedView with check-circle, headline 'Response submitted', sub-copy, and Done CTA.
findings.implementationSays: MemberPollResponseViewModel.kt:227 sets submitted = true on the Loaded state, but LoadedPoll composable (MemberPollResponseScreen.kt:133–171) never checks state.submitted and renders no success branch. The user sees the voting form indefinitely after a successful POST.
findings.evidence: MemberPollResponseViewModel.kt:227 (s.copy(submitting = false, submitted = true)); MemberPollResponseScreen.kt:133–171 (no state.submitted guard or success composable — independently confirmed by reading full file); PollResponseView.swift:290–312 (iOS submittedView fully implemented with .submitted Phase case branched at line 49 of PollResponseView.swift)

findings.title: Web: extra 'Your details' form (name + email capture) absent from design
findings.designSays: No name/email capture in any of the four frames (unanswered, answered, conflicts-detected, expired/closed). The OrganizerHeader and slot cards are the only body content before the sticky footer CTA.
findings.implementationSays: PollResponse.tsx:237–263 renders a 'Your details' card with Name (optional) and Email fields above the Submit button. canSubmit is also gated on emailValid (line 87), meaning submit is blocked until a valid email is entered — a behavioural divergence from design and mobile.
findings.evidence: poll-response-frames.jsx (all four frames confirmed — no identity capture in any); PollResponse.tsx:237–263 (the 'Your details' card), PollResponse.tsx:87 (canSubmit = answeredCount > 0 && emailValid && !submitting)

findings.title: Web: submit-enable logic requires email validity; design gates only on at least one vote cast
findings.designSays: Frame 1 (Unanswered): Submit is disabled. Frame 2 (Answered): Submit is enabled. Gate is solely whether any slot has been voted (PrimaryBtn disabled attr present in FrameUnanswered, absent in FrameAnswered).
findings.implementationSays: PollResponse.tsx:87 canSubmit = answeredCount > 0 && emailValid && !submitting. Email validity is required in addition to having voted, which blocks submit even when all votes are cast if the email field is empty or invalid. Android's canSubmit (MemberPollResponseViewModel.kt:76–77) correctly requires only options.any { it.vote != null }.
findings.evidence: poll-response-frames.jsx:63 (FrameUnanswered PrimaryBtn disabled, no email mention); poll-response-frames.jsx:80 (FrameAnswered PrimaryBtn enabled, no email mention); PollResponse.tsx:87; MemberPollResponseViewModel.kt:76–77

findings.title: iOS: submit gate requires ALL slots voted; design enables submit on at least one vote
findings.designSays: Frame 2 (Answered) shows Submit enabled with three slots each having a different vote value — the gate is 'at least one slot voted', not 'all slots voted'.
findings.implementationSays: PollResponseViewModel.swift:100 allAnswered = !options.isEmpty && options.allSatisfy { selections[$0.id] != nil }. Submit button isEnabled: viewModel.allAnswered (PollResponseView.swift:227). This means Submit stays disabled until every proposed slot is voted, which is stricter than the design specifies. Android's any { it.vote != null } correctly matches design intent.
findings.evidence: poll-response-frames.jsx:76–78 (FrameAnswered: three slots each with a vote, Submit enabled — not all-or-nothing); PollResponseViewModel.swift:100 (allAnswered uses allSatisfy); PollResponseView.swift:227 (isEnabled: viewModel.allAnswered); MemberPollResponseViewModel.kt:76–77 (Android uses any — correct)

screens.crossPlatformNotes: Android is the most divergent: the submitted/success state is entirely absent from the screen despite the ViewModel correctly setting submitted=true, leaving users on the voting form after a successful POST. Web is major-divergent on two fronts: the 'Your details' identity-capture card is absent from all four design frames, and the email-validity gate blocks submit in a way no other platform does. iOS is closest to design overall — submitted state, closed state, conflict banner, and vote controls all render correctly — but the allAnswered submit gate is stricter than the design's 'at least one vote' intent. Android's submit gate (any vote) best matches the design; iOS uses allSatisfy (all slots).
screens.verifierSummary: All three draft findings independently confirmed via direct file reads. Finding 1 (Android blocker): MemberPollResponseScreen.kt:133–171 confirmed — no branch on state.submitted anywhere in LoadedPoll; the .submitted field exists on the Loaded state class (ViewModel.kt:69) and is set at ViewModel.kt:227, but the screen never reads it. Finding 2 (Web extra 'Your details' card): poll-response-frames.jsx confirmed to have zero identity-capture UI across all four frames; PollResponse.tsx:237–263 confirmed to render the card. Finding 3 (Web email-valid gate): PollResponse.tsx:87 confirmed. The auditor's characterisation of iOS as 'correct' for submit gating was wrong — PollResponseViewModel.swift:100 uses allSatisfy (all slots must be voted), which is stricter than the design's 'at least one' intent. This was captured as a new minor iOS finding. No draft findings were dropped; no draft finding was found to be based on a misread.

findings.title: Web: header is a full-page article header, not TopBar + trailing Add
findings.designSays: TopBar with centered title 'Who's free' and trailing 'Add' text button (muted when composing/offline) — whos-free-frames.jsx lines 111 and 130
findings.implementationSays: Web renders a page-level <header> with back-arrow → Calendar link, 'Calendarly · Household' eyebrow, h1 'Who's free', subtitle, and a Timezone pill. There is no 'Add' action button anywhere in the page.
findings.evidence: Design whos-free-frames.jsx:111 (FrameComposing), 130 (FrameLoaded); web page.tsx:153-181 — confirmed by grep: zero occurrences of any 'Add' button text

findings.title: Web: legend missing Tentative and Off-hours entries; collapsed to only 3 items
findings.designSays: Legend shows four items: Free, Busy, Tentative, Off-hours (Off-hours replaced by Unknown in opted-out frame) — whos-free-frames.jsx:52-68
findings.implementationSays: Legend in WhosFreeGrid.tsx hard-codes only three items: Free, Busy, Unknown (lines 66-70). Tentative and Off-hours are absent entirely. The Cell component's fallback branch (line 62) collapses all non-free/non-unknown states into one muted style, so there is no amber tentative or hatched off-hours cell even if the data ever provided them.
findings.evidence: Design whos-free-frames.jsx:52-68; WhosFreeGrid.tsx:65-84 — confirmed by direct read

findings.title: Web: non-free/non-unknown cells collapse to one muted style — no Busy/Tentative/Off-hours distinction
findings.designSays: busy=#f3f4f6 solid, tentative=#fef3c7 amber, off-hours=#f9fafb with 45° hatch diagonal pattern — whos-free-frames.jsx:13-15
findings.implementationSays: Cell component has branches for 'free' and 'unknown' only. All other states fall through to 'bg-app-surface-muted/60' (WhosFreeGrid.tsx:62). The file comment explicitly states the API only returns free slots so only free/busy/unknown are possible, but the visual distinction for tentative and off-hours is still absent even as a reserved style.
findings.evidence: Design whos-free-frames.jsx:13-15; WhosFreeGrid.tsx:34-63 — confirmed by direct read

findings.title: Android: 'Add' button not muted when offline — only muted during loading
findings.designSays: FrameOffline (whos-free-frames.jsx:189): right={{ text:'Add', muted:true }} — Add is muted in offline-cached state as well as composing state
findings.implementationSays: WhosFreeScreen.kt line 113: canAdd = state is WhosFreeUiState.Loaded. The sealed interface has no offline-cached state variant — the Loaded state is used for both online and offline-cached scenarios. There is no isOffline check anywhere in the findatime directory. iOS correctly checks isOffline || viewModel.phase == .loading (WhosFreeView.swift:38).
findings.evidence: Design whos-free-frames.jsx:189; WhosFreeScreen.kt:113-114; WhosFreeViewModel.kt:45-63 (no Offline state); WhosFreeView.swift:38 (iOS does it correctly) — all confirmed by direct read

findings.title: Android: tapping a free cell shows inline TappedActionCard in scroll body instead of anchored popover
findings.designSays: Popover floats anchored near the tapped cell (absolute positioned, 134px wide) within the grid card — whos-free-frames.jsx:41-48
findings.implementationSays: TappedActionCard renders as a full-width card below the grid in the scroll body (WhosFreeScreen.kt:206-208, 427-462), replacing the hint row. Also has a dismiss X button (lines 452-456) that is not present in the design. iOS correctly uses a .popover() sheet anchored to the cell (WhosFreeView.swift:138-141).
findings.evidence: Design whos-free-frames.jsx:41-48 (popover inside HeatGrid with position:'absolute'); WhosFreeScreen.kt:206-208 (if/else replaces hint row), 427-462 (TappedActionCard as full-width Column) — confirmed by direct read

findings.title: Android: missing offline banner in loaded state
findings.designSays: FrameOffline (whos-free-frames.jsx:192-197) shows an amber wifi-off banner at the top of the scroll body above FilterChips
findings.implementationSays: LoadedGrid has no offline/network check and renders no offline banner (WhosFreeScreen.kt:163-238). The OfflineBannerHost component exists in the Android codebase (ui/components/OfflineBanner.kt) and is used on other screens, but is not called anywhere in the findatime package. iOS correctly conditionally renders offlineBanner above filterChips (WhosFreeView.swift:108-110).
findings.evidence: Design whos-free-frames.jsx:192-197; WhosFreeScreen.kt:163-238 (no offline check); confirmed by grep: zero occurrences of 'OfflineBanner', 'isOffline', or 'offline' in the findatime directory

findings.title: iOS: popover card is 200pt wide, design specifies 134px
findings.designSays: Popover width is 134px — whos-free-frames.jsx:42 (width:134)
findings.implementationSays: iOS popover card uses .frame(width: 200) — WhosFreeView.swift:425
findings.evidence: Design whos-free-frames.jsx:42; WhosFreeView.swift:425 — confirmed by direct read

findings.title: Android and iOS: Tentative/Off-hours CellState enum values exist but are never populated from API data
findings.designSays: Five distinct cell states: free, busy, tentative, off-hours, unknown — whos-free-frames.jsx:12-16
findings.implementationSays: Both Android (WhosFreeViewModel.kt:31) and iOS (WhosFreeViewModel.swift:29-38) declare all five CellState values including Tentative and OffHours, and both render them with correct visual styles. However, neither dayCells() (Android) nor freeHours() (iOS) ever produces Tentative or OffHours — the logic only yields Free or Busy based on whether free slots overlap the bucket. The backend /whos-free endpoint only returns free windows. Web does not even declare the states. All three platforms are in the same position data-model-wise, but mobile shows correct styles when the states do appear.
findings.evidence: WhosFreeViewModel.kt:174-184 (dayCells returns only Free/Busy); WhosFreeViewModel.swift:147-151 (same); WhosFreeGrid.tsx:3-6 (comment acknowledges limitation); whos-free-frames.jsx:12-16

screens.crossPlatformNotes: iOS is closest to design: it has the correct TopBar+Add pattern (muted when offline/loading), anchored popover, four-item legend (Free/Busy/Tentative/Off-hours or Unknown), offline banner, and diagonal hatch for off-hours/unknown cells. Android matches iOS on legend and hatch but diverges on three major points: (1) the Add button is never muted when offline because there is no offline-cached UI state; (2) the cell-tap action renders as a full-width inline card instead of an anchored popover; and (3) there is no offline banner. Web diverges on all top-bar and legend items: no TopBar component, no Add button, legend has only 3 entries (Free/Busy/Unknown), and all non-free/non-unknown cells share a single muted style.
screens.verifierSummary: All six draft findings (all blocker/major/high-confidence) were independently confirmed by direct source read and are retained without downgrade. Finding 1 (web header): confirmed — page.tsx uses a full article header with zero Add button occurrences. Finding 2 (web legend): confirmed — WhosFreeGrid.tsx Legend function hard-codes exactly three items. Finding 3 (web cell styles): confirmed — Cell component has only free/unknown branches; all others collapse to bg-app-surface-muted/60. Finding 4 (Android Add not muted offline): confirmed — canAdd is literally `state is WhosFreeUiState.Loaded` with no offline check; sealed interface has no Offline state variant. Finding 5 (Android inline card vs popover): confirmed — TappedActionCard is a full-width Column in the scroll body with an extra dismiss X button absent from design. Finding 6 (Android missing offline banner): confirmed by grep — zero occurrences of OfflineBanner/isOffline/offline anywhere in the findatime package. Two new findings added: a nit on iOS popover width (200pt vs design's 134px at WhosFreeView.swift:425), and a minor cross-platform note that Tentative/OffHours CellState enum values exist on both mobile platforms but can never be populated given the current /whos-free API only returns free slots — this explains why web omits them entirely.

findings.title: Web: quiet-hours row is a ToggleRow with different label instead of a DisclosureRow
findings.designSays: DisclosureRow (icon=moon, label='Household quiet hours', value='Weeknights 9 PM') — a disclosure/navigation row with chevron and value text, not a toggle
findings.implementationSays: ToggleRow with label 'Mute reminders during household quiet hours' backed by key 'home_quiet_hours' (boolean toggle). No value display, no chevron, different label.
findings.evidence: Design: availability-settings-frames.jsx:91 `<DisclosureRow icon="moon" label="Household quiet hours" value="Weeknights 9 PM"/>` (DisclosureRow definition with cursor:pointer at lines 52-60). Web: HouseholdAvailabilityForm.tsx:231-238 — ToggleRow with Moon icon and label 'Mute reminders during household quiet hours'.

findings.title: Web: Source section unconditionally rendered in not-set-up state (design omits it)
findings.designSays: FrameNotSetUp (availability-settings-frames.jsx:102-122) shows only ContextHeader + Banner + PrimaryBtn + disabled exposure section — no Source section.
findings.implementationSays: The notSetUp flag only gates the Banner+CTA block (lines 161-182). The Source deep-link section (lines 184-206) is unconditionally rendered regardless of notSetUp. iOS (MyHouseholdAvailabilityView.swift:67-70) and Android (HouseholdAvailabilityScreen.kt:137-141) both correctly gate the Source section to personalIsSetUp.
findings.evidence: Design: availability-settings-frames.jsx:103-121 — FrameNotSetUp has no SourceSection. Web: HouseholdAvailabilityForm.tsx:184-206 — Source div rendered unconditionally with no notSetUp guard. iOS: MyHouseholdAvailabilityView.swift:65-70 — else branch shows sourceSection. Android: HouseholdAvailabilityScreen.kt:137-141 — else branch calls SourceSection.

findings.title: Android: wrong icon on Household quiet hours DisclosureRow (Sunset instead of Moon)
findings.designSays: icon='moon' (data-lucide="moon") on the Household quiet hours DisclosureRow
findings.implementationSays: PantopusIcon.Sunset used instead of PantopusIcon.Moon at the DisclosureRow call site
findings.evidence: Design: availability-settings-frames.jsx:91 `icon="moon"`. Android: HouseholdAvailabilityScreen.kt:391 `icon = PantopusIcon.Sunset`.

findings.title: Android: DisclosureRow (quiet hours) is not tappable — missing clickable modifier and onClick
findings.designSays: DisclosureRow has cursor:'pointer' — it is a tappable navigation row that should open a quiet-hours configuration screen
findings.implementationSays: The DisclosureRow composable (lines 496-533) accepts no onClick parameter and the Row has no .clickable{} modifier — it is purely inert display text. The call site at lines 390-394 passes no tap handler.
findings.evidence: Design: availability-settings-frames.jsx:54 `cursor:'pointer'`. Android: HouseholdAvailabilityScreen.kt:496-533 — DisclosureRow composable signature has no onClick/onTap; Row modifier chain at line 505-508 has only fillMaxWidth+padding.

findings.title: iOS: DisclosureRow (quiet hours) is not tappable — no onTap parameter or Button wrapper
findings.designSays: DisclosureRow has cursor:'pointer' — a tappable navigation row
findings.implementationSays: iOS DisclosureRow struct (lines 360-385) takes only icon/label/value — no onTap parameter, no Button wrapper, no NavigationLink. It renders as inert display text identical to Android's bug.
findings.evidence: Design: availability-settings-frames.jsx:54 `cursor:'pointer'`. iOS: MyHouseholdAvailabilityView.swift:360-385 — DisclosureRow struct declaration has no onTap parameter; body is a plain VStack with no Button. Called at line 149-153 with no tap handler.

findings.title: Web: top-bar chrome is leading-title + overline breadcrumb instead of centered TopBar
findings.designSays: TopBar with centered title 'My availability' (availability-settings-frames.jsx:67-68 `<Bar/>` → `<TopBar title='My availability'/>`). No overline breadcrumb in chrome.
findings.implementationSays: Custom heading block: overline 'Household' in Home green (text-[11px] font-bold uppercase) + left-aligned h1 'My availability' (text-xl font-bold), plus a bare ArrowLeft button — not a standard TopBar primitive.
findings.evidence:
Design: availability-settings-frames.jsx:67-68 `function Bar({ title='My availability' }) { return ; }`. Web: page.tsx:31-35 — `
Household

My availability

`.

screens.crossPlatformNotes: iOS and Android share one cross-platform bug that the original audit only flagged for Android: the DisclosureRow (quiet-hours row) has no tap handler on either platform (iOS MyHouseholdAvailabilityView.swift:360-385, Android HouseholdAvailabilityScreen.kt:496-533). Android additionally uses the wrong icon (Sunset vs Moon). Web diverges most severely: it replaces the DisclosureRow with a boolean toggle, renders the Source section unconditionally in the not-set-up state, and uses a bespoke heading/breadcrumb block instead of a standard TopBar. iOS is closest to design among the three platforms (correct DisclosureRow shape with moon icon and value text, correct Source section gating) but still misses the tap handler. One draft finding was fully refuted: the PrimaryBtn color (Finding 6) — the design's home-shell.jsx PrimaryBtn explicitly uses H.accent (#16a34a, Home green), so all three platforms correctly implement Home green and are not diverging from spec.
screens.verifierSummary: Confirmed 5 of 6 draft findings and added 1 new finding. Refuted 1 (low-confidence) finding. REFUTED: 'iOS not-set-up CTA uses Home green instead of primary CTA style' — the design's PrimaryBtn in home-shell.jsx line 193 uses `background: H.accent` where H.accent = '#16a34a' (Home green), meaning all three platforms are correct. CONFIRMED: (1) Web quiet-hours ToggleRow vs DisclosureRow — verified in HouseholdAvailabilityForm.tsx:231-238 vs design:91. (2) Web Source section unconditionally rendered in not-set-up state — confirmed HouseholdAvailabilityForm.tsx:184-206 has no notSetUp guard, while iOS:67-70 and Android:137-141 correctly gate it. (3) Android Sunset icon vs Moon — confirmed HouseholdAvailabilityScreen.kt:391. (4) Android DisclosureRow not clickable — confirmed composable at lines 496-533 has no onClick param and no .clickable modifier. (5) Web top-bar breadcrumb vs TopBar — confirmed page.tsx:31-35. ADDED: iOS DisclosureRow (quiet hours) also not tappable — MyHouseholdAvailabilityView.swift:360-385 DisclosureRow struct has no onTap parameter and no Button wrapper, same structural bug as Android but not flagged in the original audit. Line numbers in all surviving findings were verified against the actual source.

findings.title: Android empty state: bespoke explainer card + template rows absent — generic EmptyState used instead
findings.designSays: Empty frame (FrameEmpty) shows an explainer card + 'TEMPLATES' overline + 5 tappable template rows (Guest room, Driveway, EV charger, Tools, Other). Subcopy reads 'Start from a template.' resources-list-frames.jsx:44–66
findings.implementationSays: Android maps Empty to generic ListOfRowsUiState.Empty, which renders EmptyState(icon, headline, subcopy='Anything members book — rooms, the driveway, tools. Add your first resource to get started.', ctaTitle='Add a resource'). No TEMPLATES overline, no 5 template rows. Subcopy text also diverges from design. iOS renders bespoke emptyFrame with explainerCard and TemplateRow for each template.
findings.evidence: ResourceListScreen.kt:86-95 (Empty mapping); ListOfRowsScreen.kt:220-229 (EmptyState render); EmptyState.kt:44-93 (generic component); ResourceListView.swift:105-126 (iOS bespoke emptyFrame); resources-list-frames.jsx:44-66 (design)

findings.title: Android error state: wrong icon (AlertCircle vs cloud-off), wrong headline and CTA label
findings.designSays: Error frame uses cloud-off icon inside a 56dp errorBg circle, headline 'Couldn't load resources', body 'Check your connection and try again.', CTA label 'Retry' with rotate-cw icon. resources-list-frames.jsx:101–113
findings.implementationSays: Android generic ErrorBanner at ListOfRowsScreen.kt:981-1009 renders AlertCircle icon (size 40dp, no errorBg circle), headline 'Couldn't load the list', CTA 'Try again' (no icon). iOS errorFrame at ResourceListView.swift:189-211 matches design exactly (cloud-off in errorBg circle, 'Couldn't load resources', 'Retry' + refreshCw icon).
findings.evidence: ListOfRowsScreen.kt:982-1009 (ErrorBanner); ResourceListScreen.kt:96 (maps Error to ListOfRowsUiState.Error); resources-list-frames.jsx:101-113 (design); ResourceListView.swift:189-204 (iOS correct implementation)

findings.title: Android empty state icon: PantopusIcon.Package (Inventory2 filled) instead of package-open

findings.implementationSays: Android passes icon=PantopusIcon.Package to EmptyState (ResourceListScreen.kt:88). Icons.kt:755 maps PantopusIcon.Package to Icons.Filled.Inventory2 (a solid closed box). PantopusIcon.PackageOpen does not exist in Icons.kt. iOS correctly uses Icon(.packageOpen) at ResourceListView.swift:130.
findings.evidence: ResourceListScreen.kt:88 (uses PantopusIcon.Package); Icons.kt:346 (Package enum entry); Icons.kt:755 (maps to Inventory2); Icons.kt (grep for PackageOpen: no result); resources-list-frames.jsx:51 (design)

findings.title: Android: no offline banner and no row dimming when offline
findings.designSays: Offline frame: amber wifi-off Banner above list, all rows rendered at opacity 0.55, no FAB. resources-list-frames.jsx:115-126
findings.implementationSays: ResourceListScreen.kt has no OfflineBannerHost, no offline detection, and no dim signal to rows. ListOfRowsScreen has no offline parameter. OfflineBannerHost exists in OfflineBanner.kt but is only used by BusinessTeamScreen.kt. iOS correctly uses .offlineBanner(isOffline: isOffline) modifier (ResourceListView.swift:67) and passes dim: isOffline to ResourceRow (ResourceListView.swift:91).
findings.evidence: ResourceListScreen.kt:40-78 (no offline handling); ListOfRowsScreen.kt:110-241 (no offline parameter); OfflineBanner.kt:109-137 (OfflineBannerHost component exists but unused here); resources-list-frames.jsx:115-126 (design)

findings.title: Web: offline state entirely missing (no amber banner, no dimmed rows)
findings.designSays: Offline frame (FrameOffline): amber wifi-off Banner at top, rows rendered at opacity 0.55, no FAB. resources-list-frames.jsx:115–126
findings.implementationSays: Web ResourceList.tsx has only loading/error/empty/loaded branches — no offline/network detection. Page.tsx has no NetworkMonitor or OfflineBanner. Web has OfflineIndicator in map components only; it is not wired to the resources list.
findings.evidence: ResourceList.tsx:83-218 (no offline branch); page.tsx (no NetworkMonitor import); resources-list-frames.jsx:115-126 (FrameOffline)

screens.crossPlatformNotes: iOS is the closest to design: it implements bespoke empty frame with template rows, correct error state (cloud-off icon, errorBg circle, 'Couldn't load resources', 'Retry' + rotate-cw), and offline banner with row dimming. Android diverges on all four of these areas by delegating to the generic ListOfRowsUiState.Empty and ListOfRowsScreen.ErrorBanner shared components, which have different copy, icons, and no bespoke template-row rendering. Web matches iOS on error and empty states (uses cloud-off icon, correct copy, PackageOpen icon, template rows) but is missing the offline frame entirely — no amber banner, no row dimming. The one finding the draft auditor marked low-confidence (Android top-bar 'Add' text) is NOT a bug: TopBarActionButton renders text when label is non-null, and ResourceListScreen.kt correctly passes label='Add'.
screens.verifierSummary: Confirmed 5 findings; refuted 1. All 5 major/blocker findings were independently verified against source files. CONFIRMED: (1) Android empty state uses generic EmptyState with no template rows — bespoke explainer card and TEMPLATES section absent (ResourceListScreen.kt:86-95 vs design lines 44-66); (2) Android ErrorBanner uses AlertCircle+no-circle, 'Couldn't load the list', 'Try again' — all three diverge from design's cloud-off+errorBg, 'Couldn't load resources', 'Retry'+rotate-cw (ListOfRowsScreen.kt:982-1009 vs design lines 101-113); (3) Android PantopusIcon.Package maps to Inventory2 filled box, not package-open — PackageOpen not registered in Icons.kt at all; (4) Android has no OfflineBannerHost and no row-dim signal — OfflineBanner.kt exists but is only wired to BusinessTeamScreen, not any scheduling screen; (5) Web has no offline detection or amber banner anywhere in ResourceList.tsx or the resources page.tsx. REFUTED: Finding 6 (Android top-bar icon vs text) — code at ResourceListScreen.kt:62-68 passes label='Add', and TopBarActionButton (ListOfRowsScreen.kt:1991-2009) branches on label!=null to render a Text composable instead of an IconButton. The action correctly renders as text 'Add', matching the design. This finding was correctly flagged as low-confidence and is now dropped.

findings.title: iOS delete confirm uses native action sheet instead of designed centered Dialog
findings.designSays: Frame 5 (FrameDelete): a centered Dialog component with a trash-2 icon, title 'Delete EV charger?', body text, and two stacked full-width 44dp buttons — red 'Delete' on top, bordered 'Keep' below (resource-editor-frames.jsx:167–184)
findings.implementationSays: iOS uses .confirmationDialog(...) (ResourceEditorView.swift:54–65) which renders as a native bottom action sheet, not a centered alert-style dialog with icon and stacked full-width buttons. Android correctly uses AlertDialog with the two stacked full-width buttons (ResourceEditorScreen.kt:376–421).
findings.evidence: resource-editor-frames.jsx:167–184 (Dialog with trash-2 icon + stacked buttons) vs ResourceEditorView.swift:54–65 (.confirmationDialog). Android ResourceEditorScreen.kt:376–421 (AlertDialog with HomePrimaryButtonStub + HomeSecondaryButton stacked in Column).

findings.title: Android stepper uses ChevronDown/ChevronUp icons instead of Minus/Plus
findings.designSays: The Stepper component uses '−' decrement and '+' increment glyphs (resource-editor-frames.jsx:53 uses <Stepper>; Web primitives.tsx:339,357 canonically render Minus/Plus Lucide icons).
findings.implementationSays: Android CounterRow uses PantopusIcon.ChevronDown for decrement and PantopusIcon.ChevronUp for increment (ResourceComponents.kt:489,500). iOS correctly uses .minus/.plus (ResourceEditorView.swift:456,462). Web correctly uses Minus/Plus (primitives.tsx:346,357).
findings.evidence: ResourceComponents.kt:489 (ChevronDown) and :500 (ChevronUp) vs primitives.tsx:346 (Minus) and :357 (Plus) vs ResourceEditorView.swift:456 (.minus) and :462 (.plus).

findings.title: Web saving state: no dimmed-form overlay — only shows inline spinner in top bar
findings.designSays: Frame 4 (FrameSaving): form content is dimmed to opacity 0.45, and a centered white card floats above with a spinning loader-circle (home-green, 26px) and 'Saving resource' label (resource-editor-frames.jsx:146–160).
findings.implementationSays: When saving=true, ResourceEditor.tsx:225–227 renders only a Loader2 spinner (h-4 w-4, text-app-text-muted) replacing the Save button in the top bar. No form dimming (opacity) and no centered overlay card are rendered. iOS implements savingOverlay correctly (ResourceEditorView.swift:39–44, 294–308). Android implements SavingOverlay correctly with .alpha(0.45f) form dimming plus centered card (ResourceEditorScreen.kt:140, 146–153; ResourceComponents.kt:866–897).
findings.evidence: resource-editor-frames.jsx:146–160 vs ResourceEditor.tsx:225–227 (only inline Loader2). ResourceEditorView.swift:39 (.opacity(0.45)), :41–45 (.overlay { savingOverlay }), :294–308. ResourceEditorScreen.kt:198 (.alpha(0.45f)), :146–153 (SavingOverlay).

findings.title: Web Overline typography: 9.5px instead of design-token 11px
findings.designSays: Overline text-size is 11px / font-weight 600 / letter-spacing 0.06em per colors_and_type.css:128 (--type-over: 11px). All section overlines (Photo, Who can book, Available hours, Booking rules) use this size.
findings.implementationSays: Web Overline component in primitives.tsx:91 renders text-[9.5px] with font-bold (700), which is 1.5px smaller than the design token and smaller than iOS (11pt via .pantopusTextStyle(.overline)) and Android (11.sp per ResourceComponents.kt:318).
findings.evidence: primitives.tsx:91 (text-[9.5px] font-bold) vs colors_and_type.css:128 (--type-over: 11px; --type-over-w: 600) vs ResourceEditorView.swift:167 (.pantopusTextStyle(.overline) → 11pt) vs ResourceComponents.kt:318 (fontSize = 11.sp).

findings.title: Web delete confirm dialog: buttons are horizontal side-by-side, missing trash-2 icon
findings.designSays: Frame 5 (FrameDelete): dialog shows a trash-2 icon at the top, and two stacked full-width 44dp buttons (resource-editor-frames.jsx:168–172). Android also renders the icon + stacked buttons correctly (ResourceEditorScreen.kt:378–420).
findings.implementationSays: Web uses the shared ConfirmDialog (ConfirmDialog.tsx:126) which renders buttons side-by-side with flex justify-end gap-3 (not stacked full-width), and has no icon parameter — the trash-2 icon is absent from the dialog. The dialog card uses max-w-md rather than the compact dialog width.
findings.evidence: resource-editor-frames.jsx:168–172 (trash-2 icon + stacked full-width buttons) vs ConfirmDialog.tsx:126 (flex justify-end gap-3 horizontal layout, no icon slot). ResourceEditor.tsx:185–192 passes confirmStore.open() with no icon option.

findings.title: Android SavingOverlay uses CircularProgressIndicator instead of loader-circle icon
findings.designSays: Frame 4 (FrameSaving): the overlay uses a loader-circle Lucide icon (resource-editor-frames.jsx:155: data-lucide='loader-circle'). iOS uses Icon(.loaderCircle) — a partial-arc line icon — matching the design.
findings.implementationSays: Android SavingOverlay (ResourceComponents.kt:884–888) uses CircularProgressIndicator (a Material3 ring spinner with a gap), which is visually similar but not identical to the Lucide loader-circle partial-arc icon. The spinning behavior and color are correct.
findings.evidence: resource-editor-frames.jsx:155 (loader-circle Lucide icon) vs ResourceComponents.kt:884 (CircularProgressIndicator) vs ResourceEditorView.swift:413 (Icon(.loaderCircle)).

screens.crossPlatformNotes: iOS and Android diverge on the delete confirm UI: iOS renders a native bottom action sheet (.confirmationDialog), Android renders a centered AlertDialog with stacked full-width buttons matching the design. Web uses a shared ConfirmDialog with horizontal side-by-side buttons and no icon — neither matches the design's stacked-full-width layout, though it is at least a centered dialog. Android is closest to design on the delete confirm and saving overlay; iOS is closest on most other elements. The stepper iconography gap is Android-only (ChevronDown/Up vs Minus/Plus). The Overline 9.5px vs 11px typography token gap is web-only. iOS is re-classified from 'minor' to 'major' due to the delete confirm action sheet deviation.
screens.verifierSummary: All four draft major findings independently confirmed via direct source reads. (1) iOS .confirmationDialog confirmed at ResourceEditorView.swift:54–65 — clearly a bottom sheet, not the designed centered Dialog. (2) Android ChevronDown/ChevronUp confirmed at ResourceComponents.kt:489,500 vs Minus/Plus in Web primitives.tsx:346,357 and iOS ResourceEditorView.swift:456,462. (3) Web saving state confirmed: ResourceEditor.tsx:225–227 shows only an inline Loader2 in the top bar with no form dimming or overlay card — iOS and Android both implement the full overlay correctly. (4) Web Overline confirmed at primitives.tsx:91 as text-[9.5px], vs design token 11px at colors_and_type.css:128. Two additional findings added: (5) Web ConfirmDialog uses horizontal side-by-side buttons and has no icon slot (ConfirmDialog.tsx:126), diverging from the design's stacked full-width buttons with trash-2 icon — this is a minor issue since it is a shared component. (6) Android SavingOverlay uses CircularProgressIndicator instead of loader-circle icon (nit); iOS correctly uses Icon(.loaderCircle). No draft findings were refuted or downgraded. iOS status upgraded from 'minor' to 'major' due to the confirmed delete confirm action sheet deviation.

findings.title: Android: pending-approval badge tap handler is a no-op (dead onClick = {})
findings.designSays: Pending-approval badge in header card is a button (cursor:pointer) that navigates to or anchors the approval queue
findings.implementationSays: Android passes onClick = {} (empty lambda) directly to PendingApprovalBadge at call site — no VM hook exists at all. iOS wires the badge to viewModel.openApprovalQueue() which is at least a named hook, but its body is also currently empty (ResourceDetailViewModel.swift line 249); the comment notes scroll-anchor intent, so iOS is structurally correct even if the scroll is unimplemented. Android has no equivalent hook, making it harder to complete later.
findings.evidence: design: resource-detail-frames.jsx lines 23-29 (button with cursor:pointer); Android: ResourceDetailScreen.kt line 300 `PendingApprovalBadge(count = loaded.pendingApprovalCount, onClick = {})`; iOS view: ResourceDetailView.swift line 104 `viewModel.openApprovalQueue()`; iOS VM: ResourceDetailViewModel.swift line 249 `func openApprovalQueue() {}`

findings.title: Web: sticky-footer CTAs missing icons (plus / calendar-clock)
findings.designSays: Frame 1 StickyFooter has PrimaryBtn icon='plus' for 'Book this'; Frame 3 has PrimaryBtn icon='calendar-clock' for 'Book next opening · Sat 9 AM'
findings.implementationSays: Both web PrimaryButton calls in the sticky footer (ResourceDetail.tsx lines 414-423) pass no icon prop. PrimaryButton supports icon?: LucideIcon (primitives.tsx line 438) but it is omitted at both call sites. Neither Plus nor CalendarClock is imported.
findings.evidence: design: resource-detail-frames.jsx lines 85, 122; web: ResourceDetail.tsx lines 414-423 (no icon= on either CTA); primitives.tsx lines 435-464 (icon prop supported but optional)

findings.title: Web: pending-approval header badge missing chevron-right, non-interactive (div instead of button)
findings.designSays: Pending-approval badge is a <button> with clock icon + text + chevron-right and cursor:pointer
findings.implementationSays: Web renders a <div> (not a button) with only clock + text span; no ChevronRight icon, no onClick handler. ChevronRight is not imported anywhere in the file.
findings.evidence: design: resource-detail-frames.jsx lines 23-29; web: ResourceDetail.tsx lines 9-17 imports (no ChevronRight), lines 286-293 (div element, no onClick, no ChevronRight)

findings.title: Web: fully-booked banner uses wrong icon (Clock instead of CalendarX)
findings.designSays: Frame 3 fully-booked Banner uses icon='calendar-x' (design line 114)
findings.implementationSays: Web passes icon={Clock} to the Banner at line 297 — Clock is already imported for the pending-approval badge. CalendarX is not imported at all.
findings.evidence: design: resource-detail-frames.jsx line 114 `<Banner tone='amber' icon='calendar-x' ...>`; web: ResourceDetail.tsx line 297 `<Banner tone='amber' icon={Clock} ...>`; imports lines 9-17 (no CalendarX)

screens.crossPlatformNotes: Android and iOS are both structurally aligned on all frames (header card, rule chips, approval queue card, booking sections, sticky footer with correct icons). The one cross-platform gap is the pending-approval badge tap: iOS has a named VM hook (openApprovalQueue, currently no-op but hookable) while Android passes a raw empty lambda with no VM method — Android needs a VM method added to be on par. Web is furthest from design: CTA icons missing on both footer buttons, pending-badge is a non-interactive div (not a button), and the fully-booked banner uses the wrong icon. iOS is closest to design; Android is major only on the onClick gap."
screens.verifierSummary: CONFIRMED (3 major findings): (1) Android onClick={} confirmed at ResourceDetailScreen.kt:300 — the PendingApprovalBadge component does wire its onClick into a clickable modifier (ResourceComponents.kt:943), so the button renders correctly but fires nothing. Auditor was right that this is major. Correction: iOS viewModel.openApprovalQueue() (ResourceDetailView.swift:104) is also a no-op body at VM level (ResourceDetailViewModel.swift:249) but at least has a named hook with documented intent (scroll anchor); Android has no VM hook at all. Scope stays Android-only for the severity finding. (2) Web sticky-footer CTA icons confirmed missing — neither Plus nor CalendarClock appears in ResourceDetail.tsx lines 414-423 or in the imports. (3) Web pending-approval badge confirmed as a non-interactive <div> with no ChevronRight and no onClick (ResourceDetail.tsx lines 286-293). DROPPED: Finding 4 (Android ResourceOverlineLabel before empty-state check, low-confidence nit) — both platforms always emit the overline label in both the empty and non-empty branches; this is functionally identical and not a real discrepancy. The auditor's own evidence said 'functionally identical' making this a non-finding. ADDED: Web fully-booked banner uses Clock icon instead of the design-specified CalendarX (ResourceDetail.tsx:297 vs design frame line 114); CalendarX is not imported. This is a minor design-parity miss the first auditor overlooked while verifying the web imports. Line numbers in all three confirmed findings match the actual file content.

findings.title: Web: Notes section completely absent
findings.designSays: Frame 1 (Default) renders a 'Notes' Section (overline='Notes') with a multiline optional note field — book-resource-frames.jsx:82: <Section overline='Notes'><Field placeholder='Add a note (optional)' multiline/>
findings.implementationSays: BookResource.tsx has no Notes section or textarea at all. The three content sections rendered are: rules chips, the When Card, and the For Whom Card. No import or usage of a Notes/textarea element exists anywhere in the file.
findings.evidence: book-resource-frames.jsx:82 (Section overline='Notes' with multiline Field); BookResource.tsx — no match for 'note' or 'Notes' in 531 lines. iOS counterpart: BookResourceView.swift:74,213–227 (notesSection SectionCard + TextField). Android counterpart: BookResourceScreen.kt:223–225 (SectionCard overline='Notes' + NoteField).

findings.title: Web: Submitting state lacks full-form opacity fade and centered floating overlay
findings.designSays: Frame 4 (Submitting): entire form body fades to opacity 0.45 AND a centered white-card overlay with a spinning loader-circle and 'Booking the charger' label floats above — book-resource-frames.jsx:127 (opacity:0.45 on form div), 133–140 (absolute-positioned centered overlay card).
findings.implementationSays: BookResource.tsx has no opacity fade on the form body and no centered overlay. When submitting=true the PrimaryButton receives loading=true which renders a small CSS border-spinner inside the button only. No wrapper-level opacity change and no floating overlay composable.
findings.evidence: book-resource-frames.jsx:127 (opacity:0.45), 133–140 (centered overlay); BookResource.tsx:496–504 (PrimaryButton loading prop only, no form-level fade). iOS correct: BookResourceView.swift:81–87 (.opacity(0.45) + submittingOverlay). Android correct: BookResourceScreen.kt:155–161 (alpha(0.45f) + SavingOverlay at line 158).

findings.title: Web: Error state missing icon and Retry action
findings.designSays: No explicit error frame in design. Cross-platform baseline (both native platforms): cloud-off error icon + error headline + message body + Retry button wired to reload.
findings.implementationSays: BookResource.tsx loadErr path (lines 303–314) shows only a bold text headline 'Couldn't load this resource' and a 'Go back' PrimaryButton that calls onCancel (not reload). No error icon, no descriptive message, no retry action.
findings.evidence: BookResource.tsx:303–314 (text headline + Go back button only); BookResourceView.swift:319–336 (Icon(.cloudOff, size:40) + headline + message + HomePrimaryButton 'Retry' -> viewModel.load()); BookResourceScreen.kt:110–115 (ErrorState(headline:, message:, onRetry: viewModel::load)).

findings.title: All platforms: Section overline uses home-600 green; design specifies accent700 (darker shade)
findings.designSays: Section overlines are colored H.accent700 (a darker home green, 700-weight) — book-resource-frames.jsx:68: <Overline color={H.accent700}>
findings.implementationSays: All three platforms use the 600-level home green for section overlines. iOS SectionCard hardcodes Theme.Color.home (ResourceComponents.swift:253); Android SectionCard defaults to PantopusColors.home (ResourceComponents.kt:296); Web Overline uses text-app-home which maps to --color-identity-home: #16A34A via tailwind.config.js:57. A homeDark token exists on iOS (Colors.swift:83) but is not used in SectionCard. H.accent700 has no explicit CSS variable in colors_and_type.css so its exact hex is unresolvable.
findings.evidence: book-resource-frames.jsx:68 (color:{H.accent700}); iOS ResourceComponents.swift:253 (foregroundStyle(Theme.Color.home)); Android ResourceComponents.kt:296 (overlineColor: Color = PantopusColors.home); web primitives.tsx Overline (text-app-home); tailwind.config.js:57 (app-home -> --color-identity-home: #16A34A); iOS Colors.swift:77,83 (home vs homeDark both defined).

screens.crossPlatformNotes: iOS and Android are closely matched and both implement all design patterns correctly (Notes section, submitting overlay with alpha fade + centered SavingOverlay, error state with icon + retry). The only shared divergence across all three platforms is the overline color using home-600 green instead of the design's accent700 (darker). Web is the outlier: it is missing the Notes section entirely, lacks the full-form submitting overlay, and has a degraded error state with no icon or retry action. iOS is closest to design.
screens.verifierSummary:
All four draft findings were independently verified against source files.

CONFIRMED (3 major findings, all web-only):
1. Web Notes absence — fully confirmed. BookResource.tsx (531 lines) has zero references to 'note' or 'Notes'. iOS BookResourceView.swift:213–227 and Android BookResourceScreen.kt:223–225 both implement it correctly.
2. Web submitting overlay — confirmed. Form body has no opacity wrapper and no floating overlay card. The CTA does show a small border-spinner via PrimaryButton loading prop, but this is not the design's full-form fade + centered overlay pattern. iOS (.opacity(0.45) + submittingOverlay at BookResourceView.swift:81–87) and Android (alpha(0.45f) + SavingOverlay at BookResourceScreen.kt:155–161) both implement it correctly.
3. Web error state — confirmed. Only shows text + 'Go back' with no icon, no message, no retry. Scope clarified to cross-platform (design has no explicit error frame but both native platforms define the pattern). Finding retained at major.

CORRECTED (1 nit finding):
4. SectionCard overline color nit — confirmed as low-confidence but extended from iOS-only to all three platforms. iOS, Android, and web all use home-600 green. iOS does have Theme.Color.homeDark (Colors.swift:83) which could serve as the 700-level equivalent but SectionCard does not use it. The design token H.accent700 has no explicit definition in colors_and_type.css making the exact target hex unresolvable.

findings.title: "Offer these times" section entirely absent — weekday grid + time-of-day chips missing
findings.designSays: Frame 1 (FrameDefault) includes a fourth Section with overline "Offer these times", containing a WeekdayGrid (S M T W T F S toggle row) and time-of-day pill chips ("Mornings only" / "9 AM – 12 PM") — visit-setup-frames.jsx:82
findings.implementationSays: No weekday grid, no time-of-day chip strip on any platform. iOS readyBody lists only explainer/detailsGroup/hostsGroup/whenGroup/accessGroup. Android Column content stops at Access SectionCard. Web ends at Access Section. Both iOS and Android ViewModels include code comments acknowledging this as an intentional v1 deferral (no backend for the slot-offering engine), but the gap is real.
findings.evidence: Design: visit-setup-frames.jsx:82. iOS: ScheduleVisitView.swift:55-61 (readyBody), ScheduleVisitViewModel.swift:8-9 (deferral comment). Android: VisitSetupScreen.kt:178-295. Web: VisitSetup.tsx:136-284, line 6-7 (deferral comment).

findings.title: "Generate a shareable booking link" toggle section absent on all platforms
findings.designSays: Frame 1 includes a final Section with a ValueRow labelled "Generate a shareable booking link" and a Toggle (on) — visit-setup-frames.jsx:84
findings.implementationSays: No such toggle section exists on any platform. iOS, Android, and Web all end the form at the Access section. Explicitly deferred per code comments (no v1 backend for the shareable link feature).
findings.evidence: Design: visit-setup-frames.jsx:84. iOS: ScheduleVisitView.swift:55-61 — no shareable-link row. Android: VisitSetupScreen.kt:280-295 — ends at Access SectionCard. Web: VisitSetup.tsx:274-281 — ends at Access section.

findings.title: "Generating" spinner state absent on all platforms
findings.designSays: Frame 4 (FrameGenerating) shows a full-screen generating state: spinning ring with door-open icon, subtitle "Building the slots you'll offer" + sub-copy, and three shimmer rows — visit-setup-frames.jsx:129-148
findings.implementationSays: No generating / slot-building intermediate state on any platform. iOS: isSaving passed to FormShell which shows a top-bar indicator only. Android: small CircularProgressIndicator in the top bar only. Web: small Loader2 spinner in the bar only. None render the full-screen FrameGenerating state.
findings.evidence: Design: visit-setup-frames.jsx:129-148. iOS: ScheduleVisitView.swift:26-27 (isSaving passed to FormShell). Android: VisitSetupScreen.kt:113-118. Web: VisitSetup.tsx:151-163.

findings.title: "Link created" success state absent on all platforms

findings.implementationSays: On save success all platforms navigate away immediately: iOS pushes .visitDetail route (ScheduleVisitViewModel.swift:136), Android navigates to visitDetail (VisitSetupScreen.kt:124), Web calls onCreated callback which triggers router navigation (VisitSetup.tsx:126). No inline link-created/success state is rendered within the setup sheet on any platform.
findings.evidence: Design: visit-setup-frames.jsx:151-170. iOS: ScheduleVisitViewModel.swift:136. Android: VisitSetupScreen.kt:123-125. Web: VisitSetup.tsx:114-127.

findings.title: Primary CTA label: design says "Next", all implementations say "Schedule"
findings.designSays: SheetBar action is "Next" in both the default and invalid frames — visit-setup-frames.jsx:76 (FrameDefault) and visit-setup-frames.jsx:94 (FrameInvalid). The generating/link-created frames represent subsequent phases; the setup phase CTA is "Next".
findings.implementationSays: All three platforms use "Schedule" as the CTA label. iOS: ScheduleVisitView.swift:24 (rightActionLabel: "Schedule"). Android: VisitSetupScreen.kt:131 (Text("Schedule")). Web: VisitSetup.tsx:159-161 (button text "Schedule").
findings.evidence: Design: visit-setup-frames.jsx:76, 94. iOS: ScheduleVisitView.swift:24. Android: VisitSetupScreen.kt:131. Web: VisitSetup.tsx:159-161.

findings.title: "When" section: concrete date/time pickers instead of offered-window range chip + Visit-length Stepper
findings.designSays: WindowDuration shows a ValueRow labelled "Offered window" with a date-range chip (e.g. "Jun 16 – Jun 27") and a ValueRow labelled "Visit length" with a Stepper (1 hr) — visit-setup-frames.jsx:61-69
findings.implementationSays: iOS renders a concrete DatePicker ("Date") + DatePicker ("Start time") + CounterRow ("Visit length") — a single concrete datetime, not an offered window. Android is identical in structure with PickerValueRow for Date and Start time plus CounterRow. Web shows a datetime-local input labelled "Starts" plus a Stepper for "Visit length". The concrete-datetime approach is an intentional v1 simplification per code comments, but is structurally different from the design.
findings.evidence: Design: visit-setup-frames.jsx:61-69. iOS: ScheduleVisitView.swift:148-158. Android: VisitSetupScreen.kt:252-278. Web: VisitSetup.tsx:244-271.

screens.crossPlatformNotes: iOS and Android are structurally equivalent to each other and both implement more design fidelity than web (correct explainer copy, LinkAccessCode row in Access section). All three platforms share the same 6 major intentional deferrals: the \"Offer these times\" weekday-grid/chip section, the shareable-booking-link toggle, the FrameGenerating full-screen state, the FrameLinkCreated success state, the \"Next\" CTA label (all use \"Schedule\"), and the concrete date/time pickers vs the design's offered-window date-range. Web additionally diverges on explainer copy and is missing the LinkAccessCode row, making web the furthest from design. iOS is closest to design among the three, ahead of Android by a negligible margin (both implement the same features).
screens.verifierSummary: All 6 draft blocker/major findings were independently confirmed by reading the actual source files. No finding was refuted or downgraded. Citation line numbers were verified as accurate (minor off-by-one on web CTA line noted but finding is correct). Key confirmation details: (1) iOS readyBody at lines 55-61 lists exactly 5 sections with no \"Offer these times\" or shareable-link section; (2) Android VisitSetupBody Column ends definitively at the Access SectionCard at line 280; (3) Web ends at Access Section at line 274 with the same absence; (4) The generating state (FrameGenerating) and link-created state (FrameLinkCreated) are absent on all platforms — confirmed by checking all save/success paths in ViewModels; (5) CTA label \"Schedule\" confirmed at exact cited lines on all three platforms; (6) The When section concrete-datetime vs offered-window difference is confirmed. Code comments in both iOS ScheduleVisitViewModel.swift:8-9 and Web VisitSetup.tsx:6-7 explicitly acknowledge the slot-offering engine and shareable link as intentional v1 deferrals with no backend support, but these are genuine parity gaps nonetheless. Two additional findings were added: web explainer banner copy differs from design and both native platforms (\"marked busy on the household calendar\" vs \"Slots come from when your chosen hosts are personally free.\"); and web is missing the \"Link an access code\" chevron row in the Access section that both iOS and Android implement per the AccessNote design spec.

findings.title: ShareManage card entirely absent on all platforms
findings.designSays: ShareManage card present in offered/reserved/active-link/expired-link/revoked states showing shareable booking URL, link status pill, slots counter, Copy/QR buttons, Revoke/Extend/Re-issue actions — design frames VisitOffered (L143), VisitReserved (L159), VisitActiveLink (L235), VisitExpiredLink (L251), VisitRevoked (L266)
findings.implementationSays: No ShareManage or equivalent component on any platform. iOS VisitDetailView.swift loadedBody (L73-91) has no share/link section. Android VisitLoaded (L208-216) has no share card. Web VisitDetail.tsx line 7 explicitly documents this as intentionally omitted ('The design's shareable-booking-link states … are intentionally omitted').
findings.evidence: Design: visit-detail-frames.jsx:84-118 (ShareManage fn), L143, L159, L235, L251, L266; iOS: VisitDetailView.swift:73-91; Android: VisitDetailScreen.kt:208-216; Web: VisitDetail.tsx:7

findings.title: Edit button shown only for the 'confirmed' derived lifecycle — design shows it on all non-terminal states
findings.designSays: TopBar trailing 'Edit' button present and active in offered/reserved/confirmed/active-link/expired-link/revoked states (design frames L138, L152, L168, L231, L246, L261); muted (gray) in completed/cancelled/no-show states (L185, L200, L215)
findings.implementationSays: iOS VisitDetailViewModel.Lifecycle has only .confirmed and .done; VisitDetailView.swift L38 guards Edit to `viewModel.lifecycle == .confirmed`. Android VisitDetailViewModel VisitLifecycle has only Confirmed and Done; VisitDetailScreen.kt L118 guards Edit to `loaded.lifecycle == VisitLifecycle.Confirmed`. Neither platform models offered/reserved/link states, so the Edit button can never appear for those 6 of 9 design states. The muted-Edit behaviour on terminal states is also unimplemented.
findings.evidence: Design: visit-detail-frames.jsx:138, 152, 168, 231, 246, 261; iOS: VisitDetailView.swift:38-44; VisitDetailViewModel.swift:26-29 (Lifecycle enum); Android: VisitDetailScreen.kt:117-130; VisitDetailViewModel.kt:33 (VisitLifecycle enum)

findings.title: Only 2 of 9 lifecycle states rendered — offered/reserved/cancelled/no-show/active-link/expired-link/revoked missing
findings.designSays: 9 states: offered, reserved, confirmed, completed, cancelled, no-show, active-link, expired-link, revoked — each with distinct HeaderCard terminal chip, Banner, StatusTimeline position, footer CTAs, and ShareManage link state (design frames L135-271)
findings.implementationSays: All platforms collapse 9 states to 2. iOS VisitDetailViewModel.Lifecycle enum has only `.confirmed` and `.done` (VM L26-29); banner and footer switches handle only these two (VisitDetailView.swift L234-249, L306-317). Android VisitLifecycle enum has only `Confirmed` and `Done` (VM L33); VisitLoaded renders only two branches (VisitDetailScreen.kt L229-252). Web uses the `isPast` boolean, a past/not-past binary (VisitDetail.tsx L153-156). All three code files document this explicitly as a deliberate v1 scope deferral — the offer/reserve/link lifecycle has no v1 backend.
findings.evidence: Design: visit-detail-frames.jsx:135-271 (9 separate state functions); iOS: VisitDetailViewModel.swift:26-29, VisitDetailView.swift:234, 306; Android: VisitDetailViewModel.kt:33, VisitDetailScreen.kt:229; Web: VisitDetail.tsx:153-156

findings.title: Banner missing for reserved/cancelled/no-show/expired-link/revoked states on all platforms
findings.designSays: Banners shown in: reserved (amber, 'Visitor picked a time', L156), cancelled (error, 'This visit was cancelled', L203), no-show (warning, 'Marked no-show', L218), expired-link (amber, 'Booking link expired', L249), revoked (error, 'Booking link revoked', L264). Confirmed shows home-tone banner (L174). Done has no banner (L183-194).
findings.implementationSays: iOS: banner switch at VisitDetailView.swift L233-249 handles only `.confirmed` (home-tone banner) and `.done` (EmptyView — correct per design). Android: `LifecycleBanner` is only called inside `if (loaded.lifecycle == VisitLifecycle.Confirmed)` at VisitDetailScreen.kt L211-213; the `Done` branch inside `LifecycleBanner` (L361-368) is effectively dead code since `LifecycleBanner` is never called with `Done`. Web: `!isPast` guard at VisitDetail.tsx L288 emits the confirmed banner only. All 5 other banner states are absent because those lifecycle states are not modeled.
findings.evidence: Design: visit-detail-frames.jsx:156, 174, 203, 218, 249, 264; iOS: VisitDetailView.swift:233-249; Android: VisitDetailScreen.kt:211-213, 350-387; Web: VisitDetail.tsx:288-292

findings.title: Terminal chip hardcoded to 'Completed'/success-green; cancelled (error-red) and no-show (warning) chips not modeled
findings.designSays: Terminal chip varies by state: Completed (successBg/#047857, icon:check, L187), Cancelled (errorBg/#b91c1c, icon:x, L202), No-show (warningBg/warning700, icon:user-x, L217)
findings.implementationSays: iOS: VisitDetailViewModel.terminalChip property (VM L42-44) returns only `('Completed', .check)` for the `.done` state; VisitDetailView.swift terminalChip() render (L157-168) always uses success colors. Android: VisitDetailScreen.kt L312-318 hardcodes `PantopusIcon.Check, 'Completed', PantopusColors.success, PantopusColors.successBg` for the Done case only. Web: VisitDetail.tsx renders no terminal chip at all — the isPast binary produces no chip element. Cancelled and No-show terminal chips are unimplemented on all platforms because those lifecycle states are not modeled.
findings.evidence: Design: visit-detail-frames.jsx:187, 202, 217; iOS: VisitDetailViewModel.swift:42-44, VisitDetailView.swift:157-168; Android: VisitDetailScreen.kt:312-318; Web: VisitDetail.tsx (no chip element present)

findings.designSays: Avatar background is `CATV = 'linear-gradient(135deg, #2dd4bf, #0d9488)'` (teal-400 → teal-600), distinct from the Home green pillar accent — design frame L57, L43

findings.title: Web loading skeleton has 2 shimmer cards vs iOS/Android 3 shimmer rows
findings.designSays: No explicit loading skeleton specified in design frame; convention is to mirror loaded geometry
findings.implementationSays: Web VisitDetail.tsx L213-229 renders 2 shimmer cards (one header card and one timeline card). iOS VisitDetailView.swift L341-345 renders 3 discrete shimmer rows. Android VisitDetailScreen.kt L137 uses `SchedulingLoadingSkeleton(rows=3)`. Web under-represents the loaded content geometry.
findings.evidence: iOS: VisitDetailView.swift:341-345; Android: VisitDetailScreen.kt:137; Web: VisitDetail.tsx:213-229

screens.crossPlatformNotes: iOS and Android are closely aligned on core structure (identical 2-state lifecycle model, teal gradient avatar, teal type chip, confirmed-only banner and Edit button). Both document the 7 missing lifecycle states as deliberate v1 deferrals. Web diverges in two additional ways beyond the shared lifecycle collapse: (1) avatar gradient replaced by flat home-green fill, and (2) type chip uses home-green tokens instead of teal. iOS is closest to design for the confirmed/done states it implements. The blocker (ShareManage absent) and the lifecycle collapse affect all three platforms equally.
screens.verifierSummary:
CONFIRMED 7 of 9 draft findings; DROPPED 1; UPGRADED 1 finding's platform scope.

CONFIRMED (all survive): Findings 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 9 were verified by reading the actual source files.

Finding 1 (ShareManage absent) — confirmed across all three platforms. Design frames L84-118, L143, L159, L235, L251, L266. No counterpart in any platform. Web explicitly documents the omission at line 7.

Finding 2 (Edit button only for confirmed) — confirmed. Both VMs expose only a 2-value Lifecycle enum (confirmed/done). The finding correctly identifies the guard conditions at iOS L38-44 and Android L117-130. Survives as-is.

Finding 3 (only 2 of 9 states) — confirmed. All three platform files explicitly document the 7 missing states as intentional v1 scope deferrals (iOS VM L9, Android VM L74, Web L7). This is a real design gap regardless of intent.

Finding 4 (banner missing for 5 states) — confirmed with one correction to the Android evidence. The auditor cited Android L351-387 as where banners are handled; actually `LifecycleBanner` is defined there and does handle both Confirmed and Done — but the outer `if` at L211-213 means it is only ever called for Confirmed. The Done branch in `LifecycleBanner` is dead code. Finding survives with corrected evidence.

Finding 5 (terminal chip hardcoded) — confirmed and EXPANDED to include web. iOS VM terminalChip property (VM L42-44) returns only ("Completed", .check). Android L312-318 hardcodes success colors. Web renders no terminal chip at all. Platforms updated to include web.

Finding 6 (web avatar flat home-green) — confirmed. `bg-app-home` resolves to `#16A34A` (green) via globals.css L62. Design specifies teal gradient. iOS and Android correctly use teal gradient via categoryUnboxing tokens.

Finding 7 (web type chip home-green) — confirmed. `bg-app-home-bg` = `#DCFCE7`, `text-app-home` = `#16A34A` (green). Design specifies `#ccfbf1`/`#0f766e` (teal).

Finding 8 (ShareManage overline sub-point) — DROPPED. It is explicitly a sub-point of Finding 1 with no independent content. Keeping it would double-count the same missing section.

Finding 9 (web loading skeleton) — confirmed as nit/low-confidence. iOS has 3 shimmer rows, Android uses SchedulingLoadingSkeleton(rows=3), Web has 2 shimmer cards. Survives at nit severity.

NO NEW FINDINGS were discovered. The auditor's coverage of the files was accurate.

findings.title: Web page title is 'Scheduler' instead of 'Calendar'
findings.designSays: GatedTopBar renders a left-aligned title 'Calendar' (gated-scheduler-frames.jsx:14: `fontSize:15.5, fontWeight:600` text 'Calendar')
findings.implementationSays: The F15 route at /homes/[id]/scheduling/page.tsx renders an h1 with text 'Scheduler' (scheduling/page.tsx:46) beneath a 'Household' superlabel. The PermissionGate component (which renders HomeAgenda with canEdit=false for the gated read-only state) does not override or replace this page-level title. The separate /homes/[id]/calendar/page.tsx does show 'Calendar' but is a different route used for the full-access view, not the F15 gated path.
findings.evidence: design: gated-scheduler-frames.jsx:14. Web: frontend/apps/web/src/app/(app)/app/homes/[id]/scheduling/page.tsx:46. iOS correct: GatedSchedulerView.swift:24 (.navigationTitle("Calendar")). Android correct: PermissionGatedSchedulerScreen.kt:216 (Text("Calendar")).

findings.title: Web access action pill is inside the hint bar instead of in the top bar
findings.designSays: GatedTopBar (gated-scheduler-frames.jsx:11-21) is a single flex row with 'Calendar' title on the left and either 'Ask to manage' button or 'Request sent' pill as a trailing element on the right. HintBar (gated-scheduler-frames.jsx:24-31) is a separate component that contains only an eye icon and hint text — no access affordance.
findings.implementationSays: HomeAgenda.tsx (lines 214-233) places the 'Ask to manage' / 'Request sent' pill inside the same div as the eye icon and hint text — there is no separate top bar with the pill. The page chrome (scheduling/page.tsx:34-48) has only a back arrow and 'Scheduler' h1 with no access affordance at all.
findings.evidence: design: gated-scheduler-frames.jsx:11-31. Web: frontend/apps/web/src/components/scheduling/home/HomeAgenda.tsx:214-233. iOS correct: GatedSchedulerView.swift:27-31 (ToolbarItem(.topBarTrailing) hosts accessAction, HintBar is a separate view with eye icon only). Android correct: PermissionGatedSchedulerScreen.kt:200-232 (TopBar composable with trailing pill, HintBar composable with only eye icon).

findings.title: Android 'Ask to manage' pill uses ShieldCheck icon instead of ShieldPlus
findings.designSays: gated-scheduler-frames.jsx:18 specifies `data-lucide="shield-plus"` for the Ask to manage pill button icon.
findings.implementationSays: PermissionGatedSchedulerScreen.kt:249 uses `PantopusIcon.ShieldCheck` in AskToManagePill. iOS correctly uses `.shieldPlus` (GatedSchedulerView.swift:57).
findings.evidence: design: gated-scheduler-frames.jsx:18. Android: frontend/apps/android/app/src/main/java/app/pantopus/android/ui/screens/scheduling/home/PermissionGatedSchedulerScreen.kt:249. iOS: frontend/apps/ios/Pantopus/Features/Scheduling/Home/GatedScheduler/GatedSchedulerView.swift:57.

screens.crossPlatformNotes: iOS is closest to design: it correctly places the 'Calendar' title via navigationTitle and the access action pill as a trailing ToolbarItem in the top bar, with HintBar as a separate component below. Android also correctly separates GatedTopBar (with 'Calendar' title + trailing pill) from HintBar, matching the design structure — the only deviation is a minor icon mismatch (ShieldCheck vs ShieldPlus). Web diverges most: the F15 gated path lives in scheduling/page.tsx which shows 'Scheduler' not 'Calendar', and HomeAgenda collapses the access pill into the hint bar div rather than keeping it in a separate top bar element. Android's 'major' status (per the full 9-finding draft) reflects findings not among the two submitted for scrutiny; both submitted Android findings were not found to be issues.
screens.verifierSummary: Both submitted major/high-confidence findings were independently confirmed valid against the source. Finding 1 (web title 'Scheduler'): the F15 gated path is scheduling/page.tsx:46 which hard-codes 'Scheduler'; the separate calendar/page.tsx shows 'Calendar' but is a different non-gated route. Finding 2 (web pill placement): HomeAgenda.tsx:214-233 embeds both the eye icon, hint text, and the 'Ask to manage'/'Request sent' pill in a single div, while the design separates GatedTopBar (with trailing pill) from HintBar (eye + text only) — confirmed by contrasting iOS (ToolbarItem trailing) and Android (TopBar composable) which both implement the correct split. One new nit-level finding was added: Android's AskToManagePill uses PantopusIcon.ShieldCheck (PermissionGatedSchedulerScreen.kt:249) while design specifies shield-plus and iOS correctly uses .shieldPlus. No submitted findings were refuted or downgraded.

findings.title: Android: Strict rule member rows show grip-vertical reorder handle (design intent ambiguous but iOS/Web both omit it)
findings.designSays: roundrobin-frames.jsx SeatRow lines 125-127: trailing renders grip-vertical for any non-balanced mode via the ternary `mode==='balanced' ? WeightStepper : grip-vertical`. No design frame ever shows a Strict-selected state with multiple members, so whether grip-vertical should render for Strict is not explicitly specified. The design component's logic as written would produce grip-vertical for strict mode.
findings.implementationSays: RoundRobinSheet.kt lines 287-295: Priority and Strict are grouped in a Kotlin when-branch that both render GripVertical. iOS (RoundRobinAssignmentSheet.swift lines 175-176) returns EmptyView() for .strict. Web (RoundRobinSheet.tsx lines 282-287) renders null for strict (only priority gets GripVertical). Android alone shows a reorder handle for Strict, diverging from iOS and Web.
findings.evidence: roundrobin-frames.jsx:125-127 vs RoundRobinSheet.kt:287-295; RoundRobinAssignmentSheet.swift:175-176; RoundRobinSheet.tsx:282-287
