findings.designSays: roundrobin-frames.jsx line 127: grip-vertical icon implies drag-to-reorder for priority mode members
findings.implementationSays: RoundRobinAssignmentSheet.swift lines 168-174: gripVertical icon is shown but wrapped in a Menu with 'Move up'/'Move down' buttons rather than a drag gesture — functionally different but achieves the same ordering goal. Android and Web use the raw icon without a menu.
findings.evidence: roundrobin-frames.jsx:127 vs RoundRobinAssignmentSheet.swift:168-174; RoundRobinSheet.kt:290-294; RoundRobinSheet.tsx:283-286

screens.crossPlatformNotes: iOS and Web agree on omitting any trailing for Strict mode; Android diverges by showing GripVertical for both Priority and Strict. iOS deviates from Android/Web on Priority mode by wrapping the grip icon in a context Menu (Move up/Move down) rather than a direct drag gesture. Web is closest to design for the rule tile icons (uses the actual Lucide Scale and ListOrdered components from lucide-react). Android has two major icon mismatches (Gauge for Scale, FormatListBulleted for ListOrdered) because Icons.kt lacks Scale and ListOrdered entries entirely. iOS correctly uses .scale and .listOrdered PantopusIcon cases (verified at RoundRobinAssignmentSheet.swift lines 54-55), suggesting those cases exist in the iOS icon registry but not in Android's Icons.kt.
screens.verifierSummary: Finding 1 (Gauge vs Scale, Android) — CONFIRMED. roundrobin-frames.jsx:150 uses icon='scale'; Android uses PantopusIcon.Gauge which resolves to Icons.Filled.Speed (speedometer). No Scale entry exists in Icons.kt. Finding 2 (FormatListBulleted vs list-ordered, Android) — CONFIRMED. roundrobin-frames.jsx:151 uses icon='list-ordered'; Android uses PantopusIcon.List which resolves to Icons.AutoMirrored.Filled.FormatListBulleted (bullet list). No ListOrdered entry in Icons.kt. Finding 3 (Strict shows GripVertical, Android+Web) — PARTIALLY REFUTED. The design's SeatRow component (lines 125-127) uses a two-branch ternary: balanced → WeightStepper; anything else → grip-vertical. The design never draws a frame showing the Strict-mode+multiple-members trailing state explicitly, so Android's behavior of rendering GripVertical for Strict actually matches the literal SeatRow component logic. The Android half is downgraded to minor/medium-confidence cross-platform finding. Web correctly renders null for strict (diverging from the design ternary but matching iOS), so the auditor's claim that Android is wrong and Web is right is not supported by the design source; both are defensible interpretations. An additional minor cross-platform finding was added: iOS wraps the Priority-mode grip icon in a Menu (Move up/Move down) rather than a drag gesture, which all other platforms and the design do not show.

findings.title: No-overlap warning state (Frame 3) missing on iOS and Android
findings.designSays: Frame 3 (FrameNoOverlap in collective-frames.jsx:125-147) shows a `Note tone="warning" icon="alert-triangle"` inserted between the ModeTiles and the Members section when selected members share no free windows. Save button remains enabled.
findings.implementationSays: iOS VM explicitly documents 'Live no-overlap detection is deferred' (CollectiveEventSetupViewModel.swift:13-14); CollectiveEventSetupSheet.swift has no warning branch anywhere in readyBody. Android UiState.Content has no warning flag and CollectiveEventSetupScreen.kt:128-141 renders only BizNoteTone.Info notes, never BizNoteTone.Warning. Web correctly implements this: CollectiveSetup.tsx:266-269 computes `noOverlap` via `intersectFreeWindows` and CollectiveSetup.tsx:364-369 renders `<Note tone="warning" ...>` when it is true.
findings.evidence: collective-frames.jsx:133 (`Note tone="warning" icon="alert-triangle"`); iOS: CollectiveEventSetupViewModel.swift:13-14 (deferral comment), CollectiveEventSetupSheet.swift:50-76 (no warning branch); Android: CollectiveEventSetupViewModel.kt:59-74 (UiState.Content has no warning field), CollectiveEventSetupScreen.kt:128-141 (only Info-tone BizNote); Web correct: CollectiveSetup.tsx:266-269, 364-369

findings.title: Android and Web place sheet title/subhead in scrollable body, not in pinned header chrome
findings.designSays: SheetFrame (biz-kit.jsx:61-68) renders title and subhead in a flex-shrink:0 header div above the scrollable content area — they are pinned and never scroll away.
findings.implementationSays: Android: BizTopBar at CollectiveEventSetupScreen.kt:66 receives only `title`; the subhead is rendered as the first child `Text(style=PantopusTextStyle.caption)` inside the verticalScroll Column at CollectiveEventSetupScreen.kt:94-99, so it scrolls away with content. Web: CollectiveSetup.tsx:325-333 passes no `title` prop to BottomSheet; instead title and subhead are hardcoded as the first child `<div>` inside the scrollable content area (CollectiveSetup.tsx:334-344), so they also scroll. iOS correctly uses BizSheetHeader (CollectiveEventSetupSheet.swift:23) which is positioned outside and above the ScrollView.
findings.evidence: biz-kit.jsx:61-68 (pinned flexShrink:0 header); Android: CollectiveEventSetupScreen.kt:66 (BizTopBar title-only), CollectiveEventSetupScreen.kt:94-99 (scrollable subhead); Web: CollectiveSetup.tsx:325-344 (no title prop to BottomSheet, both in scrollable body); iOS correct: CollectiveEventSetupSheet.swift:22-34

findings.title: Web subhead copy appends dynamic event-type name not present in design
findings.designSays: All four SheetFrame instances in collective-frames.jsx (lines 84, 98, 127, 153) use the static subhead: "Every required member must be free at the same time."
findings.implementationSays: Web CollectiveSetup.tsx:339-342 renders: "Every required member must be free at the same time for {eventTypeName}." — appending the live service name. iOS and Android match the design's static copy exactly.
findings.evidence: collective-frames.jsx:84,98,127,153 (static subhead); CollectiveSetup.tsx:339-342 (appends `for {eventTypeName}`); CollectiveEventSetupSheet.swift:23 (static match); CollectiveEventSetupScreen.kt:95 (static match)

screens.crossPlatformNotes: iOS is closest to design: it correctly pins title+subhead in a BizSheetHeader outside the scroll area and the no-overlap deferral is the only missing state. Android and Web both fail to pin the title/subhead (scrollable), Android additionally lacks the no-overlap warning. Web uniquely implements the no-overlap detection (intersectFreeWindows) that iOS and Android both defer. The auditor's low-confidence info-Note color finding was refuted: all three platforms intentionally use business-violet for the info tone in the business pillar context; the design's biz-kit Note in this screen is likewise violet, and the CSS `--color-info-bg` sky-blue is a generic semantic token not targeted here. Android's BusinessKit.kt already provides a separate `BizNoteTone.InfoBlue` for sky-blue when needed.
screens.verifierSummary: Confirmed 2 of 3 draft findings, refuted 1, corrected 1, and added 1 new finding. (1) No-overlap warning missing on iOS+Android: CONFIRMED via direct code read — iOS VM comments 'deferred', no warning branch in sheet; Android UiState.Content has no warning field, screen has no Warning-tone BizNote. Web correctly implements this with intersectFreeWindows. (2) Android subhead in scrollable body: CONFIRMED and EXPANDED — web also places both title and subhead inside the scrollable content (passes no title prop to BottomSheet); finding was corrected to include web as a second failing platform. (3) Info Note color mismatch (low-confidence nit): REFUTED — all three platforms consistently use business-violet for info notes in the business pillar; the biz-kit design Note in this screen is likewise violet-tinted; the semantic info-blue token in colors_and_type.css is not referenced by this design screen at all. New finding added: web subhead copy appends 'for {eventTypeName}' making it diverge from the static design copy on all four frames.

findings.title: Android trailing top-bar icon uses UsersRound; iOS uses slidersHorizontal
findings.designSays: No trailing icon is specified in any of the five design frames — all five TopBar calls are `<TopBar title="Booking availability"/>` with no trailing slot (teamavail-frames.jsx:64, 97, 113, 136, 153). Neither icon choice is prescribed by design.
findings.implementationSays: Android uses PantopusIcon.UsersRound (TeamBookingAvailabilityScreen.kt:80); iOS uses Icon(.slidersHorizontal, size:19) (TeamBookingAvailabilityView.swift:73). The icons have different semantics: UsersRound reads as a 'people' icon; slidersHorizontal reads as 'settings/assignment'. The draft called this design-parity — refuted, because the design specifies no icon at all. This is purely a cross-platform divergence.
findings.evidence: Android: TeamBookingAvailabilityScreen.kt:79-84 `PantopusIconImage(icon = PantopusIcon.UsersRound, contentDescription = "Assign a service")`. iOS: TeamBookingAvailabilityView.swift:73 `Icon(.slidersHorizontal, size: 19, color: Theme.Color.appText)`. Design: teamavail-frames.jsx:64, 97, 113, 136, 153 — all five frames pass no trailing prop.

findings.title: Android (and iOS) loading skeleton missing chip shimmer row; Android also lacks avatar and toggle shimmers
findings.designSays: Frame 2 ShimRow (teamavail-frames.jsx:79-90) shows: 36px avatar circle, two text lines, a pill chip shimmer (84px wide, 16px tall, radius 9999) below the text, and a 46×28 toggle pill — all four elements mirror the loaded MemberRow geometry.
findings.implementationSays: Android SchedulingLoadingSkeleton(rows=4) (TeamBookingAvailabilityScreen.kt:91, SchedulingStateScaffolding.kt:107-120) renders only a card with two text-line shimmers per row — no avatar circle, no chip shimmer, no toggle shimmer. iOS BizShimmerRow(showTrailingPill:true) (BusinessKit.swift:394-415) includes avatar (34px circle) and toggle pill but also omits the chip shimmer row (Sk w={84} h={16} r={9999}). Web TeamSkeleton correctly includes avatar, two text lines, chip shimmer (`h-4 w-20 rounded-full`), and toggle pill (TeamAvailabilityGrid.tsx:406-414). Android is the bigger offender (three missing elements vs iOS's one).
findings.evidence: Design: teamavail-frames.jsx:81-88 ShimRow. Android: SchedulingStateScaffolding.kt:117-118 — only two Shimmer text lines. iOS: BusinessKit.swift:399-413 — avatar + 2 text lines + optional trailing pill but no chip line. Web: TeamAvailabilityGrid.tsx:408-415 — all four shimmer elements present.

findings.title: Android missing dedicated empty-roster state (falls through to empty Content)
findings.designSays: n/a — no design frame for zero-member roster. iOS and Web both handle it explicitly.
findings.implementationSays: Android UiState sealed interface has Loading/Content/BusinessOnly/Error with no Empty case (TeamBookingAvailabilityViewModel.kt:71-84). When members list is empty after load, Content(rows=emptyList()) is emitted and TeamContent renders a BizCard with no rows — no copy, no icon, no CTA. iOS has a dedicated .empty phase (TeamBookingAvailabilityViewModel.swift:48) set at line 159 (`phase = rows.isEmpty ? .empty : .loaded`) and renders EmptyState(icon: .usersRound, headline: 'No team members yet', ...) (TeamBookingAvailabilityView.swift:87-93). Web renders inline 'No team members yet' copy inside the card when roster.length === 0 (TeamAvailabilityGrid.tsx:309-319).
findings.evidence: Android: TeamBookingAvailabilityViewModel.kt:71-84 sealed interface — no Empty; load() at line 139 emits Content regardless of roster size. iOS: TeamBookingAvailabilityViewModel.swift:48 `case loading, loaded, empty, businessOnly, error(String)` and line 159 `phase = rows.isEmpty ? .empty : .loaded`. Web: TeamAvailabilityGrid.tsx:309 `roster.length === 0` branch.

findings.title: Web member chip shows 'Inactive seat' instead of design's 'Business hours'
findings.designSays: RosterRow (teamavail-frames.jsx:36-39) shows two chip states: `<Chip tone="biz" icon="user">Personal hours</Chip>` when bound=true, and `<Chip tone="neutral" icon="building-2">Business hours</Chip>` when bound=false. The non-personal chip always reads 'Business hours'.
findings.implementationSays: Web MemberRow (TeamAvailabilityGrid.tsx:102-110) shows 'Personal hours' when member.isActive, but 'Inactive seat' (tone neutral, icon Building2) when !member.isActive — substituting a seat-status label for the hours-source label. iOS and Android both use 'Business hours' for the non-personal case, matching the design.
findings.evidence: Design: teamavail-frames.jsx:38 `Business hours`. Web: TeamAvailabilityGrid.tsx:107-109 `<Chip tone="neutral" icon={Building2}>Inactive seat</Chip>`. iOS: TeamBookingAvailabilityView.swift:157-159 `BizChip(tone: .neutral, icon: .building2, text: "Business hours")`. Android: TeamBookingAvailabilityScreen.kt:204-205 `BizChip(text = "Business hours", tone = BizChipTone.Neutral)`.

screens.crossPlatformNotes: iOS is closest to design (4 minor issues: no chip shimmer in skeleton, no 'You' badge cross-platform parity note). Android has the most gaps: wrong trailing icon vs iOS, skeleton missing three geometric elements (avatar/chip/toggle), and no empty-roster state. Web matches geometry well but diverges on the inactive-member chip label ('Inactive seat' instead of 'Business hours') and adds an unspecified 'You' badge for the current user. All three platforms agree on member-row structure (avatar + name + summary + chip + toggle + chevron), explainer note, coverage strip, and gated lock note.
screens.verifierSummary: Finding 1 refuted as design-parity (the design specifies no trailing icon in any of its five frames) and reclassified as cross-platform/minor — the icon divergence between Android (UsersRound) and iOS (slidersHorizontal) is real but not a design spec violation. Severity downgraded from major to minor. Finding 2 confirmed with an addition: iOS BizShimmerRow also omits the chip shimmer row (design frame line 86), making Android the worse offender (3 missing elements) and iOS a minor one; the finding now covers both platforms. Finding 3 confirmed exactly as stated: Android has no Empty UiState case and falls through to empty Content. Two new findings added: (a) Web's 'Inactive seat' chip label deviates from the design-specified 'Business hours' (minor, design-parity); (b) the chip shimmer omission in iOS was captured within the revised Finding 2 rather than as a separate item. No findings were dropped.

findings.title: Web read-only (inherits-personal) body replaces design with a bespoke summary card — no dimmed week grid, no 'View personal' link
findings.designSays: FrameInherits: inherits-banner pill + dimmed (opacity:0.6) weekday grid with readonly range chips. A 'View personal' sky link button sits inline in the banner (memberhours-frames.jsx:173-178).
findings.implementationSays: Web read-only branch (MemberHoursEditor.tsx:407-444, the !isYou block) renders: inherits-banner (LinkIcon + paragraph only, no 'View personal' button) + AccentOverline 'Bookable days' + a Card with a freeWeekdaysLabel text summary + a footnote paragraph. No dimmed week grid whatsoever.
findings.evidence: memberhours-frames.jsx:169-181 (FrameInherits); MemberHoursEditor.tsx:407-444

findings.title: Web read-only inherits-banner is missing the 'View personal' sky link button
findings.designSays: FrameInherits banner contains a sky-colored 'View personal' button at the trailing edge (memberhours-frames.jsx:176: `<button style={{color:E.blue600}}>View personal</button>`).
findings.implementationSays: Web inherits-banner (MemberHoursEditor.tsx:412-421) has only LinkIcon + paragraph text. No 'View personal' button or link. iOS correctly implements this (MemberWorkingHoursSheet.swift:309-315); Android also implements it (MemberWorkingHoursScreen.kt:424-430).
findings.evidence: memberhours-frames.jsx:176; MemberHoursEditor.tsx:412-421; MemberWorkingHoursSheet.swift:309-315 (correct iOS reference)

findings.title: Android loading skeleton uses generic 5-row scaffolding instead of screen-specific 7-day shimmer geometry
findings.designSays: FrameLoading (memberhours-frames.jsx:200-216): a Card containing 7 rows (one per WEEK entry), each row having a short day-label shimmer (Sk w=30 h=11) + pill-width shimmer (Sk w='60%' h=22 r=9999) + 26×26 circle shimmer. Note: the design shows a live TzChip (not a shimmer) above the card — the original finding's claim of a 'TzChip placeholder shimmer' in the design frame is incorrect; iOS adds a TzChip shimmer on its own initiative.
findings.implementationSays: Android passes SchedulingLoadingSkeleton(rows=5) (MemberWorkingHoursScreen.kt:89), which renders 5 generic card blocks each with two plain text-line shimmers (SchedulingStateScaffolding.kt:94-122). Row count is 5 not 7, and per-row geometry (pill shimmer + circle button) is absent.
findings.evidence: memberhours-frames.jsx:200-216; MemberWorkingHoursScreen.kt:89; SchedulingStateScaffolding.kt:94-122; MemberWorkingHoursSheet.swift:324-345 (iOS correct reference)

screens.crossPlatformNotes: iOS is closest to design: it correctly implements the inherits-banner with 'View personal' button, the dimmed (opacity:0.6) week grid in read-only mode, and a screen-specific 7-row loading skeleton with correct per-row shimmer geometry. Android matches the read-only layout correctly (inherits-banner + 'View personal' + dimmed WeekGrid) but diverges on the loading skeleton (5 generic rows vs 7 day-specific rows). Web is the furthest from design: the entire read-only branch is replaced with a bespoke summary card that omits both the dimmed week grid and the 'View personal' link button.
screens.verifierSummary: Findings 1 and 2 (web read-only layout and missing 'View personal' button) are independently confirmed by direct inspection of MemberHoursEditor.tsx:407-444. The !isYou branch renders no dimmed week grid, no opacity modifier, and no 'View personal' button — all three are present in FrameInherits (memberhours-frames.jsx:169-181). iOS implements both correctly (MemberWorkingHoursSheet.swift:285-320). Finding 3 (Android loading skeleton) is confirmed for the row-count mismatch (5 vs 7) and wrong per-row shimmer geometry, but the auditor's claim that the design specifies a 'TzChip placeholder shimmer' is wrong: FrameLoading (lines 200-216) renders the live TzChip component inside Header, not a shimmer of it. The finding is corrected and downgraded from major to minor because it affects only the transient loading state, not functional content. No additional cross-platform discrepancies were found while verifying: Android and iOS both correctly implement the InheritsNote/inheritsLinkRow with the 'View personal' button, whereas web omits it entirely.

findings.title: Web adds 'Team' and 'Assignment' sections absent from design
findings.designSays: Five frames in bizsettings-frames.jsx (lines 106–207) each contain exactly: Confirmation · Scheduling · Policy · Notifications · Payments. No Team or Assignment section appears in any frame.
findings.implementationSays: BusinessSettings.tsx lines 322–341 render a Group titled 'Team' with 'Team booking availability' and 'Members & seats' rows, followed by <AssignmentSection> (line 341), both placed before Confirmation. iOS and Android have neither section.
findings.evidence: bizsettings-frames.jsx:106–207 (FrameSaved/Auto/Payments/Loading/Gated — no Team or Assignment); BusinessSettings.tsx:322–341

findings.title: Web renders Scheduling rows as expand-in-place accordions instead of chevron-navigate rows
findings.designSays: bizsettings-frames.jsx lines 55–64 (Scheduling component): all four rows — Minimum notice, Booking horizon, Buffers, Time zone — are SRow elements with a trailing <Chevron> indicating navigation to a sub-screen. No inline editor opens.
findings.implementationSays: BusinessSettings.tsx lines 399–494: Minimum notice, Booking horizon, and Buffers are ExpandRow components that reveal inline Stepper/chip editors on tap. Only Time zone (lines 495–511) remains a plain navigate-row. iOS (BusinessSchedulingSettingsView.swift:129–162) and Android (BusinessSchedulingSettingsScreen.kt:132–162) both use plain navigate rows matching the design.
findings.evidence: bizsettings-frames.jsx:55–64; BusinessSettings.tsx:399–494 (three ExpandRow + one SettingRow); BusinessSchedulingSettingsView.swift:129–162 (four BizSettingsRow); BusinessSchedulingSettingsScreen.kt:132–162 (four SettingsRow with onNavigate)

findings.title: Web top bar replaced by inline page Header — no back button, no BizTopBar chrome
findings.designSays: bizsettings-frames.jsx line 109 (all 5 frames): <TopBar title="Booking"/> — standard BizTopBar with chevron-back, centered title 'Booking', and a hairline bottom rule.
findings.implementationSays: BusinessSettings.tsx Header component (lines 633–657) renders an uppercase 'Business' accent label, 'Booking settings' H1, and a BusinessSwitcher. No back button, no centered title, no hairline. iOS (BusinessSchedulingSettingsView.swift:27) and Android (BusinessSchedulingSettingsScreen.kt:63) both implement BizTopBar with chevron-back and hairline, matching the design.
findings.evidence: bizsettings-frames.jsx:109; BusinessSettings.tsx:633–657; BusinessSchedulingSettingsView.swift:27, 270–302; BusinessSchedulingSettingsScreen.kt:63

findings.title: Web: Payments section hidden behind schedulingPaid feature flag — off in production
findings.designSays: Payments Group is present unconditionally in FrameSaved (line 116), FrameAuto (line 166), and FramePayments (line 182). No feature-flag gate on the Payments section.
findings.implementationSays: BusinessSettings.tsx line 141: paid = webFeatureFlags.schedulingPaid; line 567: {paid && <section>Payments</section>}. featureFlags.ts lines 33–34 show schedulingPaid defaults ON in non-prod but OFF in production (NEXT_PUBLIC_SCHEDULING_PAID_ENABLED unset). iOS shows Payments unless isGated (no separate paid flag). Android derives showPayments from flags.paidSchedulingEnabled || connected || hasPaid — a different but less restrictive gate.
findings.evidence: bizsettings-frames.jsx:116, 166, 182; BusinessSettings.tsx:141, 567; frontend/apps/web/src/lib/featureFlags.ts:29–43; BusinessSchedulingSettingsView.swift:72; BusinessSchedulingSettingsViewModel.kt:129

findings.title: Web: Permission-gated state (Frame 5) entirely absent — no dimming, no lock note, no admin guard
findings.designSays: bizsettings-frames.jsx lines 190–207 (FrameGated): Confirmation segmented control dims to 0.55 opacity, Scheduling and Notifications dim to 0.7, Policy and Payments are hidden, and a lock note 'Only admins can change booking settings.' with a lock icon appears at the bottom.
findings.implementationSays: BusinessSettings.tsx (lines 308–631) has no gated concept — no opacity modifier, no admin permission check, no lock note. Any non-owner team member sees the fully interactive settings. iOS (BusinessSchedulingSettingsView.swift:70–73, 99–141, 176, 213–223) and Android (BusinessSchedulingSettingsScreen.kt:108–214) both fully implement the gated state with dimming, chevron suppression, Policy/Payments hiding, and BizLockNote.
findings.evidence: bizsettings-frames.jsx:190–207; BusinessSettings.tsx:308–631 (no gated branch anywhere); BusinessSchedulingSettingsView.swift:70–73, 99, 102, 141, 176, 213–223; BusinessSchedulingSettingsScreen.kt:108, 165, 177, 213–215

findings.title: iOS and Android: Scheduling sub-rows all navigate to a single schedulingDefaultsRoute rather than per-row sub-screens
findings.designSays: bizsettings-frames.jsx lines 55–64: each of the four Scheduling rows has an independent Chevron implying navigation to a dedicated sub-screen per row (Minimum notice / Booking horizon / Buffers / Time zone each navigate separately).
findings.implementationSays: iOS BusinessSchedulingSettingsView.swift lines 129–139: all three non-timezone rows call model.openSchedulingDefaults() — a single route. Android BusinessSchedulingSettingsScreen.kt lines 140, 149, 158: all three call onNavigate(viewModel.schedulingDefaultsRoute()). Web diverges in a different direction (inline accordions). All three platforms fail to provide per-row sub-screen navigation.
findings.evidence: bizsettings-frames.jsx:55–64; BusinessSchedulingSettingsView.swift:129–139; BusinessSchedulingSettingsScreen.kt:132–162

screens.crossPlatformNotes: iOS and Android are closely aligned with each other and with the design on structure, gated state, top-bar chrome, and section layout. Both lack Team/Assignment (matching the design). Web diverges on 5 major points: extra Team+Assignment sections, expand-in-place accordions for Scheduling rows, no BizTopBar, production-gated Payments section, and entirely missing permission-gated state. iOS is the closest platform to the design; Android is nearly identical to iOS. The one shared native gap (Scheduling rows all routing to a single defaults screen rather than per-row sub-screens) is minor and cross-platform.
screens.verifierSummary: All five draft findings (blocker/major, high-confidence) were independently confirmed by reading bizsettings-frames.jsx alongside BusinessSettings.tsx, BusinessSchedulingSettingsView.swift, and BusinessSchedulingSettingsScreen.kt. No finding was refuted or downgraded. Finding 4 (Payments flag) was verified against featureFlags.ts to confirm the flag defaults OFF in production, making the major severity appropriate. Finding 5 (gated state) was verified by confirming both iOS (isGated pattern with opacity + gatedNote) and Android (BizLockNote, dim modifier) fully implement Frame 5 while web has zero gated logic. One new finding was added: all three platforms (including web's navigate-row for Time Zone) route all three non-timezone Scheduling rows to a single schedulingDefaultsRoute instead of per-row sub-screens — minor cross-platform issue consistent across native platforms.

findings.title: Web: not_applicable state renders a compact InlineNote rather than a full not-applicable scaffold
findings.designSays: n/a — no design frame for not-applicable; iOS and Android provide a full-screen icon + headline + body explanation.
findings.implementationSays: Web (SchedulingConnectPanel.tsx lines 239-245) renders a single <InlineNote tone='info'> chip with no navigation chrome and no icon, while iOS (PaymentsKit.swift PaymentsNotApplicableView lines 128-157) and Android (PaymentsSetupScreen.kt PaymentsNotApplicable composable lines 570-592, called with Modifier.fillMaxSize() at line 125) both render a full centered scaffold with a circle icon, bold headline, and explanatory body text plus a top bar.
findings.evidence: Web: SchedulingConnectPanel.tsx lines 239-245; iOS: PaymentsKit.swift lines 128-157; Android: PaymentsSetupScreen.kt lines 125, 570-592

screens.crossPlatformNotes: The design file (paymentssetup-frames.jsx) contains exactly 5 frames — not-connected, incomplete, ready, restricted, returned-from-Stripe — and has no not-applicable frame at all. The not-applicable divergence is therefore purely a cross-platform consistency issue. iOS and Android are aligned with each other (full-screen centered scaffold with icon + headline + body + top bar); web is the outlier (single-line InlineNote chip, no icon, no top bar chrome). iOS is closest to the design frames for all five specified states. Android has minor token and size-class differences (e.g., icon circle is 56dp vs iOS's 72dp in the not-applicable view) but matches the design intent across all five design-specified frames.
screens.verifierSummary: CONFIRMED the sole blocker/major/low-confidence finding. Independently verified all three cited files: (1) Web SchedulingConnectPanel.tsx lines 239-245 unambiguously renders an InlineNote chip and nothing else for the not_applicable branch. (2) iOS PaymentsKit.swift lines 128-157 implements PaymentsNotApplicableView as a full VStack scaffold with BizTopBar, a 72dp circle icon, an h3 headline, and a body paragraph — matching the description exactly. (3) Android PaymentsSetupScreen.kt line 125 passes Modifier.fillMaxSize() to PaymentsNotApplicable; that composable (lines 570-592) is a centered Column with a 56dp circle icon, bold headline text, and secondary body text. Evidence and line numbers in the draft finding are accurate. Severity major is upheld: the web not-applicable state provides no navigation chrome and no explanatory content hierarchy, making it meaningfully worse than the native treatment. The finding's cross-platform scope label is correct because the design itself does not specify this state. No additional discrepancies were encountered during verification that rise to the level of a new finding.

findings.title: Web hero uses violet (business) background instead of sky blue gradient
findings.designSays: Hero card uses a sky gradient: linear-gradient(155deg, #075985 0%, #0369a1 55%, #0284c7 100%). Design comment on line 6 explicitly states 'Wallet chrome stays neutral; only the booking source/category go violet.' (wallet-earnings-frames.jsx:14,29)
findings.implementationSays: Web Hero component (PayoutsEarnings.tsx:113) applies className 'bg-app-business', which resolves via tailwind.config.js:59 to var(--color-identity-business) = #7C3AED (violet). iOS correctly uses BalanceHero with primary800→primary600 sky gradient (BalanceHero.swift:126-130). Android also uses the shared BalanceHero component with the correct sky gradient.
findings.evidence: wallet-earnings-frames.jsx:6 (explicit comment), :14 (W.p800='#075985'), :29 (gradient definition); PayoutsEarnings.tsx:113 ('bg-app-business'); tailwind.config.js:59 ('app-business': 'var(--color-identity-business)'); globals.css:64 ('--color-identity-business: #7C3AED'); BalanceHero.swift:125-130 (primary800→primary600 gradient)

findings.title: Web withdraw CTA is a handoff link instead of an inline Withdraw button
findings.designSays: Sticky bottom bar contains a full-width Withdraw button (arrow-down-to-line icon, amount right-aligned, sky W.p600 background) that initiates withdrawal directly (wallet-earnings-frames.jsx:166-173, rendered at :208)
findings.implementationSays: Web renders a <Link href='/app/wallet'> styled as a button, labelled 'Manage & withdraw in Wallet' (PayoutsEarnings.tsx:477-485), routing users away from the screen. The file header comment (line 8) confirms this is intentional: 'a manage & withdraw in Wallet handoff. Payout SETTLEMENT is deferred server-side.' iOS (PayoutsEarningsView.swift:498-528) and Android (PayoutsEarningsScreen.kt:602-626) both implement a real WithdrawButton that calls model.withdraw() / onWithdraw() directly.
findings.evidence: wallet-earnings-frames.jsx:166-173 (Withdraw button component), :208 (rendered in FramePopulated); PayoutsEarnings.tsx:476-486 (Link href='/app/wallet', label 'Manage & withdraw in Wallet'); PayoutsEarningsView.swift:498-528 (PayoutsWithdrawButton with model.withdraw()); PayoutsEarningsScreen.kt:602-626 (WithdrawButton calling onWithdraw)

findings.title: Web is missing the Tax Documents row
findings.designSays: Frame 1 (populated) includes a TaxRow component below the Payout method section, showing a file-text icon, 'Tax documents' title, '1099-NEC for 2025 ready' or 'YTD $4,180 · docs mid-Jan' subtitle, and a chevron-right (wallet-earnings-frames.jsx:149-159, rendered at :205)
findings.implementationSays: PayoutsEarnings.tsx contains no Tax Documents row. No mention of TaxDoc, 1099, or file-text icon anywhere in the file. iOS (PayoutsEarningsView.swift:114-120) renders TaxDocsRow conditionally when payoutState == .enabled && !isEmpty. Android (PayoutsEarningsScreen.kt:232-238) also renders TaxDocsRow under the same condition. Both mobile platforms implement this; web does not.
findings.evidence: wallet-earnings-frames.jsx:149-159 (TaxRow component definition), :205 (rendered in FramePopulated); PayoutsEarnings.tsx: no TaxRow, no 1099, no file-text import; PayoutsEarningsView.swift:114-120 (TaxDocsRow rendered); PayoutsEarningsScreen.kt:232-238 (TaxDocsRow rendered)

findings.title: Web pending split-cell label reads 'Processing' instead of 'Pending'
findings.designSays: The pending sub-cell inside the hero card uses the overline label 'Pending' (wallet-earnings-frames.jsx:47)
findings.implementationSays: Web Hero (PayoutsEarnings.tsx:159) renders the overline as 'Processing'. iOS uses 'Pending' (PayoutsEarningsView.swift:136). Android also uses 'Pending' (PayoutsEarningsScreen.kt:195).
findings.evidence: wallet-earnings-frames.jsx:47 ('Pending'); PayoutsEarnings.tsx:159 ('Processing'); PayoutsEarningsView.swift:136 ('Pending'); PayoutsEarningsScreen.kt:195 ('Pending')

screens.crossPlatformNotes: iOS and Android are closely aligned with each other: both use the shared BalanceHero component (sky-blue primary600/800 gradient), both implement a real Withdraw button with an inline withdrawal flow, and both render TaxDocsRow conditionally. Web diverges on all three points — violet hero background, handoff link instead of Withdraw button, and absent Tax Documents row. iOS is the closest to design among the three platforms. The 'Processing' vs 'Pending' label divergence affects web only; iOS and Android match the design copy.
screens.verifierSummary: All three draft major findings were independently confirmed against the source files and design frame. Finding 1 (violet hero on web): confirmed — bg-app-business resolves to #7C3AED via tailwind.config.js:59 and globals.css:64, while the design specifies a sky-blue linear-gradient and the comment at wallet-earnings-frames.jsx:6 explicitly states 'Wallet chrome stays neutral'. iOS and Android both use the sky-blue BalanceHero component. Finding 2 (handoff link vs Withdraw button on web): confirmed — web renders <Link href='/app/wallet'> (PayoutsEarnings.tsx:477); iOS (PayoutsEarningsView.swift:498) and Android (PayoutsEarningsScreen.kt:602) both implement a real WithdrawButton with an inline confirm flow. The web file header acknowledges this is an intentional architectural decision but it remains a design-parity gap. Finding 3 (missing Tax Documents row on web): confirmed — PayoutsEarnings.tsx has no TaxRow or any 1099/file-text reference, while iOS (PayoutsEarningsView.swift:114) and Android (PayoutsEarningsScreen.kt:232) both render TaxDocsRow. One new minor finding was added: web's pending split-cell label reads 'Processing' instead of the design's 'Pending' (wallet-earnings-frames.jsx:47 vs PayoutsEarnings.tsx:159). Per-platform status is unchanged from the draft: iOS minor, Android minor, web major.

findings.title: Android uses `Package` icon instead of `Layers` for package rows, empty state, and payouts gate
findings.designSays: packageslist-frames.jsx line 24: `<i data-lucide="layers"/>` in PkgRow; line 73: EmptyHero `icon="layers"`; line 89: PayoutsGate EmptyHero `icon="layers"`. All three contexts use the `layers` Lucide icon.
findings.implementationSays: PackagesListScreen.kt line 262: `PantopusIcon.Package` in PackageRow icon box; line 389: `PantopusIcon.Package` in EmptyActive; line 430: `PantopusIcon.Package` in PayoutsGate hero icon. iOS correctly uses `Icon(.layers, …)` in all three locations (SchedulingPackagesListView.swift lines 242, 124, 149).
findings.evidence: packageslist-frames.jsx:24,73,89 vs PackagesListScreen.kt:262,389,430; confirmed against SchedulingPackagesListView.swift:124,149,242

findings.title: Web filter/segmented control hidden during empty state; design always shows it
findings.designSays: packageslist-frames.jsx Frame 2 (Empty, line 72) and Frame 3 (Gate, line 88) both render `<FilterSeg value="Active"/>` above the empty hero — the segmented control is always present regardless of list content.
findings.implementationSays: PackageList.tsx line 185: the segmented control is conditionally rendered only when `phase === "ready" && packages.length > 0`. When packages array is empty the filter disappears entirely, leaving the header with just the title and description.
findings.evidence: packageslist-frames.jsx:72,88 (FilterSeg always present) vs PackageList.tsx:185-208 (condition `packages.length > 0`)

findings.title: Web uses individual per-row cards instead of a single grouped card with dividers
findings.designSays: packageslist-frames.jsx line 59: `<Card>{PKGS.map((p, i) => <PkgRow … last={i===PKGS.length-1}/>)}</Card>` — rows share a single card container; dividers are rendered via `borderBottom` on each row except the last.
findings.implementationSays: PackageList.tsx line 332: each `PackageRow` is an independent `div` with `rounded-[14px] border border-app-border bg-app-surface p-3 shadow-sm` and a `gap-3` flex column wrapping them. No shared outer card; no row dividers — each row is its own card with full shadow and border.
findings.evidence: packageslist-frames.jsx:59 (single Card wrapper) vs PackageList.tsx:330-335 (independent per-row bordered div)

findings.title: Web renders extra payouts-gate state: banner above existing rows when packages exist but payouts not connected
findings.designSays: packageslist-frames.jsx Frame 3 is exclusively an empty+gate state — payouts gate is shown as the EmptyHero action when there are no packages. There is no design frame showing a populated list simultaneously with a gate banner.
findings.implementationSays: PackageList.tsx lines 254-263: when `packages.length > 0` AND `needsPayouts` AND `filter === 'active'`, a `StripeGate` banner is rendered above the row list in addition to all existing rows. This combined state (rows + gate banner) is not in the design. The empty+gate state on lines 226-251 does correctly match design Frame 3.
findings.evidence: packageslist-frames.jsx:83-103 (Frame 3 gate replaces list entirely) vs PackageList.tsx:254-263 (gate rendered above rows when packages exist)

findings.title: Android segmented control outer radius uses Radii.md (8dp); design specifies 9dp, iOS matches exactly
findings.designSays: packageslist-frames.jsx line 15: `borderRadius:9` on the outer segmented wrapper div.
findings.implementationSays: PackagesKit.kt line 405: `RoundedCornerShape(Radii.md)` where `Radii.md = 8.dp` (1dp under spec). iOS PackagesKit.swift line 264: `clipShape(RoundedRectangle(cornerRadius: 9, style: .continuous))` — literal 9 matching design exactly.
findings.evidence: packageslist-frames.jsx:15 vs PackagesKit.kt:401-406 + Radii.kt (md=8dp) vs PackagesKit.swift:264

screens.crossPlatformNotes: iOS is closest to design: correctly uses `.layers` icon in all three contexts (row, empty, gate), renders a single grouped PkgRowCard with HorizontalDivider between rows, and always shows the filter segmented control. Android diverges on iconography (Package vs Layers in all three locations) and has a 1dp radius nit on the segmented control outer wrapper. Web diverges on card grouping idiom (per-row cards vs single grouped card), hides the segmented filter during empty states, and adds an undocumented populated-list + gate-banner combined state. All three platforms agree on the payouts-gate copy, the status chip design, and the Restore button for archived rows.
screens.verifierSummary: All five scrutinized findings survived verification. Finding 1 (Android icon): independently confirmed — PackagesListScreen.kt lines 262, 389, 430 all use PantopusIcon.Package; design at lines 24, 73, 89 specifies `layers`; iOS at lines 124, 149, 242 correctly uses `.layers`. Finding 2 (Web filter hidden): confirmed — PackageList.tsx line 185 gates on `packages.length > 0`; design Frames 2 and 3 render FilterSeg unconditionally. Finding 3 (Web per-row cards): confirmed — each PackageRow is an independent bordered div; design wraps all rows in a single Card with borderBottom dividers. Finding 4 (Web extra gate state): confirmed with medium confidence — the populated-list + gate-banner combination at lines 254-263 has no design counterpart; kept as major because it represents an undocumented UX state. Finding 5 (Android Radii.md nit): confirmed — Radii.md = 8dp vs design borderRadius:9; iOS uses literal cornerRadius:9. Auditor line references were accurate throughout; no findings were refuted or downgraded. No additional clear discrepancies observed beyond those already in the full 13-finding draft.

findings.title: Web: Description field missing from Details card
findings.designSays: DetailsCard renders two fields: Name and TextInput label='Description' multiline — createpackage-frames.jsx:72-78
findings.implementationSays: Web Details card (Card overline='Details') contains only a Name TextField. The file-level comment explicitly notes description 'has no backing field, so omitted rather than faked' — PackageEditor.tsx:9-12. iOS and Android both render Description as a view-only multiline field.

Web: PackageEditor.tsx:207-215 — only TextField label='Name'. iOS: PackageEditorView.swift:99-103 (PkgMultilineField label='Description' rendered). Android: PackageEditorScreen.kt:194-199 (PkgMultilineField label='Description' rendered). iOS/Android VMs document field as view-only (not sent on save): PackageEditorViewModel.swift:50-54, PackageEditorViewModel.kt:90-93.

findings.title: Web: Expiry card entirely absent
findings.designSays: ExpiryCard renders a Segmented control with options '90 days / 1 year / Never' — createpackage-frames.jsx:81-87; card appears in all four frames

Web: PackageEditor.tsx:201-305 — no Expiry card present. iOS: PackageEditorView.swift:154-165 (expiryCard with PkgSegmented, view-only). findings.title: Web: 'has active buyers' locked state (Frame 4) not implemented
findings.designSays: Frame 4 (FrameActiveBuyers): a Note tone='warning' icon='lock' ('12 people own credits — you can't change sessions or eligibility while credits are active') appears above RedeemCard; RedeemCard and SessionsCard receive locked prop (opacity 0.6, disabled) — createpackage-frames.jsx:154-172

Web: PackageEditor.tsx:66 (phase type has no locked variant); lines 201-305 (ready render has no conditional for locked state). findings.title: Android: 'has active buyers' locked state (Frame 4) not implemented
findings.designSays: Frame 4 shows locked editing state with warning Note and disabled tiles/stepper when active buyers exist — createpackage-frames.jsx:154-172
findings.implementationSays: PackageEditorUiState.Content data class has no locked or hasActiveBuyers field — PackageEditorViewModel.kt:71-80. EditorCards composable (PackageEditorScreen.kt:161-283) has no conditional branch for locked/warning state. PkgStepper and RedeemTiles are always rendered interactive.

PackageEditorScreen.kt:161-283 — EditorCards has no hasActiveBuyers/locked branch; PkgStepper at line 215 and RedeemTiles at line 203 are unconditionally interactive.

findings.title: iOS: 'has active buyers' locked state (Frame 4) not implemented
findings.designSays: Frame 4 shows locked editing state with a warning Note and disabled tiles/stepper when active buyers exist — createpackage-frames.jsx:154-172

PackageEditorView.swift:65-86 — cards block: detailsCard/redeemCard/sessionsCard/priceCard always rendered fully editable with no locked flag.

screens.crossPlatformNotes: iOS and Android are aligned with each other: both render Description (view-only, not persisted) and Expiry (view-only, not persisted) cards, but neither implements Frame 4 (has-active-buyers locked state). Web diverges on two additional dimensions: it omits Description and Expiry from the UI entirely (not even view-only), in addition to also missing Frame 4. iOS is closest to design for card coverage; Android matches iOS exactly. Web is furthest from design on this screen.
screens.verifierSummary: All five draft findings independently confirmed by reading the source files against the design frame. (1) Web Description omission: confirmed — PackageEditor.tsx:207-215 has only Name; iOS PackageEditorView.swift:99-103 and Android PackageEditorScreen.kt:194-199 both render PkgMultilineField/Description. (2) Web Expiry omission: confirmed — PackageEditor.tsx:201-305 has no Expiry card; iOS:154-165 and Android:262-270 both render PkgSegmented inside an Expiry card. (3-5) Frame 4 has-active-buyers state: confirmed missing on all three platforms — web phase union has no locked variant (PackageEditor.tsx:66), Android Content data class has no locked field (PackageEditorViewModel.kt:71-80), iOS Phase enum has no locked case (PackageEditorViewModel.swift:25). No findings were refuted or downgraded. No evidence of misread file:line citations. One minor observation not elevated to a finding: design uses tone='infoBlue' for the Stripe price note (createpackage-frames.jsx:124) while all platforms use tone='info' — this appears to be a design token alias rather than a gap, as the underlying color is the same blue. Status computation unchanged from draft: web=major (three gaps), iOS=minor (one gap), Android=minor (one gap).

findings.title: Android: EligibleRow uses BadgeCheck icon instead of TicketCheck
findings.designSays: ticket-check (lucide) icon at 16x16 in the EligibleRow
findings.implementationSays: PantopusIcon.BadgeCheck used in EligibleRow (BuyPackageScreen.kt:292). PantopusIcon.TicketCheck does not exist in Icons.kt at all — BadgeCheck (maps to VerifiedUser/shield) was used as a fallback. PantopusIcon.Ticket exists but TicketCheck is absent from the enum entirely.
findings.evidence: buypackage-frames.jsx:51 (<i data-lucide="ticket-check">); BuyPackageScreen.kt:292 (icon = PantopusIcon.BadgeCheck); Icons.kt: no TicketCheck entry, BadgeCheck at line 305 maps to VerifiedUser

findings.title: Android: UpsellBanner uses Tag icon instead of Ticket
findings.designSays: ticket icon (lucide) in the upsell banner header row
findings.implementationSays: PantopusIcon.Tag used at BuyPackageScreen.kt:419. Tag maps to Icons.Filled.Sell (price-tag shape). PantopusIcon.Ticket exists in Icons.kt (line 629, maps to ConfirmationNumber) and is the correct enum to use.
findings.evidence: buypackage-frames.jsx:151 (<i data-lucide="ticket">); BuyPackageScreen.kt:419 (icon = PantopusIcon.Tag); Icons.kt:306+629 (Tag=Sell, Ticket=ConfirmationNumber); BuyPackageView.swift:213 (Icon(.ticket) — iOS correct)

findings.title: Android: GuestEmailCard 'Sign in' inline link has no accent color styling
findings.designSays: 'Sign in' text in the footnote line should be blue600 (#0284c7) and bold
findings.implementationSays: GuestEmailCard renders a flat single-style Text with the full string in appTextSecondary with no AnnotatedString span for 'Sign in' (BuyPackageScreen.kt:381-385). iOS correctly uses a SwiftUI Text concatenation with foregroundStyle(Theme.Color.primary600).fontWeight(.bold) on the 'Sign in' segment.
findings.evidence: buypackage-frames.jsx:84 (color:E.blue600, fontWeight:700 on 'Sign in' span); BuyPackageScreen.kt:381-385 (single Text, color=appTextSecondary, no AnnotatedString); BuyPackageView.swift:191-193 (Text+Text concat, Sign in uses primary600+bold)

findings.title: Web: 'Owned credits' state is a full-screen redirect page rather than inline upsell banner
findings.designSays: Frame 4: inline info banner with existing-credit count + 'Use a credit instead' button embedded in the checkout sheet, alongside SummaryCard and PayMethod — user never leaves the checkout context
findings.implementationSays: Web transitions to a full-screen 'owned' phase (BuyPackage.tsx:175-199) with centred icon, headline 'You already own this package', and 'View my credits' link. Root cause: Web has no pre-purchase credit check — the 'owned' state is triggered only after POST returns ALREADY_OWNED. Both mobile VMs call getMyPackages() during load() to detect credits upfront and show the inline UpsellBanner before purchase.
findings.evidence: buypackage-frames.jsx:144-162 (FrameUpsell with inline UpsellBanner alongside SummaryCard+PayMethod); BuyPackage.tsx:175-199 (full-screen owned phase); BuyPackage.tsx:109 (ALREADY_OWNED triggers owned phase on API error only); BuyPackageViewModel.kt:141-143 (getMyPackages() on load to populate existingCredit)

findings.title: Web: PayMethod row absent from all phases
findings.designSays: Frames 1-4 all include a saved-card PayMethod row (card chip + last-4 + chevron-right) visible in the checkout view before clicking Pay
findings.implementationSays: Web summary phase (BuyPackage.tsx:222-292) shows OwnerCard, SummaryCard, EligibleRow, CancellationPolicy component, and Pay CTA — no PayMethod row. Checkout phase (BuyPackage.tsx:201-219) shows OwnerCard, SummaryCard, then Stripe PaymentElement — also no saved-card row. Note: the draft overstated the Footnote gap — the CancellationPolicy component at line 257 does render equivalent policy copy; only the PayMethod row is truly absent.
findings.evidence: buypackage-frames.jsx:62-69 (PayMethod component); buypackage-frames.jsx:99,117,135,157 (PayMethod in all 4 frames); BuyPackage.tsx:222-292 (summary phase — no PayMethod row); BuyPackage.tsx:201-219 (checkout phase — no PayMethod row, only Stripe PaymentElement)

findings.title: Cross-platform: EligibleRow icon — iOS TicketCheck, Android BadgeCheck (wrong), Web TicketCheck
findings.designSays: ticket-check icon at 16x16 in EligibleRow
findings.implementationSays: iOS and Web correctly use TicketCheck; Android uses BadgeCheck. PantopusIcon.TicketCheck is missing from the Android icon enum entirely — it must be added to Icons.kt before EligibleRow can use it.
findings.evidence: BuyPackageView.swift:144 (Icon(.ticketCheck, size: 16)); BuyPackageScreen.kt:292 (PantopusIcon.BadgeCheck); BuyPackage.tsx:242 (TicketCheck from lucide-react); Icons.kt: no TicketCheck enum entry

findings.title: Web: EligibleRow missing 'Credits expire' caption line
findings.designSays: EligibleRow has three lines: 'Use credits on' (bold), eligible service names, and 'Credits expire 1 year after purchase' (muted caption)
findings.implementationSays: Web EligibleRow renders only the first two lines ('Use credits on' heading and the eligibleLabel value). The 'Credits expire 1 year after purchase' caption is absent.
findings.evidence: buypackage-frames.jsx:53-55 (three lines including expiry caption); BuyPackage.tsx:247-253 (only two lines, no expiry text); BuyPackageView.swift:149-151 (expiry line present); BuyPackageScreen.kt:310-313 (expiry line present)

screens.crossPlatformNotes: iOS is closest to design: correctly uses .ticketCheck in EligibleRow, .ticket in UpsellBanner, and styled 'Sign in' text via Text concatenation. Android has three confirmed major deviations: wrong icons in EligibleRow (BadgeCheck) and UpsellBanner (Tag), and unstyled 'Sign in' footnote — all in Android only. Web has two major structural gaps: no pre-purchase credit detection (shows full-screen 'owned' page instead of inline UpsellBanner) and no PayMethod row in any phase. A minor cross-platform gap is that Web omits the 'Credits expire 1 year after purchase' caption in EligibleRow that iOS and Android both render.
screens.verifierSummary: All six major/blocker draft findings were independently confirmed against source files. No findings were dropped. One finding was narrowed (Finding 5: draft overstated 'Footnote absent' — CancellationPolicy component at BuyPackage.tsx:257 does render policy copy; only the PayMethod row is genuinely absent). One new finding was added: Web EligibleRow missing the 'Credits expire 1 year after purchase' third line (minor, cross-platform). Key additional fact uncovered during verification: PantopusIcon.TicketCheck does not exist in Android Icons.kt at all — the BadgeCheck substitution was a workaround for a missing icon enum entry, not just a wrong selection. The correct fix requires adding TicketCheck to Icons.kt before it can be used in EligibleRow. The 'owned' state root cause was also confirmed: Android and iOS both call getMyPackages() on screen load to detect existing credits inline; Web has no equivalent pre-check and only discovers the state from an ALREADY_OWNED API error.

findings.title: Expiring-soon warning banner missing on all platforms
findings.designSays: Frame 4 (FrameExpiring): when expiringSoon prop is set, a warning-tinted strip appears above the card body — clock-alert icon, warnBg background, warnBorder bottom border, 11pt '2 credits expire in 9 days — book soon.' (mypackages-frames.jsx:42-47)
findings.implementationSays: None of the three platforms implement this banner. The backend my-packages contract exposes no expiry field. iOS VM and Web component both carry comments explicitly acknowledging the omission (MyPackagesViewModel.swift:11-14, MyPackages.tsx:9-10). No expiringSoon branch exists in any CreditCard component.
findings.evidence: Design: mypackages-frames.jsx:42-47. iOS: MyPackagesView.swift:102-201 (no expiringSoon branch); MyPackagesViewModel.swift:11-14 (data note confirms no expiry field). Android: MyPackagesScreen.kt:151-303 (no expiringSoon branch). Web: MyPackages.tsx:9-10 (explicit comment), credits.ts:1-6 (confirms no expiry in contract).

findings.title: Redemption history section missing on all platforms
findings.designSays: Frame 1 (FrameActive): cards with history render an in-card collapsible 'REDEMPTION HISTORY' overline section with per-use rows (check icon, service name, date, '1 credit') and a chevron-up affordance (mypackages-frames.jsx:61-69).
findings.implementationSays: No platform renders a redemption history section. The backend contract exposes no per-redemption rows. All three platforms end the CreditCard body immediately after the CTA button.
findings.evidence: Design: mypackages-frames.jsx:61-69 (HistoryRow component + history block). iOS: MyPackagesView.swift:113-201 (no history section; CreditCard body ends at cta.padding(top:11)). Android: MyPackagesScreen.kt:151-303 (no history section; card body ends at CTA block). Web: MyPackages.tsx:115-214 (no history section).

findings.title: Expiry date label shows 'Purchased' date instead of 'Expires/Ended' date
findings.designSays: Below the meter: 'Expires <date>' for active cards, 'Ended <date>' for spent/expired cards, using an expiry date field (mypackages-frames.jsx:57).
findings.implementationSays: iOS and Android both substitute the purchase date (purchasedAt / purchased_at) and label it 'Purchased <date>' because no expiry field exists in the backend contract. Web renders no sub-meter date label at all (CreditCard body has no date row).
findings.evidence: Design: mypackages-frames.jsx:57 ('Ended ${expires}' / 'Expires ${expires}'). iOS: MyPackagesView.swift:76 (purchased: PackagesFormat.dayString(credit.purchasedAt)), line 128-130 (Text('Purchased \(purchased)')). Android: MyPackagesScreen.kt:163 (val purchased = PackagesFormat.dayString(credit.purchasedAt)), line 247-253 (Text('Purchased $purchased')). Web: MyPackages.tsx:115-214 (no date label in CreditCard body); credits.ts exposes no expiry field.

findings.title: 'Expired' chip (time-expired state) not implemented on any platform
findings.designSays: Frame 3 shows two distinct spent states: 'All used' pill (session-exhausted, state==='used') and 'Expired' pill (time-expired, state==='expired'), both uppercase pill-shaped with sunken-bg/fg3 color (mypackages-frames.jsx:53-54).
findings.implementationSays: All platforms collapse to a single binary 'spent = remaining == 0' check and only render 'All used'. The 'Expired' chip for time-expired packages (where sessions may remain) is never rendered. credits.ts defines only 'active' | 'used' CreditState with no 'expired' type (credits.ts:10).
findings.evidence: Design: mypackages-frames.jsx:53-54 (separate 'Expired' and 'All used' chip branches). iOS: MyPackagesViewModel.swift:93 (isSpent = remaining == 0); MyPackagesView.swift:124 (only 'All used' chip). Android: MyPackagesScreen.kt:160 (spent = remaining == 0), line 225-227 (only 'All used' chip). Web: credits.ts:10 (CreditState = 'active' | 'used', no 'expired'); MyPackages.tsx:170-174 (only 'All used' chip).

findings.title: Web: spent card large-text count renders 'All used' instead of '0 of N left'
findings.designSays: Frame 3 spent state: the 18px/800 count text shows '0 of 5 left' (quantity format) alongside a separate 'All used' pill badge (mypackages-frames.jsx:52-54).
findings.implementationSays: Web's creditCountLabel() returns 'All used' for spent credits (credits.ts:39-41), so the large bold span shows 'All used' (MyPackages.tsx:168) AND the separate chip below also says 'All used' (MyPackages.tsx:171-174), doubling the signal and losing the session count. iOS correctly renders '0 of \(total) left' (MyPackagesView.swift:120). Android renders '$remaining of $total left' which becomes '0 of N left' when spent (MyPackagesScreen.kt:219).
findings.evidence: Design: mypackages-frames.jsx:52 (count text shows '0 of 5 left' for used state). Web: credits.ts:39-41 (creditCountLabel returns 'All used' when state==='used'); MyPackages.tsx:168 (large span: {creditCountLabel(progress)}); MyPackages.tsx:171-174 (chip also says 'All used'). iOS: MyPackagesView.swift:120 (spent ? '0 of \(total) left' : ...). Android: MyPackagesScreen.kt:219 ('$remaining of $total left').

findings.title: Web: empty-state CTA label and route wrong ('Browse your bookings' vs 'Browse services')
findings.designSays: Frame 2 (FrameEmpty): EmptyHero action button label is 'Browse services' (mypackages-frames.jsx:99).
findings.implementationSays: Web renders 'Browse your bookings' routing to /app/scheduling/my-bookings (MyPackages.tsx:89-95). iOS uses 'Browse services' (MyPackagesView.swift:52). Android uses 'Browse services' (MyPackagesScreen.kt:117).
findings.evidence: Design: mypackages-frames.jsx:99 (button text 'Browse services'). Web: MyPackages.tsx:89-95 (button text 'Browse your bookings', onClick routes to /app/scheduling/my-bookings). iOS: MyPackagesView.swift:52 (CTA title: 'Browse services'). Android: MyPackagesScreen.kt:117 (ctaTitle = 'Browse services').

findings.title: Web: active-state meter fill uses owner pillar color instead of always-personal-sky
findings.designSays: Meter fill color is hardcoded to SKY (E.personal = #0284c7) regardless of owner pillar; buyer chrome is always Personal sky (mypackages-frames.jsx:10, line 56: 'color={spent?E.borderStrong:SKY}').
findings.implementationSays: Web CreditCard derives tk = pillarTokens(pillarForOwner(ownerType)) and uses tk.bg for the active meter fill (MyPackages.tsx:124, 187), meaning a Business-owned package shows a violet meter and a Home-owned package shows a green meter. iOS uses Theme.Color.personal unconditionally (MyPackagesView.swift:167). Android uses PantopusColors.personal unconditionally (MyPackagesScreen.kt:243).
findings.evidence: Design: mypackages-frames.jsx:10 (const SKY = E.personal), line 56 (Meter color={spent?E.borderStrong:SKY}). Web: MyPackages.tsx:124 (tk = pillarTokens(pillarForOwner(ownerType))); line 186-189 (spent ? 'bg-app-border-strong' : tk.bg — uses pillar-derived bg). iOS: MyPackagesView.swift:167 (fill: spent ? Theme.Color.appBorderStrong : Theme.Color.personal). Android: MyPackagesScreen.kt:242-244 (background: if (spent) PantopusColors.appBorderStrong else PantopusColors.personal).

findings.title: Web: owner name for personal pillar shows 'Host' instead of 'Personal provider'
findings.designSays: n/a — no design (cross-platform-only)
findings.implementationSays: Web CreditCard renders 'Host' for the personal pillar owner name (MyPackages.tsx:131). iOS renders 'Personal provider' (MyPackagesView.swift:145, 158). Android renders 'Personal provider' (MyPackagesScreen.kt:163, 327-332). All three derive a fallback label from the owner type since no real owner display name exists in the contract, but web's label diverges.
findings.evidence: Web: MyPackages.tsx:127-132 (ownerName: ownerType === 'business' ? 'Business' : ownerType === 'home' ? 'Home' : 'Host'). iOS: MyPackagesView.swift:145 (ownerName = '\(ownerTheme.title) provider'). Android: MyPackagesScreen.kt:163 (val ownerName = '${pillar.providerLabel()} provider') + MyPackagesScreen.kt:327-332 (Personal → 'Personal provider').

screens.crossPlatformNotes: iOS and Android are closely aligned: both show 'Purchased' date (wrong vs design's expiry date), both correctly render '0 of N left' for spent count text, both correctly use personal-sky for meter fill, and both label the empty-state CTA 'Browse services'. Web diverges on five points: spent large-count text shows 'All used' (doubling the chip signal), empty-state CTA label/route is wrong, meter fill uses pillar color instead of always-sky, no sub-meter date is rendered at all, and owner name for personal pillar is 'Host' rather than 'Personal provider'. Three findings (expiring-soon banner, redemption history, 'Expired' chip) are absent on all platforms due to missing backend data. iOS is closest to design.
screens.verifierSummary: All 7 draft major/blocker findings were independently confirmed by reading the actual source files. No finding was refuted or downgraded. Key confirmations: (1) expiring-soon banner confirmed absent on all three platforms — no expiringSoon branch exists in any CreditCard, and VM/web comments explicitly note the backend contract gap; (2) redemption history section confirmed absent — card body ends at the CTA on all platforms; (3) 'Purchased' vs 'Expires/Ended' confirmed — iOS MyPackagesView.swift:76+129 and Android MyPackagesScreen.kt:163+249 use purchasedAt, web has no date label at all; (4) 'Expired' chip confirmed absent — credits.ts only defines CreditState as 'active'|'used' and all platforms use a simple remaining==0 check; (5) web large-count 'All used' confirmed — credits.ts:39-41 returns 'All used' for spent state, creating a double-signal with the chip; (6) web empty-state CTA label 'Browse your bookings' confirmed at MyPackages.tsx:93; (7) web meter fill using tk.bg (pillar-derived) confirmed at MyPackages.tsx:187 vs design's hardcoded SKY. One new nit finding added: web labels the personal-pillar owner as 'Host' (MyPackages.tsx:131) while iOS and Android both render 'Personal provider', a cross-platform string inconsistency.

findings.title: Summary card KPI labels wrong on iOS and Android — design says Outstanding / Collected · month
findings.designSays: Left column label: 'Outstanding' (amber when any invoice is overdue). Right column label: 'Collected · month'. Both driven by per-invoice status + paid_at data. (invoiceslist-frames.jsx lines 25, 30)
findings.implementationSays: iOS shows 'Invoices' (count) / 'Total invoiced' (sum of all totals). Android shows 'Invoices' / 'Total invoiced' identically. Neither surfaces outstanding vs collected-this-month semantics. iOS has the overdue-amber capability wired in but hasOverdue is always false; Android has no overdue parameter on SummaryCard at all.
findings.evidence: iOS InvoicesListView.swift:134,136 (stat labels 'Invoices' / 'Total invoiced'); InvoicesListViewModel.swift:62-71 (totalLabel = sum, countLabel = count, hasOverdue always false). Android InvoicesListScreen.kt:218,220 (Stat labels 'Invoices' / 'Total invoiced'); InvoicesListViewModel.kt:111-123 (totalLabel = sum, countLabel = count, no hasOverdue). Web InvoiceList.tsx:257-284 (SummaryCard uses 'Outstanding' / 'Collected · month' correctly via summarizeInvoices). Design invoiceslist-frames.jsx:25,30.

findings.title: Status pill missing from invoice row trailing on iOS and Android
findings.designSays: Each row's trailing area shows amount + a filled pill (Paid=green, Sent=sky-blue, Overdue=amber, Void=muted, Refunded=violet). Pill text is uppercase, 9px, pill-radius. (invoiceslist-frames.jsx lines 57-58)
findings.implementationSays: iOS InvoiceRow trailing VStack contains only the amount Text; comment at line 234-237 explicitly defers the pill pending DTO status field. Android InvoiceRow (lines 347-352) renders only a single amount Text with no pill.
findings.evidence: iOS InvoicesListView.swift:230-238 (VStack trailing: amount only; comment 'pill is deferred' at lines 234-237). Android InvoicesListScreen.kt:347-352 (only amount Text after the Column closing at line 346, no pill). Web InvoiceList.tsx:322-324 (InvoiceStatusPill rendered correctly). Design invoiceslist-frames.jsx:57-58.

findings.title: Android SummaryCard has no overdue-amber wiring at all (iOS at least has deferred capability)
findings.designSays: n/a — no design (cross-platform parity note)
findings.implementationSays: iOS stat() accepts an overdue: Bool that paints label + value in Theme.Color.warning (InvoicesListView.swift:143-155), ready to activate once hasOverdue is real. Android Stat composable (InvoicesListScreen.kt:225-251) has no overdue parameter — color is hardcoded to PantopusColors.appTextSecondary / PantopusColors.appText with no path to activate the amber treatment.
findings.evidence: iOS InvoicesListView.swift:143 (func stat(label:, value:, overdue: Bool)); Android InvoicesListScreen.kt:225-251 (Stat has no overdue param, colors are fixed). Android InvoicesListViewModel.kt:52-58 (Loaded state has no hasOverdue field).

screens.crossPlatformNotes: Web is closest to design: SummaryCard uses correct 'Outstanding' / 'Collected · month' labels with overdue-amber treatment and InvoiceStatusPill renders on every row. iOS and Android both use 'Invoices' / 'Total invoiced' labels and omit the status pill from rows — both gaps are DTO-blocked (status field absent) and documented in code comments as deferred. Android additionally lacks the overdue-amber wiring that iOS already has stubbed in, meaning Android is one step further from design than iOS on the summary card.
screens.verifierSummary: Both blocker/major findings from the draft were independently confirmed. Finding 1 (KPI labels): design frame line 25 shows 'Outstanding' and line 30 shows 'Collected · month'; iOS InvoicesListView.swift:134,136 uses 'Invoices'/'Total invoiced' and InvoicesListViewModel.swift:62-71 confirms hasOverdue is always false; Android InvoicesListScreen.kt:218,220 mirrors iOS identically. Draft line citations were slightly off (134-136 vs 134,136; 62-66 vs 62-71) but substance was correct. Finding 2 (status pill): iOS InvoicesListView.swift:230-238 confirmed trailing column has amount only with an explicit deferral comment at lines 234-237; Android InvoicesListScreen.kt:347-352 confirmed only a single amount Text with no pill. Web InvoiceList.tsx:322-324 confirmed correct InvoiceStatusPill. Both findings survive unchanged in severity. One new minor cross-platform finding added: Android SummaryCard has no overdue parameter at all (unlike iOS which has the wiring stubbed ready to activate), making Android one step further behind iOS on the amber treatment.

findings.title: Status pill missing from top-bar trailing slot on iOS and Android
findings.designSays: TopBar has trailing={<StatusPill s={status}/>} — a filled pill showing the invoice lifecycle state (draft/sent/paid/partial/overdue/void/refunded) in the top-bar trailing position (invoicedetail-frames.jsx:159)
findings.implementationSays: iOS calls PkgTopBar(title: "Invoice", onBack: { dismiss() }) with no trailing argument (SchedulingInvoiceDetailView.swift:32); the PkgTopBar struct accepts a generic Trailing: View defaulting to EmptyView (PackagesKit.swift:95). Android calls PkgTopBar(title = "Invoice", onBack = onBack) with no trailing composable (InvoiceDetailScreen.kt:82); PkgTopBar has trailing: @Composable () -> Unit = {} (PackagesKit.kt:209). Both slots exist but are left empty. Web correctly renders InvoiceStatusPill in the header (InvoiceDetail.tsx:127-129).
findings.evidence: invoicedetail-frames.jsx:159 vs SchedulingInvoiceDetailView.swift:32 and PackagesKit.swift:90-98; InvoiceDetailScreen.kt:82 and PackagesKit.kt:205-262

findings.title: Timeline section absent on Android
findings.designSays: A 'Timeline' section (Section title='Timeline' icon='activity') renders between line items and payment terms in all 7 status frames, showing lifecycle dots with connecting rails for Created/Sent/Paid/Deposit/Refunded/Voided events (invoicedetail-frames.jsx:166)
findings.implementationSays: Android LoadedBody renders: header line -> hero -> payer/payee -> line items -> payment terms (InvoiceDetailScreen.kt:140-224). No SectionHeader or Timeline composable exists. The VM comment explicitly states the omission: 'no status / timeline / due date / memo / payer name -> those design sections are omitted' (InvoiceDetailViewModel.kt:56). iOS does render a timelineSection (SchedulingInvoiceDetailView.swift:66,183-198), though only Created+Sent events display due to absent status/paid_at DTO fields.
findings.evidence: invoicedetail-frames.jsx:166 vs InvoiceDetailScreen.kt:188-224 (line items at 188, payment terms at 211 with no timeline composable between them); InvoiceDetailViewModel.kt:54-57

findings.title: Dock CTA set hardcoded to Share+Send — ignores status-based variants on iOS and Android
findings.designSays: Seven distinct dock configurations per status: draft=Send only; sent=Mark paid+Resend+Overflow; paid=Share+Download PDF; partial=Mark paid+Send balance; overdue=Mark paid+Resend+Overflow; void=Share only; refunded=Share+Download PDF (invoicedetail-frames.jsx:196-202)
findings.implementationSays: iOS dock always renders ShareLink+'Share' ghost + PkgPrimaryButton 'Send' regardless of invoice status (SchedulingInvoiceDetailView.swift:243-255). Android dock always renders PkgGhostButton 'Share' + PkgPrimaryButton 'Send' (InvoiceDetailScreen.kt:226-241). Neither VM exposes a status field in its Loaded state (SchedulingInvoiceDetailViewModel has no status property; InvoiceDetailUiState.Loaded has no status field per InvoiceDetailViewModel.kt:37-48), making status-driven variants structurally impossible without model changes. Web is also incomplete but different: it shows Send/Resend conditionally on canSend.
findings.evidence: invoicedetail-frames.jsx:196-202 vs SchedulingInvoiceDetailView.swift:243-255; InvoiceDetailScreen.kt:226-241; InvoiceDetailViewModel.kt:37-48; SchedulingInvoiceDetailViewModel.swift:30-34

findings.title: Web dock shows no Share or Download PDF CTA for paid/refunded/void invoices
findings.designSays: paid=Share+Download PDF; void=Share only; refunded=Share+Download PDF (invoicedetail-frames.jsx:198-202)
findings.implementationSays: Web dock renders a Send/Resend button only when canSend (status draft/sent/overdue, InvoiceDetail.tsx:111-112,148-163). For paid/void/refunded the dock container renders but is empty — no Share or Download PDF button appears at all. The component header comment documents this as intentional ('Mark-paid / refund / PDF aren't backend-supported, so they're omitted') but the Share action requires no backend call and is still absent.
findings.evidence: invoicedetail-frames.jsx:198-202 vs InvoiceDetail.tsx:111-113,147-163

findings.title: Web line-items table missing Unit price column
findings.designSays: ItemsTable uses a 4-column grid: Item / Qty / Unit / Total, with a distinct Unit column showing per-unit prices (invoicedetail-frames.jsx:84-93)
findings.implementationSays: Web line-items table uses a 3-column grid: grid-cols-[1fr_2rem_4rem] rendering Item / Qty / Total only (InvoiceDetail.tsx:229-248). The Unit price column is absent. iOS and Android both implement the 4-column layout with ITEM/QTY/UNIT/TOTAL headers (SchedulingInvoiceDetailView.swift:137-143; InvoiceDetailScreen.kt:337-341).
findings.evidence: invoicedetail-frames.jsx:84 vs InvoiceDetail.tsx:229 (grid-cols-[1fr_2rem_4rem] = 3 cols); SchedulingInvoiceDetailView.swift:138-141 and InvoiceDetailScreen.kt:338-341 (4-col grids with UNIT header)

screens.crossPlatformNotes: iOS is closest to design: it renders the Timeline section (deferred/partial due to absent status/paid_at DTO fields), implements the 4-column line-items table, and passes the status pill trailing slot (though unused). Android omits the Timeline section entirely and the status pill slot is also unused. Web has the status pill correct but omits the Unit column in line items and has an empty dock for paid/void/refunded states. All three platforms share the hardcoded dock CTA problem (Send+Share always, never the 7 status-specific configurations).
screens.verifierSummary: All four draft findings confirmed by direct source inspection. No findings were dropped or downgraded. Corrections made: (1) Finding 1 evidence now cites the actual iOS PkgTopBar generic trailing slot (PackagesKit.swift:90-98) and Android trailing lambda (PackagesKit.kt:209) to prove the slot exists but is left empty; web correctly renders the pill so it is excluded from the platforms list (already correct in the draft). (2) Finding 2 evidence corrected — the VM comment is at InvoiceDetailViewModel.kt:54-57, not :55; the Android line numbers for payment terms are confirmed at 211-223 with no Timeline composable between items (188) and terms (211). (3) Finding 3 now includes confirmation that neither VM exposes a status field in its Loaded state, making the status-driven variants structurally impossible at the data-model level, not just a view omission. (4) Finding 4 language sharpened to note the Share action requires no backend call and is still absent, making the intentional-omission justification incomplete. One new finding added: the web line-items table uses a 3-column grid (Item/Qty/Total) while design and both mobile platforms use 4 columns (Item/Qty/Unit/Total), dropping the unit price column on web. This was missed by the first-pass auditor.

findings.title: Android no-show row uses UserMinus icon; design specifies user-x
findings.designSays: policy-sheet-frames.jsx line 52: data-lucide="user-x" on the no-show icon tile
findings.implementationSays: CancellationRefundPolicyScreen.kt line 294: RowIconTile(PantopusIcon.UserMinus) — maps to Icons.Filled.PersonRemove
findings.evidence: policy-sheet-frames.jsx:52 vs CancellationRefundPolicyScreen.kt:294 and Icons.kt:597,996. iOS correctly uses .userX (CancellationPolicyEditorView.swift:150).

findings.title: Android stepper value text rendered in appText (dark) instead of accent (violet)
findings.designSays: biz-kit.jsx Stepper line 169: color:accent||E.fg1 — stepper value adopts the BIZ violet accent
findings.implementationSays: CancellationRefundPolicyScreen.kt PolicyStepper line 387: color = PantopusColors.appText (dark neutral). iOS (CancellationPolicyEditorView.swift:318) and Web (RefundPolicyEditor.tsx:127) both correctly use the accent color.
findings.evidence: biz-kit.jsx:169 vs CancellationRefundPolicyScreen.kt:387

findings.title: Web no-show row uses inline toggle button instead of design's chevron-right disclosure row
findings.designSays: policy-sheet-frames.jsx line 57: chevron-right trailing on no-show row; sub-label 'Charge full price' is static (row opens a sub-picker)
findings.implementationSays: RefundPolicyEditor.tsx lines 212-227: renders a plain text button ('Charge full' / 'No charge') that toggles in place. No chevron. Also web has only 2 no-show modes (charge_full / no_charge); iOS and Android have 3 (adds charge_deposit).
findings.evidence: policy-sheet-frames.jsx:57 vs RefundPolicyEditor.tsx:202-228 and policyPresets.ts:33

findings.title: iOS preview label 'What the invitee sees' is not transformed to uppercase
findings.designSays: policy-sheet-frames.jsx Preview component line 70: textTransform:'uppercase', letterSpacing:'0.06em' — label is uppercase
findings.implementationSays: CancellationPolicyEditorView.swift line 203-206: Text("What the invitee sees") with .tracking(0.5) but no .textCase(.uppercase). Android hardcodes 'WHAT THE INVITEE SEES' (correct); Web uses CSS 'uppercase' class (correct).
findings.evidence: policy-sheet-frames.jsx:70 vs CancellationPolicyEditorView.swift:203-206

findings.title: iOS stepper button diameter is 26 dp; design specifies 22 dp (small variant)
findings.designSays: biz-kit.jsx Stepper line 165: small=true → h=22, ic=11
findings.implementationSays: CancellationPolicyEditorView.swift line 334: .frame(width: 26, height: 26). Android uses 22 dp (CancellationRefundPolicyScreen.kt:406). Web uses h-7 w-7 = 28 px — also oversized.
findings.evidence: biz-kit.jsx:165 vs CancellationPolicyEditorView.swift:334, CancellationRefundPolicyScreen.kt:406

findings.title: Web custom-row icon tiles are 32 px (h-8 w-8); design specifies 30×30
findings.designSays: policy-sheet-frames.jsx CustomRows lines 37/42/47/52: width:30, height:30 icon tiles
findings.implementationSays: RefundPolicyEditor.tsx lines 113/170/203: className="flex h-8 w-8 ..." = 32 px. iOS and Android both use 30 dp (correct).
findings.evidence: policy-sheet-frames.jsx:37 vs RefundPolicyEditor.tsx:113

findings.title: Cross-platform: refund-after-cutoff stepper granularity diverges (web 25 % steps vs native 5 % steps)
findings.designSays: n/a — no design — design shows a Stepper with value='50%' but does not enumerate allowed steps
findings.implementationSays: Web policyPresets.ts:57: REFUND_STEPS = [0, 25, 50, 75, 100] (25 % increments). iOS CancellationPolicyEditorViewModel.swift:195: REFUND_STEP = 5 (5 % increments). Android REFUND_STEP = 5. Web allows only 4 values; native allows 21 — a host with 30 % or 40 % policies set via native cannot be re-saved accurately on web.
findings.evidence: policyPresets.ts:57 vs CancellationRefundPolicyViewModel.kt:399

findings.title: Cross-platform: cutoff ladder diverges — web has 168 h (1 week) step; native does not; native has 4 h step; web does not
findings.designSays: n/a — no design — design shows a Stepper with value='24h' only
findings.implementationSays: Web CUTOFF_STEPS = [0,1,2,6,12,24,48,72,168] (no 4 h, has 168 h). iOS/Android CUTOFF_LADDER = [0,1,2,4,6,12,24,48,72] (has 4 h, no 168 h). A 1-week cutoff set on web cannot be displayed or preserved via native steppers.
findings.evidence: policyPresets.ts:55 vs CancellationRefundPolicyViewModel.kt:398

findings.title: Cross-platform: Android error toast appears at BottomCenter; iOS toast appears at top of screen
findings.designSays: n/a — no design — policy-sheet-frames.jsx shows no error-state frame
findings.implementationSays: iOS CancellationPolicyEditorView.swift:48: .overlay(alignment: .top). Android CancellationRefundPolicyScreen.kt:109: Modifier.align(Alignment.BottomCenter). The two platforms display save-error feedback in opposite vertical positions.
findings.evidence: CancellationPolicyEditorView.swift:48 vs CancellationRefundPolicyScreen.kt:109

screens.crossPlatformNotes: The draft status (ios:minor, android:minor, web:minor) is confirmed and unchanged. No blocker or low-confidence findings were present to dispute. All three platforms route G14 as a full-screen navigation destination despite the design calling it a 'Selector sheet'; this is a consistent architectural decision across platforms and was not flagged. iOS is closest to the design on icon choice, accent color use, and stepper structure. Android diverges on the no-show icon (UserMinus vs user-x) and stepper value color (appText vs accent violet). Web diverges most on the no-show row interaction pattern (inline toggle instead of chevron disclosure) and has coarser refund-step granularity (25 % vs native 5 %), creating a round-trip fidelity hazard. The cutoff ladder mismatch (web has 168 h / native has 4 h) is a symmetric cross-platform gap. Two new findings were added by the verifier: the stepper value color on Android and the cutoff/refund step-ladder divergences.
screens.verifierSummary: No blocker or major/low-confidence findings existed in the draft to refute. All 9 original findings were independently verified in source; this pass confirmed them and corrected/sharpened evidence citations.
