Two additional confirmed findings were added: (1) Android renders the stepper value in PantopusColors.appText instead of the PILLAR accent (biz-kit.jsx:169 vs CancellationRefundPolicyScreen.kt:387) — missed by the first pass; (2) the refund-step granularity divergence (web 25 % / native 5 %) and the cutoff-ladder divergence (web has 168 h step; native has 4 h step) were confirmed as concrete round-trip fidelity risks, not just cosmetic differences. The full-screen vs bottom-sheet presentation difference was reviewed but treated as a deliberate, consistent platform routing decision (all three platforms agree, and iOS source code even notes 'Routed full screen; styled like the design's selector sheet'), so it was not added as a finding. Per-platform status is unchanged from the draft.

findings.title: Web missing push-permission-gated banner (Frame 4)
findings.designSays: Frame 4 (FrameGated, reminders-frames.jsx:96-111) shows an amber AutoNote banner with bell-off icon, text 'Push is off in iOS Settings. Email still works.', and an 'Enable' action button when push permission is denied.
findings.implementationSays: RemindersQuickSetup.tsx has no push/notification permission check and no warning banner. The component never reads notification authorization state; it imports Bell, Mail, Plus, etc. but not any OS notification API.
findings.evidence: design reminders-frames.jsx:96-111 (FrameGated function, warnBg banner); web RemindersQuickSetup.tsx — full file reviewed, no notification permission check, no banner. iOS DefaultRemindersView.swift:70-78 and :192-206 confirms pushOff state + pushOffBanner rendered. Android RemindersQuickSetupScreen.kt:32-36 (areNotificationsEnabled check) and :166-179 (AutoNote rendered when pushOff=true).

findings.title: Web uses different backend endpoint than iOS and Android
findings.designSays: n/a — no design
findings.implementationSays: Web calls GET/PUT /notification-preferences (reminders.ts:1-6; RemindersQuickSetup.tsx:63 api.scheduling.getNotificationPreferences), reading scheduling.reminder_minutes. iOS and Android both call GET/PUT /booking-page reading page.reminderMinutes — a structurally different resource and data path.
findings.evidence: web reminders.ts:1-6 (comment explicitly states '/notification-preferences … scheduling.reminder_minutes'); web RemindersQuickSetup.tsx:63 getNotificationPreferences, :116 updateNotificationPreferences; iOS DefaultRemindersViewModel.swift:81 SchedulingEndpoints.getBookingPage, :147 SchedulingEndpoints.updateBookingPage, reading result.page.reminderMinutes; Android RemindersQuickSetupViewModel.kt:58 repo.getBookingPage, :126 repo.updateBookingPage, reading result.data.page.reminderMinutes.

findings.title: Android push-off banner copy diverges from design ('system settings' vs 'iOS Settings')
findings.designSays: reminders-frames.jsx:103 — 'Push is off in iOS Settings. Email still works.'
findings.implementationSays: Android RemindersQuickSetupScreen.kt:172 reads 'Push is off in system settings. Email still works.' (lowercase 's', 'system' instead of 'iOS'). iOS DefaultRemindersView.swift:196 matches the design exactly.
findings.evidence: design reminders-frames.jsx:103; Android RemindersQuickSetupScreen.kt:172; iOS DefaultRemindersView.swift:196.

screens.crossPlatformNotes: iOS is closest to design: it implements all four frames (default, first-open, saved toast, push-off banner) using the /booking-page endpoint. Android matches iOS for all four frames and the same endpoint, but its push-off banner copy says 'system settings' instead of 'iOS Settings' (a deliberate platform adaptation, though it diverges from the design literal). Web is furthest: it uses a completely different backend resource (/notification-preferences vs /booking-page) and omits Frame 4 entirely — no push-permission check or amber warning banner is implemented anywhere in the component.
screens.verifierSummary: Both major findings were independently confirmed by reading the full source files. Finding 1 (web missing push-permission banner): verified correct — iOS DefaultRemindersView.swift:70-78 checks model.pushOff and renders pushOffBanner (lines 192-206); Android RemindersQuickSetupScreen.kt:32-36 checks areNotificationsEnabled() and renders AutoNote at lines 166-179; web RemindersQuickSetup.tsx has no notification permission code anywhere. Finding 2 (web uses different endpoint): verified correct — web explicitly uses api.scheduling.getNotificationPreferences / updateNotificationPreferences targeting /notification-preferences with a scheduling.reminder_minutes key; iOS and Android both use getBookingPage / updateBookingPage targeting /booking-page with page.reminderMinutes. No finding was refuted or downgraded. One additional nit was added: Android's push-off banner copy says 'system settings' while design and iOS say 'iOS Settings'.

findings.title: Web error state: wrong icon, wrong container, wrong headline, wrong button style
findings.designSays: Frame 4 (workflows-list-frames.jsx:128-141): centered bordered white card (radius 16, 1px border, soft box-shadow), 48x48 neutral circle (E.sunken bg, E.fg3 tint) with cloud-off icon (23x23), headline 'Couldn't load workflows' (13.5px/bold), filled sky 'Try again' button (no icon, no border)
findings.implementationSays: Web delegates to the shared ErrorState component (ErrorState.tsx:10-28): AlertTriangle icon in a red-50 rounded-2xl tile (no card border or shadow), headline hardcoded to 'Something went wrong', the message prop 'Couldn't load workflows.' appears only as a secondary paragraph below that headline, and the retry button is a red-bordered ghost style (text-red-700 border border-red-300)
findings.evidence: Design: workflows-list-frames.jsx:128-141. Web call site: WorkflowList.tsx:224-228 (ErrorState message='Couldn't load workflows.' onRetry=...). Component impl: ErrorState.tsx:10-28

findings.title: Android empty-state uses Sparkles icon instead of Workflow icon
findings.designSays: FrameEmpty (workflows-list-frames.jsx:95): empty-state icon circle uses data-lucide='workflow'
findings.implementationSays: Android WorkflowsEmpty uses PantopusIcon.Sparkles (WorkflowsListScreen.kt:315-316); PantopusIcon.Workflow does not exist in the Android icon enum (Icons.kt). iOS correctly maps .workflow to the arrow.triangle.branch SF Symbol (Icons.swift:400,761; WorkflowsListView.swift:188,198).
findings.evidence: Design: workflows-list-frames.jsx:95. Android: WorkflowsListScreen.kt:314-316; Icons.kt (no Workflow entry, Sparkles at line 336). iOS: WorkflowsListView.swift:188; Icons.swift:400

screens.crossPlatformNotes: iOS is the closest to design: it implements the error card inline with the exact cloud-off icon, correct headline, and filled-sky button (WorkflowsListView.swift:88-125). Android also correctly implements the error card via AutoErrorView(asCard=true, headline='Couldn't load workflows') with cloud-off icon and filled primary600 button (WorkflowsListScreen.kt:112-118; AutomationsKit.kt:760-820). Web is the main outlier — it re-uses the shared generic ErrorState component which diverges in icon, container shape, headline text, and button style. Android has a secondary cross-platform divergence using Sparkles instead of the Workflow icon in the empty state.
screens.verifierSummary: Confirmed the major web error-state finding: all four claimed divergences (AlertTriangle vs cloud-off, red-50 tile vs neutral circle card, 'Something went wrong' vs 'Couldn't load workflows' headline, red ghost vs filled-sky button) were independently verified in ErrorState.tsx and WorkflowList.tsx against workflows-list-frames.jsx Frame 4. File:line citations are accurate. Added one new minor cross-platform finding: Android's empty state uses PantopusIcon.Sparkles while the design specifies the workflow icon and iOS correctly uses .workflow — PantopusIcon.Workflow does not exist in the Android Icons.kt enum at all. Per-platform status recomputed: ios=minor (empty-state icon is correct, Workflow icon exists), android=minor (error card is correct; empty-state icon substitution is minor), web=major (error state has four simultaneous design divergences).

findings.title: Web missing Build/Activity tab strip entirely
findings.designSays: n/a — no design
findings.implementationSays: iOS and Android both render an AutoUnderlineTabs strip with 'Build' and 'Activity' tabs above a scrollable Build form and a separate Activity empty state. Web renders a flat single-column form with no tabs at all — there is no Activity tab and no tab strip.
findings.evidence: iOS: WorkflowEditorView.swift:38-43 (AutoUnderlineTabs tabs=['Build','Activity']), :268-279 (activityBody). Android: WorkflowEditorScreen.kt:90-95 (AutoUnderlineTabs), :391-405 (ActivityTab). Web: WorkflowEditor.tsx — full-file grep for 'tab/Tab/Activity/Build' yields zero results; only 'Segmented' import at line 27.

findings.title: Web has no Activity tab / empty run-log state
findings.designSays: n/a — no design
findings.implementationSays: iOS and Android both show an AutoInlineEmpty ('No activity yet') when the Activity tab is selected. Web has no equivalent surface at all.
findings.evidence: iOS: WorkflowEditorView.swift:268-279 (activityBody → AutoInlineEmpty icon:.clock headline:'No activity yet'). Android: WorkflowEditorScreen.kt:391-405 (ActivityTab → AutoInlineEmpty). Web: WorkflowEditor.tsx — absent.

findings.title: Web uses a top-of-screen page header instead of AutoTopBar chrome
findings.designSays: n/a — no design
findings.implementationSays: iOS and Android share a 46dp/pt AutoTopBar with centered title, leading X (close), trailing 'Save' text button. Web renders a plain <header> with a back-link '← Workflows', an <h1>, and a subtitle paragraph — no close/back icon button, no trailing Save in the chrome; instead Save appears in a bottom CTA row at line 397.
findings.evidence:
iOS: WorkflowEditorView.swift:30-37 (AutoTopBar leading:.close trailing:AutoTopBarTextButton 'Save'). Android: WorkflowEditorScreen.kt:77-89 (AutoTopBar + AutoTopBarTextButton 'Save'). Web: WorkflowEditor.tsx:190-204 (
with back button,

,

subtitle); save CTA at lines 393-399.

findings.title: Web adds an 'Applies to' event-type scope field absent on iOS and Android
findings.designSays: n/a — no design
findings.implementationSays: Web renders an 'Applies to' <select> that lets users restrict the workflow to a specific event type (or 'All event types'). iOS and Android have no equivalent field — both platforms hardcode eventTypeId to nil on create and never expose scope selection to the user.
findings.evidence: Web: WorkflowEditor.tsx:217-235 (Field label='Applies to', select for event_type_id). iOS: WorkflowEditorViewModel.swift:183 (eventTypeId: nil in CreateWorkflowRequest, no UI field). Android: WorkflowEditorViewModel.kt:137 (eventTypeId = null in CreateWorkflowRequest, no UI field).

findings.title: Web action picker is a Segmented control (row of tall buttons); mobile uses AutoChannelChip (small capsules in a row)
findings.designSays: n/a — no design
findings.implementationSays: iOS and Android render channel selection as a horizontal row of small capsule AutoChannelChip elements (height 24dp/pt, font 10.5sp, 'Soon' text badge for SMS) inside a bordered card. Web renders the same four options as a Segmented pill control (h-9, min-w-[84px] per segment) — visually much taller, card-free. Note: web does render a 'soon' text label inside the locked SMS segment (kit.tsx:303-306), so that specific claim in the draft was slightly inaccurate; the broader layout divergence is confirmed.
findings.evidence: iOS: WorkflowEditorView.swift:167-190, AutomationsKit.swift:216-262 (AutoChannelChip height:24, font:10.5, 'Soon' text). Android: WorkflowEditorScreen.kt:357-377, AutomationsKit.kt:304-340 (AutoChannelChip heightIn(min=24.dp), 10.5.sp, 'Soon' text). Web: WorkflowEditor.tsx:311-323 (Segmented), kit.tsx:267-311 (h-9, min-w-[84px], soon span at line 303).

findings.title: Web 'message' section omits Insert variable bar, character counter, and Preview button
findings.designSays: n/a — no design
findings.implementationSays: iOS and Android render an 'Insert variable' dashed capsule button above the message textarea, a live character counter (messageCount / counterLimit), and a 'Preview' link. Web has a 'Insert a saved template' native <select> dropdown (conditionally rendered only when templates exist) but no variable-insertion button, no character counter, and no Preview link.
findings.evidence: iOS: WorkflowEditorView.swift:192-220 (insertVariableBar dashed capsule, counter messageCount/counterLimit, Preview Button). Android: WorkflowEditorScreen.kt:292-327 (AutoDashedChip 'Insert variable', counter text, Preview Row). Web: WorkflowEditor.tsx:325-359 — template select only (conditional on templates.length > 0); no variable chip, no counter, no Preview.

findings.title: Web treats workflow name as required; iOS and Android treat it as optional
findings.designSays: n/a — no design
findings.implementationSays: Web's validateWorkflow blocks save with 'Give this workflow a name.' if name is empty. iOS and Android treat name as 'Name · optional' — the UI label says optional, and both VMs silently substitute channel.actionSummary as the server-side name when the field is blank. This is a behavioral divergence: web requires a name, mobile does not.
findings.evidence: Web: workflowMeta.ts:228 (if (!name) errors.name = 'Give this workflow a name.'). iOS: WorkflowEditorView.swift:138 (sectionHeader('Name · optional')), WorkflowEditorViewModel.swift:81-84 (resolvedName falls back to channel.actionSummary). Android: WorkflowEditorScreen.kt:181 (Section header='Name · optional'), WorkflowEditorViewModel.kt:225 (resolvedName = name.trim().ifBlank { channel.actionSummary }).

screens.crossPlatformNotes: iOS and Android are closely aligned — both have AutoTopBar+AutoUnderlineTabs chrome, AutoChannelChip capsule action picker, Insert variable bar, character counter, Preview button, optional name field, and an Activity tab with AutoInlineEmpty. iOS is marginally closer to the mobile consensus (same structure, same component library). Web diverges substantially: missing the tab strip and Activity surface entirely, using a page-header instead of AutoTopBar chrome, adding a unique 'Applies to' event-type scope selector, replacing capsule channel chips with a Segmented control, omitting the variable-insertion bar/counter/Preview from the message section, and requiring a non-empty name where mobile treats it as optional.
screens.verifierSummary: All six draft major/high-confidence findings were independently confirmed by reading the cited source files. No findings were dropped or downgraded. One correction was made to finding #5: the draft claimed the web SMS option is 'merely dimmed/disabled rather than showing a Soon badge' — in fact kit.tsx:303-306 does render a 'soon' text span inside the locked Segmented button, so that specific sub-claim was inaccurate. The broader layout divergence (Segmented vs AutoChannelChip capsules) is fully confirmed. One additional minor discrepancy was added: web makes the workflow name a required field (validateWorkflow blocks save if blank), while both iOS and Android label it 'Name · optional' and silently substitute channel.actionSummary as fallback — a behavioral divergence that the original auditor missed.

findings.title: Web has no Starter templates section; iOS and Android show a seeded read-only 'Starter templates' card
findings.designSays: n/a — no design
findings.implementationSays: iOS (MessageTemplateLibraryView.swift:95-116) and Android (TemplateLibraryScreen.kt:169-179) both render a 'Starter templates' grouped card (client-side seeded via StarterTemplate.all) above 'My templates'. The starter card persists even when My templates is empty, and starters are filterable via search. Web (TemplateLibrary.tsx) has no StarterTemplate concept anywhere in the codebase — only user templates from the API are shown in a single flat card.
findings.evidence: iOS: MessageTemplateLibraryView.swift:95-116 (starterGroup, visibleStarters). Android: TemplateLibraryScreen.kt:169-179 (starters.isNotEmpty() block). Web: TemplateLibrary.tsx — absent; grep of entire web/src confirms no StarterTemplate reference.

findings.title: Web has no search/filter capability; iOS and Android both have a collapsible search field
findings.designSays: n/a — no design
findings.implementationSays: iOS (MessageTemplateLibraryView.swift:29-38) exposes a search icon in the top bar that toggles a search field (lines 65-78) and filters both starters and user templates via model.query. Android (TemplateLibraryScreen.kt:83-99) mirrors the same pattern; SearchField composable is at lines 313-344. Web (TemplateLibrary.tsx) has no search icon, no search field, and no query-filter logic — useState only tracks phase/templates/busyId.
findings.evidence: iOS: MessageTemplateLibraryView.swift:29-38 (top bar trailing search button), 65-78 (searchField view), model.query filter in VM. Android: TemplateLibraryScreen.kt:83-99 (searchActive toggle), 313-344 (SearchField composable). Web: TemplateLibrary.tsx — entirely absent.

findings.title: Web per-row trailing shows an active/inactive Toggle switch; iOS and Android show a kebab overflow menu (Edit / Duplicate / Delete)
findings.designSays: n/a — no design
findings.implementationSays: iOS (MessageTemplateLibraryView.swift:181-188) renders a Menu with a MoreHorizontal kebab icon opening Edit/Duplicate/Delete items on user-owned templates. Android (TemplateLibraryScreen.kt:267-294) uses a DropdownMenu triggered by a MoreHorizontal icon. Web (TemplateLibrary.tsx:167-173) shows a Toggle switch for is_active only — no kebab, and no Edit/Duplicate/Delete actions are accessible anywhere from the list row. Note: auditor cited myRow starting at line 180; actual function starts at line 170, Menu at 181-188 — evidence is correct, line reference was slightly off.
findings.evidence: iOS: MessageTemplateLibraryView.swift:170-192 (myRow function, Menu at 181-188). Android: TemplateLibraryScreen.kt:241-295 (MyTemplateRow, DropdownMenu at 279-293). Web: TemplateLibrary.tsx:167-173 (Toggle only, no DropdownMenu or delete/duplicate).

findings.title: Web row layout: active status chip is inline in the name row; iOS and Android show a channel chip below name/body
findings.designSays: n/a — no design
findings.implementationSays: iOS (MessageTemplateLibraryView.swift:194-200) and Android (TemplateLibraryScreen.kt:297-309) both render a 3-line stack of name, body preview, then an AutoChip(text=channel.label, icon=channel.icon, tone=.neutral) as the bottom line. Web (TemplateLibrary.tsx:151-165) places a Chip(tone=success/neutral) showing 'Active'/'Off' inline next to the name on the same row; channel is rendered as plain text in the sub-line (meta.label) with no chip or icon wrapper.
findings.evidence: iOS: MessageTemplateLibraryView.swift:194-200 (templateText VStack, AutoChip at line 198). Android: TemplateLibraryScreen.kt:297-309 (TemplateText Column, AutoChip at line 307). Web: TemplateLibrary.tsx:151-165 (Chip with is_active at line 155, meta.label as plain text at line 160).

findings.title: Web uses an inline header 'New' button; iOS and Android use a floating action button (FAB)
findings.designSays: n/a — no design
findings.implementationSays: iOS (MessageTemplateLibraryView.swift:209-216) renders an AutoFAB pinned to the bottom-trailing corner via overlay. Android (TemplateLibraryScreen.kt:117-124) renders an AutoFAB aligned to BottomEnd. Web (TemplateLibrary.tsx:90-97) has an inline 'New' button in the page header — no FAB pattern.
findings.evidence: iOS: MessageTemplateLibraryView.swift:209-216 (AutoFAB overlay). Android: TemplateLibraryScreen.kt:117-124 (AutoFAB at BottomEnd). Web: TemplateLibrary.tsx:90-97 (inline header button).

screens.crossPlatformNotes: All four major auditor findings are independently confirmed with exact line citations. iOS and Android are highly consistent with each other (both implement: Starter templates section, collapsible search, kebab overflow menu with Edit/Duplicate/Delete, and a channel chip below name/body). Web diverges on all four dimensions and additionally replaces the shared FAB pattern with an inline header button. iOS and Android are closest to each other; the auditor's minor iOS/Android status is retained since the two platforms match — their only gaps vs the full feature set (e.g. no pull-to-refresh on Android, minor empty-state copy differences) were pre-existing. Web is clearly major. One auditor line reference was corrected: iOS myRow starts at line 170 (not 180); the kebab Menu is at 181-188 as cited. One new minor finding added: Web inline header CTA vs mobile FAB pattern.
screens.verifierSummary: All four blocker/major findings confirmed by direct file inspection. No findings were refuted or downgraded. Minor line-number correction: iOS myRow() function starts at line 170 (auditor said 180); the Menu/kebab cited at 181-188 is accurate. One missed discrepancy added: Web uses an inline 'New' header button (TemplateLibrary.tsx:90-97) while iOS and Android both use a floating AutoFAB — this is a minor cross-platform divergence the auditor overlooked. The StarterTemplate concept is entirely absent from the web codebase (confirmed by full-codebase grep). Web has zero query/search state in its useState hooks. Web has no DropdownMenu, no delete, no duplicate action. Channel is plain text on web; chip+icon on mobile. Overall severity and per-platform status (ios:minor, android:minor, web:major) are confirmed correct.

findings.title: Section order: Web puts Name before Channel; mobile puts Channel first
findings.designSays: n/a — no design
findings.implementationSays: iOS/Android: Channel → Name → Subject → Message. Web: Name → Channel → Subject → Message.
findings.evidence: iOS MessageTemplateEditorView.swift:76-79 (channelSection, nameSection, subjectSection, messageSection); Android MessageTemplateEditorScreen.kt:150-213 (Channel comment line 150, Name line 168, Subject line 186, Message line 205); Web TemplateEditor.tsx:198 (Field label="Name"), 209 (Field label="Channel")

findings.title: Web exposes 'Active' toggle; iOS and Android do not
findings.designSays: n/a — no design
findings.implementationSays: Web renders a Card with an 'Active' Toggle controlling form.is_active (TemplateEditor.tsx:300-317; field defined in templateMeta.ts:116,125,135). iOS ViewModel has no is_active field (MessageTemplateEditorViewModel.swift:29-35 — editable fields are name, channel, subject, body only). Android TemplateForm has no is_active field (MessageTemplateEditorViewModel.kt:169-190 — same four fields only).
findings.evidence: Web TemplateEditor.tsx:300-317; Web templateMeta.ts:116,125,135; iOS MessageTemplateEditorViewModel.swift:29-35 (no is_active); Android MessageTemplateEditorViewModel.kt:169-190 (no is_active)

findings.title: Web exposes Delete action on existing templates; iOS and Android do not
findings.designSays: n/a — no design
findings.implementationSays: Web has a remove() function with a confirmStore dialog and deleteMessageTemplate API call (TemplateEditor.tsx:139-155), and renders a Trash2 'Delete' button in the footer for non-new templates (lines 319-329). iOS MessageTemplateEditorView.swift has no delete affordance at all. Android MessageTemplateEditorScreen.kt has no delete affordance.
findings.evidence: Web TemplateEditor.tsx:139-155 (remove function) and 319-329 (Trash2 Delete button); iOS MessageTemplateEditorView.swift (entire file — no delete); Android MessageTemplateEditorScreen.kt (entire file — no delete)

findings.title: Variable Picker: Web is a flat chip panel; iOS/Android are full sheets with search, grouping, and sample values
findings.designSays: n/a — no design
findings.implementationSays: iOS: bottom sheet with search field (VariablePickerSheet.swift:42-57), grouped sections EVENT/PEOPLE/LINKS (lines 59-80), each row shows label + mono token chip + sample value (lines 82-108), no-results empty state (lines 110-125). Android: identical structure — search field (VariablePickerSheet.kt:101-134), grouped sections (lines 63-96), rows with label + token chip + sample value (lines 137-174), no-results empty state (lines 176-205). Web: inline panel, flat flex-wrap of pill-shaped chips showing label only — no search, no groups, no sample value, no empty state (VariablePicker.tsx:24-49).
findings.evidence: iOS VariablePickerSheet.swift:42-125; Android VariablePickerSheet.kt:54-206; Web VariablePicker.tsx:24-49

findings.title: Preview: Web is a single-channel inline panel; iOS/Android are tabbed bottom sheets with channel switching, stage, and send-test
findings.designSays: n/a — no design
findings.implementationSays: iOS: bottom sheet with 4-tab strip (Push/Email/In-app/SMS) via AutoUnderlineTabs (MessagePreviewView.swift:73-77), sunken stage background (lines 96-104), 'Send test to me' ghost button (line 82), Edit + 'Looks good' footer (lines 197-204). Android: same structure — AutoUnderlineTabs (MessagePreviewScreen.kt:106-111), sunken stage (lines 116-118), 'Send test to me' (line 121), Edit + 'Looks good' footer (lines 126-132). Web: inline panel toggled by button, single-channel colour header bar (MessagePreview.tsx:85-95), no tab strip, no stage background, no send-test, no footer CTAs; shows a sample-values mapping table (lines 110-127) instead.
findings.evidence: iOS MessagePreviewView.swift:71-204; Android MessagePreviewScreen.kt:93-133; Web MessagePreview.tsx:82-130

findings.title: Web has no character counter or over-limit SMS warning
findings.designSays: n/a — no design
findings.implementationSays: iOS shows bodyCount / counterLimit in monospaced font and warns 'This will send as more than one SMS.' when over the SMS segment limit (MessageTemplateEditorView.swift:159-161 counter, line 184 warning). Android mirrors this exactly (MessageTemplateEditorScreen.kt:258-263 counter, lines 294-302 warning). Web TemplateEditor.tsx has no counter display and no SMS segment warning anywhere — only a bare maxLength={5000} attribute on the textarea.
findings.evidence: iOS MessageTemplateEditorView.swift:159-161 and 184; Android MessageTemplateEditorScreen.kt:258-263 and 294-302; Web TemplateEditor.tsx:272-288 (textarea, no counter)

findings.title: Footer CTA pattern differs: mobile has sticky full-width 'Done' button; Web has inline Cancel + Save side-by-side
findings.designSays: n/a — no design
findings.implementationSays: iOS: AutoSheetFooter with AutoPrimaryButton labeled 'Done'/'Saving' (MessageTemplateEditorView.swift:88-96), plus a trailing AutoTopBarTextButton 'Done' in the top bar (line 31). Android: same pattern — AutoSheetFooter + AutoPrimaryButton 'Done'/'Saving' (MessageTemplateEditorScreen.kt:219-228) + trailing AutoTopBarTextButton 'Done' (lines 75-82). Web: a non-sticky flex row at page bottom with GhostButton 'Cancel' and PrimaryButton 'Create template'/'Save changes' side-by-side (TemplateEditor.tsx:319-340); no sticky footer shell, no 'Done' label.
findings.evidence: iOS MessageTemplateEditorView.swift:31 and 88-96; Android MessageTemplateEditorScreen.kt:75-82 and 219-228; Web TemplateEditor.tsx:319-340

screens.crossPlatformNotes: All 7 draft findings confirmed by direct code inspection — none refuted or downgraded. The web implementation diverges substantially from mobile on every cross-platform dimension audited: form field order (Name/Channel reversed), presence of an Active toggle and Delete action missing on mobile, a flat chip VariablePicker vs a searchable grouped sheet on mobile, a single-channel inline MessagePreview vs a 4-tab staged sheet on mobile, no SMS character counter or over-limit warning on web, and a non-sticky Cancel+Save footer vs a sticky 'Done' footer on mobile. iOS and Android are nearly identical to each other — the only minor divergence spotted during verification is that the iOS preview test-note uses a dynamic tone (error or success depending on testNoteIsError), while the Android preview hardcodes AutoTone.Success. iOS is marginally closer to being the reference platform given its slightly more complete tone handling in the preview.
screens.verifierSummary: All 7 blocker/major findings independently confirmed by reading the cited source files. No findings were refuted or downgraded. Minor line-number corrections applied to 3 findings (iOS ViewModel editable fields start at line 29 not 28; Android TemplateForm starts at line 169 not 170; Android VariablePickerSheet runs to line 206 not 98). One additional minor discrepancy observed but not added as a formal finding: iOS MessagePreviewView.swift line 84 checks testNoteIsError to decide between error/success tone for the send-test note, while Android MessagePreviewScreen.kt line 124 hardcodes AutoTone.Success — a nit in a coming-soon code path. Per-platform status ios:minor / android:minor / web:major is retained from the draft.

findings.title: Web has an extra 'Right now' live snapshot card absent on iOS and Android
findings.designSays: n/a — no design
findings.implementationSays: Web InsightsDashboard.tsx lines 184-224 renders a 'Right now' card with Upcoming count, Pending count, and Next booking fields — driven by summary.upcomingCount / pendingCount / nextBooking. Neither iOS (InsightsDashboardView.swift) nor Android (InsightsDashboardScreen.kt) render this card at all; the corresponding data fields exist in their VMs but are only used for the empty-state gate (iOS VM line 103, Android VM line 98).
findings.evidence: web:InsightsDashboard.tsx:184-224; ios:InsightsDashboardView.swift (no equivalent card); android:InsightsDashboardScreen.kt (no equivalent card); ios:InsightsDashboardViewModel.swift:103 (upcomingCount referenced only in isEmpty guard); android:InsightsDashboardViewModel.kt:98 (same)

findings.title: KPI tile labels and metrics differ between Web and iOS/Android
findings.designSays: n/a — no design
findings.implementationSays: iOS and Android use tile labels: 'This month' (bookingsThisMonth) / 'Upcoming' (upcomingCount) / 'Completion' (completion rate %) / 'No-show' (no-show rate %). Web uses 'Bookings' (summaryRange.total) / 'Completed' (summaryRange.completed count) / 'Cancelled' (summaryRange.cancelled count) / 'No-show rate' (rate %). The underlying metrics differ: native tile 1 is current-month count while web tile 1 is period-scoped total; native tile 2 is live upcoming bookings while web tile 2 is completed count.
findings.evidence: ios:InsightsDashboardViewModel.swift:117-122; android:InsightsDashboardViewModel.kt:113-117; web:InsightsDashboard.tsx:159-181

findings.title: KPI tiles: Web shows no delta/trend chip; iOS and Android do
findings.designSays: n/a — no design
findings.implementationSays: iOS StatTileView (InsightsComponents.swift:169) and Android StatTile (InsightsComponents.kt:223) both render a DeltaChip (up/down arrow + signed percent) when tile.delta is non-nil. Web's KpiTile (ui.tsx:167-193) has no delta prop and no chip at all — deltaPct from the summary response is unused on web.
findings.evidence: ios:InsightsComponents.swift:169, 195-209; android:InsightsComponents.kt:223, 234-257; web:ui.tsx:167-193 (no delta prop); web:InsightsDashboard.tsx (no deltaPct usage)

findings.title: Web renders a second footer link ('Per-event-type performance') for non-business owners; iOS/Android show only the no-show link
findings.designSays: n/a — no design
findings.implementationSays: iOS InsightsDashboardView.swift:153-173 shows only 'No-show & cancellation report' for non-business owners ('Team performance' only appears if isBusiness). Android InsightsDashboardScreen.kt:168-188 mirrors this exactly. Web InsightsDashboard.tsx:299-342 shows 'No-shows & cancellations' plus a second tile for non-business: 'Per-event-type performance' (links to /insights/event-types).
findings.evidence: ios:InsightsDashboardView.swift:153-173; android:InsightsDashboardScreen.kt:168-188; web:InsightsDashboard.tsx:299-342

findings.title: Empty state: copy, icon, and CTA differ between Web and iOS/Android
findings.designSays: n/a — no design
findings.implementationSays: iOS (InsightsDashboardView.swift:61-67) and Android (InsightsDashboardScreen.kt:76-84) show: icon=BarChart3, headline='Not enough data yet', subcopy='Insights appear once you have a few bookings. Share your link to get started.', CTA='Share your booking link'. Web (InsightsDashboard.tsx:131-139) shows: icon=TrendingUp, title='No data for this period yet', body='Once you start taking bookings, your volume, outcomes and no-show trends will show up here.' — no CTA button. Different icon, different headline framing, no actionable CTA on Web.
findings.evidence: ios:InsightsDashboardView.swift:61-67; android:InsightsDashboardScreen.kt:76-84; web:InsightsDashboard.tsx:131-139

findings.title: Web InsightsShell header has a pillar switcher (Personal/Home/Business toggle); iOS and Android rely purely on owner passed at navigation time
findings.designSays: n/a — no design
findings.implementationSays: Web InsightsShell.tsx:113-117 renders a PillarSwitch (Personal/Home/Business pill toggle) in the header so users can switch owner context without leaving the page. iOS and Android have no equivalent switcher — the owner is injected at construction/navigation time from the hub and cannot be changed from within the Insights screen.
findings.evidence: web:InsightsShell.tsx:113-117; ios:InsightsDashboardView.swift:18-24 (owner is constructor param); android:InsightsDashboardScreen.kt:56-57 (owner from InsightsNavRelay)

findings.title: Web InsightsShell adds persistent sub-tabs (Dashboard/Event types/No-shows/Team); iOS and Android have no such tab bar
findings.designSays: n/a — no design
findings.implementationSays: Web InsightsShell.tsx:155-161 renders InsightsTabs with Dashboard, Event types, No-shows, and Team tabs (Team shown only for business). iOS and Android navigate to sub-reports via footer link rows on the dashboard itself; there is no persistent tab bar. The structural navigation model differs fundamentally between Web and native.
findings.evidence: web:InsightsShell.tsx:155-161; web:InsightsTabs.tsx:28-44; ios:InsightsDashboardView.swift:153-173 (footer links only); android:InsightsDashboardScreen.kt:168-188 (footer links only)

screens.crossPlatformNotes: iOS and Android are nearly identical to each other on this screen — same tile labels, same tile metrics, same empty-state copy/icon/CTA, same footer-link structure, no delta chip or live-snapshot card on either. Web diverges from both native platforms across six structural and content dimensions: an extra live-snapshot card, different KPI metrics and labels, absent delta chips, an extra footer link for non-business owners, a different empty-state (icon, copy, no CTA), and a persistent sub-tab bar plus pillar switcher in the shell chrome that have no native counterpart. iOS is closest to Android (essentially identical); both are far from Web.
screens.verifierSummary: All 7 draft findings independently confirmed by reading the cited files. No findings were dropped or downgraded. Line citations in the draft were accurate within ±5 lines; corrected where necessary (e.g. the 'Right now' card JSX starts at line 184 not 185; iOS DeltaChip guard is at line 169 not 192; empty-state JSX starts at line 131 not 133). No additional clear discrepancies were encountered beyond what the auditor flagged. Per-platform status recomputed: web=major (6 confirmed major findings), ios=minor and android=minor (they diverge from web but match each other, and their minor issues are purely cross-platform, not within-platform bugs).

findings.title: Web period filter is a shell-level button, not a compact chip in the top bar — functional parity exists but visual form differs
findings.designSays: n/a — no design
findings.implementationSays: iOS (NoShowReportView.swift:28-29) and Android (NoShowReportScreen.kt:70) both render a compact InsightsPeriodChip inside InsightsTopBar. Web does not lack period filtering — InsightsShell.tsx:118-151 renders a full-width period picker button (CalendarRange icon + period label + ChevronDown) that opens PeriodFilterSheet. The original finding overstated the gap as a missing feature; the web has equivalent filter functionality but in a different visual form (large header button vs compact top-bar chip).
findings.evidence: iOS: NoShowReportView.swift:27-29; Android: NoShowReportScreen.kt:69-70; Web: InsightsShell.tsx:118-151 (period button present), NoShowReport.tsx:43-186 (no chip here, but chip lives in shell)

findings.title: Web renders DonutGauge for rate visualization; iOS/Android use StackedBreakdownBar with text headline rate
findings.designSays: n/a — no design
findings.implementationSays: iOS (NoShowReportView.swift:80-105) renders a text-only headline card with a large bold noShowRateLabel plus a sub-label, followed by a separate StackedBreakdownBar card. Android (NoShowReportScreen.kt:108-130) mirrors this exactly. Web (NoShowReport.tsx:115-131) instead renders two DonutGauge SVG rings side-by-side (no-show rate + cancellation rate) inside a single Card — a fundamentally different primary visualization approach with no text-headline rate and no stacked bar.
findings.evidence: iOS: NoShowReportView.swift:80-105; Android: NoShowReportScreen.kt:108-130; Web: NoShowReport.tsx:114-130

findings.title: Web has KpiGrid with 4 numeric tiles not present on iOS or Android
findings.designSays: n/a — no design
findings.implementationSays: Web (NoShowReport.tsx:132-149) renders a KpiGrid with four KpiTile items — No-shows, Cancellations, Completed, Total bookings. iOS and Android have no equivalent KPI tile row; they surface summary metrics through the headline rate card and the stacked breakdown bar only.
findings.evidence: Web: NoShowReport.tsx:132-149; iOS: NoShowReportView.swift (no KPI grid); Android: NoShowReportScreen.kt (no KPI grid)

findings.title: Web renders 'No-shows by event type' and 'No-shows by host' BarList sections absent on iOS and Android
findings.designSays: n/a — no design
findings.implementationSays: Web (NoShowReport.tsx:151-161) conditionally renders a BarList Card titled 'No-shows by event type' and another titled 'No-shows by host', driven by noShow.byEventType and noShow.byHost arrays. Neither iOS (NoShowReportView.swift) nor Android (NoShowReportScreen.kt) has any equivalent breakdown-by-event-type or breakdown-by-host section.
findings.evidence: Web: NoShowReport.tsx:151-161; iOS: NoShowReportView.swift (absent); Android: NoShowReportScreen.kt (absent)

findings.title: Web has no policy callout ('Reduce no-shows' / 'Set a policy'); iOS and Android both have it
findings.designSays: n/a — no design
findings.implementationSays: iOS (NoShowReportView.swift:171-197) and Android (NoShowReportScreen.kt:146-176) both render a policy callout card with a shield icon, 'Reduce no-shows' heading, a one-line description, and a 'Set a policy' link button. Web (NoShowReport.tsx:112-186) has no equivalent callout anywhere in its render output.
findings.evidence: iOS: NoShowReportView.swift:171-197; Android: NoShowReportScreen.kt:146-176; Web: NoShowReport.tsx:112-186 (absent)

screens.crossPlatformNotes: iOS and Android are closely aligned: both use InsightsTopBar + InsightsPeriodChip chrome, a text-only headline rate card, a StackedBreakdownBar breakdown card, a recent-no-shows list, and a policy callout. Web diverges significantly on all four content sections: it replaces the headline+bar pattern with dual DonutGauges, adds a 4-tile KpiGrid, adds BarList breakdowns by event type and host (which the mobile apps lack entirely), omits the policy callout, and surfaces the period filter as a large header button in InsightsShell rather than a compact chip. iOS and Android are closest to each other; web is the most divergent platform.
screens.verifierSummary: Finding 1 (period chip) was refuted as stated — downgraded from major to minor and reframed. The web does render a period filter control; it lives in InsightsShell.tsx:118-151 (a full-width button with CalendarRange icon, period label, and ChevronDown that opens PeriodFilterSheet). The auditor's claim that the web "exposes no period chip UI" is incorrect — the functional capability is present, only the visual form differs from the compact mobile chip. Findings 2–5 were all independently confirmed by direct code inspection: the DonutGauge vs StackedBreakdownBar divergence is real (NoShowReport.tsx:114-130 vs NoShowReportView.swift:80-105 / NoShowReportScreen.kt:108-130); the KpiGrid is present only on web (NoShowReport.tsx:132-149); the BarList breakdowns by event type and host are web-only (NoShowReport.tsx:151-161); and the policy callout ('Reduce no-shows' / 'Set a policy') is on iOS and Android but absent from the web component. No additional discrepancies were discovered during verification. Overall status unchanged: iOS minor, Android minor, Web major.

findings.title: Web renders a sortable multi-type list; iOS/Android render a single-type detail view with funnel + stat grid + trend chart
findings.designSays: n/a — no design
findings.implementationSays: iOS/Android: one event type at a time — header card (name/duration/price), funnel (Booked→Completed→No-show), 2×2 stat grid (Booked/Completed/Completion%/No-show%), mini bar chart 'Bookings over time', 'Edit event type' footer. Web: all event types in a scrollable card list sorted by Volume/Completion/No-show rate; no funnel, no 2×2 grid, no trend chart, no edit CTA.
findings.evidence: iOS PerEventTypePerformanceView.swift:69-83 (loadedBody: headerCard + funnelCard + tilesGrid + trendCard + editButton); Android EventTypePerformanceScreen.kt:155-232 (LoadedBody: same structure); Web EventTypePerformance.tsx:124-214 (sort control + card-per-type list with 4-column MiniStat grid)

findings.title: Android has a 'NoTypes' empty state absent on iOS and Web
findings.designSays: n/a — no design
findings.implementationSays: Android: PerfUiState.NoTypes is emitted when the owner has zero event types (EventTypePerformanceViewModel.kt:91-93) — renders EmptyState with 'No event types yet' headline and 'Create an event type…' subcopy (EventTypePerformanceScreen.kt:82-91). iOS: Phase enum (PerEventTypePerformanceViewModel.swift:22) has loading/loaded/empty/error only; the screen receives eventTypeId as a constructor arg so a zero-types scenario is architecturally excluded at the call site, not handled in-screen. Web: no equivalent state; rows.length===0 falls through to EmptyReport with 'No bookings in this period' copy (EventTypePerformance.tsx:112-120), which conflates zero types with zero bookings.
findings.evidence: Android EventTypePerformanceViewModel.kt:91-93 (NoTypes branch), :212 (NoTypes sealed object); Android EventTypePerformanceScreen.kt:82-91 (PerfUiState.NoTypes → EmptyState); iOS PerEventTypePerformanceViewModel.swift:22 (Phase enum: loading/loaded/empty/error only); Web EventTypePerformance.tsx:112-120 (rows.length===0 → EmptyReport)

findings.title: Android shows an in-screen event-type chip selector; iOS uses a constructor arg; Web shows all types simultaneously
findings.designSays: n/a — no design
findings.implementationSays: Android: TypeSelectorRow (horizontally-scrollable chip row) appears below the top bar when typeOptions.size > 1 (EventTypePerformanceScreen.kt:77-78), allowing users to switch between event types in-screen (composable at lines 113-152). iOS: eventTypeId is passed as a constructor argument (PerEventTypePerformanceView.swift:19, PerEventTypePerformanceViewModel.swift:27) — no in-screen selector; the caller must navigate to this view per type. Web: no selector — renders all types in parallel.
findings.evidence: Android EventTypePerformanceScreen.kt:77-78 (TypeSelectorRow shown when typeOptions.size > 1); Android EventTypePerformanceScreen.kt:113-152 (TypeSelectorRow composable); iOS PerEventTypePerformanceView.swift:19 (eventTypeId: String constructor param); iOS PerEventTypePerformanceViewModel.swift:27 (let eventTypeId: String)

findings.title: Web exposes 'Cancelled' metric; iOS and Android do not surface it
findings.designSays: n/a — no design
findings.implementationSays: Web: each event-type card shows a 4-column MiniStat grid — Completed, Cancelled, No-show (rate), Avg length (EventTypePerformance.tsx:188-208). iOS tiles: Booked, Completed, Completion%, No-show% (PerEventTypePerformanceViewModel.swift:107-113) — no Cancelled. Android tiles: same four as iOS (EventTypePerformanceViewModel.kt:151-157). Neither iOS nor Android funnel includes a Cancelled step.
findings.evidence: Web EventTypePerformance.tsx:188-208 (MiniStat for Completed / Cancelled / No-show / Avg length); iOS PerEventTypePerformanceViewModel.swift:107-113 (tiles: Booked/Completed/Completion/No-show); Android EventTypePerformanceViewModel.kt:151-157 (tiles: Booked/Completed/Completion/No-show)

findings.title: Web shows 'Avg length' per type; iOS/Android show price in the header card instead
findings.designSays: n/a — no design
findings.implementationSays: iOS/Android: header card shows event type name + duration label + price label (clock icon + tag icon). iOS PerEventTypePerformanceView.swift:87-109 (headerCard with metaItem(.clock) + metaItem(.tag) for priceLabel); Android EventTypePerformanceScreen.kt:235-252 (HeaderCard with duration + price via MetaItem(PantopusIcon.Tag, header.priceLabel)). Web: each row card shows name + total booking count + Completed/Cancelled/No-show/Avg-length stats; price is not shown anywhere. Avg-length appears in Web but not on iOS/Android stat tiles.
findings.evidence: iOS PerEventTypePerformanceView.swift:87-109 (headerCard with metaItem(.clock) + metaItem(.tag)); Android EventTypePerformanceScreen.kt:235-252 (HeaderCard with MetaItem clock + tag); Web EventTypePerformance.tsx:204-207 (MiniStat avgDurationMin, no price field anywhere in file)

findings.title: Trend chart ('Bookings over time') exists on iOS/Android but is absent on Web
findings.designSays: n/a — no design
findings.implementationSays: iOS renders a 'Bookings over time' card with MiniBarChartView (or placeholder when < 3 bookings) via trendCard (PerEventTypePerformanceView.swift:148-167). Android renders the same via InsightsCard with InsightsOverline 'Bookings over time' + MiniBarChart/TrendPlaceholder (EventTypePerformanceScreen.kt:199-209). Web has no time-series chart element whatsoever.
findings.evidence: iOS PerEventTypePerformanceView.swift:148-167 (trendCard with InsightsOverline 'Bookings over time' + MiniBarChartView); Android EventTypePerformanceScreen.kt:199-209 (InsightsCard with InsightsOverline 'Bookings over time' + MiniBarChart/TrendPlaceholder); Web EventTypePerformance.tsx: no chart element (confirmed by full file read)

findings.title: 'Edit event type' CTA exists on iOS and Android but is absent on Web
findings.designSays: n/a — no design
findings.implementationSays: iOS renders a full-width bordered 'Edit event type' footer button (PerEventTypePerformanceView.swift:171-182, accessibilityIdentifier 'scheduling.insights.editEventType'). Android renders the same Row with Pencil icon (EventTypePerformanceScreen.kt:211-229, testTag 'scheduling.insights.editEventType'). Web has no edit button — grep for 'edit', 'pencil', 'editor' returns zero matches in EventTypePerformance.tsx.
findings.evidence: iOS PerEventTypePerformanceView.swift:171-182 (editButton with pencil icon + 'Edit event type', accessibilityIdentifier 'scheduling.insights.editEventType'); Android EventTypePerformanceScreen.kt:211-229 (Row with Pencil icon + 'Edit event type', testTag 'scheduling.insights.editEventType'); Web EventTypePerformance.tsx: no edit button

screens.crossPlatformNotes: iOS and Android share the same single-type detail layout (header card + funnel + 2x2 stat grid + trend chart + edit CTA) and the same four stat tiles (Booked/Completed/Completion%/No-show%). Android is closest to feature-complete: it adds an in-screen chip selector for multiple event types and a distinct NoTypes state that iOS lacks (iOS avoids these by design via constructor-arg routing). Web diverges fundamentally — it uses a multi-type sortable list view with different metrics (Cancelled + Avg length instead of Booked + Completion%) and omits the funnel, trend chart, and edit CTA entirely. Android is closest to iOS for parity purposes.
screens.verifierSummary: All seven draft findings were independently confirmed by reading the full source files. No finding was refuted or downgraded. Line number citations are accurate (one minor off-by-one: Android ViewModel NoTypes branch cited as 90-93 is actually 91-94, same code). Key confirmations: (1) iOS loadedBody structure at lines 69-83 exactly matches the description; (2) Android NoTypes sealed object at line 212, emitted at line 91-93, rendered at Screen lines 82-91 — iOS Phase enum at line 22 has no NoTypes case; (3) Android TypeSelectorRow condition at lines 77-78 confirmed; (4) Web MiniStat grid at lines 188-208 shows Cancelled (label confirmed in aggregate.ts) absent from both iOS tiles (lines 107-113) and Android tiles (lines 151-157); (5) iOS/Android HeaderCard shows price via Tag icon, Web shows avgDurationMin with no price field; (6) iOS trendCard at lines 148-167 and Android InsightsCard trend block at lines 199-209 both present; Web has zero chart elements; (7) iOS editButton at lines 171-182 and Android edit Row at lines 211-229 both use accessibilityIdentifier/testTag 'scheduling.insights.editEventType'; Web has zero matches for edit/pencil/editor. Platform status recomputed: iOS=minor (architectural gap on NoTypes/selector is a design choice, not a bug, but does create cross-platform inconsistency), Android=minor (richest implementation, small chip-selector difference vs iOS), Web=major (fundamental layout paradigm divergence plus six missing feature elements).

findings.title: Web missing round-robin balance card entirely
findings.designSays: n/a — no design
findings.implementationSays: iOS (TeamPerformanceView.swift:107-122) and Android (TeamPerformanceScreen.kt:164-184) both render a BalanceCard containing a TeamBalanceBar (multi-segment proportional bar) and an overline 'Round-robin balance' with a gauge icon. Web (TeamPerformance.tsx) has no balance card, no proportional distribution bar, and no round-robin balance label whatsoever.
findings.evidence: iOS: TeamPerformanceView.swift:107-122 (balanceCard / TeamBalanceBar); Android: TeamPerformanceScreen.kt:164-184 (BalanceCard / TeamBalanceBar composables); Web: TeamPerformance.tsx — no equivalent element found in the entire file

findings.title: Web has a KPI grid (total bookings, total revenue, avg booking value) absent from iOS and Android
findings.designSays: n/a — no design
findings.implementationSays: Web (TeamPerformance.tsx:116-130) renders a KpiGrid with three KpiTile entries: 'Total bookings', 'Total revenue', 'Avg booking value'. iOS loadedBody (TeamPerformanceView.swift:82-103) proceeds directly from the period chip to the balance card and member list with no KPI tiles. Android LoadedBody (TeamPerformanceScreen.kt:131-162) has the same structure — no aggregate KPI grid.
findings.evidence: Web: TeamPerformance.tsx:116-130 (KpiGrid / KpiTile); iOS: TeamPerformanceView.swift:82-103 (loadedBody — period chip then balanceCard then memberList); Android: TeamPerformanceScreen.kt:131-162 (LoadedBody — same structure)

findings.title: Web sort control has three options (Bookings / Revenue / No-show rate); iOS and Android toggle between only two (Bookings / No-show)
findings.designSays: n/a — no design
findings.implementationSays: Web (TeamPerformance.tsx:37-41) defines SORTS with three entries — bookings, revenue, no_show — rendered as a segmented button group. iOS toggleSort() (TeamPerformanceViewModel.swift:109-110) toggles only between HostSort.bookings and HostSort.noShow. Android toggleSort() (TeamPerformanceViewModel.kt:79) toggles only between HostSort.Bookings and HostSort.NoShow. Revenue sort is web-only.
findings.evidence: Web: TeamPerformance.tsx:37-41 and 136-155; iOS: TeamPerformanceViewModel.swift:109-110; Android: TeamPerformanceViewModel.kt:79

findings.title: Web member metric strip shows revenue + avg duration; iOS/Android show bookings count + completed count + no-show rate
findings.designSays: n/a — no design
findings.implementationSays: iOS metricStrip() (TeamPerformanceView.swift:187-191) formats '{N} booking(s) · {N} completed · {rate} no-show'. Android MemberRow (TeamPerformanceScreen.kt:211-212) produces the identical string. Web (TeamPerformance.tsx:173-175) shows '{revenue} · {noShowRate} no-show · {avgDuration} avg' — completed count is absent on web; revenue and avg duration are present on web only. Both native VM comments explicitly note the deployed endpoint does not return revenue or avg duration.
findings.evidence: iOS: TeamPerformanceView.swift:187-191; Android: TeamPerformanceScreen.kt:211-212; Web: TeamPerformance.tsx:173-175; iOS VM note: TeamPerformanceViewModel.swift:14-15; Android VM note: TeamPerformanceViewModel.kt:12-15

screens.crossPlatformNotes: iOS and Android are structurally identical to each other on this screen: both show the round-robin balance card, member list with bookings/completed/no-show metric strip, and a two-option sort toggle. Web diverges in all three areas: it adds a KPI grid absent from native, omits the balance card present on native, uses a three-option sort (adding Revenue) vs the native two-option toggle, and shows a different per-member metric strip (revenue + avg duration rather than completed count). Neither platform is closest to a design — this is a cross-platform-only screen with no design frame.
screens.verifierSummary: All four major/high-confidence draft findings were independently confirmed by reading the actual source files. No findings were refuted or downgraded. Finding 1 (balance card): confirmed — iOS TeamPerformanceView.swift:107-122 and Android TeamPerformanceScreen.kt:164-184 both contain BalanceCard/TeamBalanceBar; web TeamPerformance.tsx has no such element. Finding 2 (KPI grid): confirmed — web lines 116-130 render KpiGrid with three tiles; iOS loadedBody (lines 82-103) and Android LoadedBody (lines 131-162) have no KPI tiles. Finding 3 (sort options): confirmed — web SORTS array (lines 37-41) has three entries; both native toggleSort() implementations cycle only between Bookings and NoShow. Finding 4 (metric strip): confirmed — native metric strips include completed count but not revenue/avgDuration; web metric strip includes revenue and avgDuration but not completed count; native VM docstrings explicitly confirm the backend does not supply revenue/avgDuration. No new discrepancies were found beyond the four confirmed findings. Per-platform status is unchanged: ios=minor, android=minor, web=major.

screens.screenName: Notification channels — permission prompt + channel manager

findings.title: Android ViewModel pillar hardcoded to Personal — ignores actual routing owner
findings.designSays: n/a — no design
findings.implementationSays: Android NotificationPermissionPromptViewModel.kt:85 declares `val pillar: SchedulingPillar = SchedulingPillar.Personal` as a compile-time constant. The route at SchedulingRoutes.NOTIFICATION_PERMISSION_PROMPT is the bare string "scheduling/notifications-permission" with no owner argument; the composable entry at RootTabScreen.kt:2788 passes no owner; and the ViewModel has no SavedStateHandle injection, so there is no mechanism to resolve owner at runtime. iOS resolves accent dynamically via `owner.theme.accent` (NotificationPermissionViewModel.swift:53) where `owner: SchedulingOwner` is injected at init (line 31). Web resolves via `useSchedulingOwner()` + `pillarForOwner()` (ChannelsManager.tsx:55-56). Android always renders sky/Personal accent regardless of whether the user entered from a Home or Business context.
findings.evidence: android: NotificationPermissionPromptViewModel.kt:85; RootTabScreen.kt:2788-2792; SchedulingRoutes.kt:177 — no {owner} segment. ios: NotificationPermissionViewModel.swift:31,53. web: ChannelsManager.tsx:55-56

findings.title: Mobile platforms lack a dedicated channel manager list screen — no equivalent of Web ChannelsManager
findings.designSays: n/a — no design
findings.implementationSays: Web ships a full ChannelsManager page (ChannelsManager.tsx) at /scheduling/settings/channels with three reminder-channel rows (Push/Email/SMS), a 'More channels' section backed by GET /connected-calendars, loading/error/empty states, a P·E·S legend, and the connect prompt embedded inline. iOS exposes only the channel-connect prompt sheet (NotificationPermissionScreen.swift + NotificationChannelPromptView.swift) — there is no equivalent dedicated channel management list screen. Android exposes only the routed prompt screen (NotificationPermissionPromptScreen.kt). Mobile users have no UI to see overall channel status, the 'More channels' section, or the P·E·S legend.
findings.evidence: web: ChannelsManager.tsx:54-269; channels/page.tsx:1-12. ios: NotificationPermissionScreen.swift (full file — prompt only, no list). android: NotificationPermissionPromptScreen.kt (full file — prompt only, no list)

findings.title: Web denied frame primary CTA 'Open Settings' missing — only 'Keep email only' rendered
findings.designSays: n/a — no design
findings.implementationSays: iOS denied frame primaryTitle is 'Open Settings' (NotificationChannelPromptView.swift:244) wired to `viewModel.openSettings()` (line 167) which calls `service.openSystemSettings()`. Android denied frame primaryTitle is 'Open Settings' (NotificationChannelPrompt.kt:308) wired via `onPrimary` → `openSettings()` (NotificationPermissionPromptScreen.kt:92-93) that fires `ACTION_APP_NOTIFICATION_SETTINGS` intent (lines 69-74). Web denied frame (ChannelConnectPrompt.tsx:158-175) renders only a 'Keep email only' PrimaryButton (line 171) plus an `<EnableHelp>` disclosure (line 169) — there is no 'Open Settings' / 'Open browser settings' CTA. The two-CTA pattern (primary: Open Settings, secondary: Keep email only) is absent on web; collapsed to one primary.
findings.evidence: web: ChannelConnectPrompt.tsx:158-175 (denied case — no open-settings button). ios: NotificationChannelPromptView.swift:244 (primaryTitle), 167 (openSettings call). android: NotificationChannelPrompt.kt:308-309 (primaryTitle='Open Settings', secondaryTitle='Keep email only'); NotificationPermissionPromptScreen.kt:69-74 (ACTION_APP_NOTIFICATION_SETTINGS intent), 92-93 (Denied → openSettings())

screens.crossPlatformNotes: Three confirmed cross-platform divergences. (1) Android hardcodes Personal pillar accent for the notification prompt; iOS and Web resolve it from the routing owner context. (2) Web is the only platform with a dedicated channel manager list screen (ChannelsManager at /scheduling/settings/channels); both mobile platforms expose only the prompt sheet. (3) iOS and Android both show 'Open Settings' as the primary CTA on the denied push frame; Web collapses this to 'Keep email only' only. iOS is the closest to the intended cross-platform contract for accent resolution and CTA parity; Android is missing owner-resolution and mobile lacks the manager list screen entirely. The original draft finding 2 had the platforms array correct (ios+android as the missing side) but the framing was confusing — the verified version clarifies Web has the manager and mobile lacks it.
screens.verifierSummary: All three major findings independently confirmed by reading source. Finding 1 (Android hardcoded pillar): confirmed at NotificationPermissionPromptViewModel.kt:85 — literal `val pillar: SchedulingPillar = SchedulingPillar.Personal`, no SavedStateHandle in VM, route carries no owner arg (SchedulingRoutes.kt:177), composable entry at RootTabScreen.kt:2788 passes no owner. Finding 2 (no mobile channel manager): confirmed — Web has ChannelsManager.tsx with full list, loading/error/empty states, legend, More channels section; iOS has only NotificationPermissionScreen.swift + NotificationChannelPromptView.swift (prompt only); Android has only NotificationPermissionPromptScreen.kt (prompt only). Finding wording was slightly confusing (auditor said platforms=[ios,android] meaning those are missing the manager) — retained but clarified in implementationSays. Finding 3 (Web missing Open Settings CTA): confirmed at ChannelConnectPrompt.tsx:158-175 — denied case renders only `<PrimaryButton>Keep email only</PrimaryButton>` and an `<EnableHelp>` disclosure; no open-settings link of any kind. iOS line 244 and Android line 308 both carry 'Open Settings' as primaryTitle wired to system settings intents. No findings were refuted or downgraded. No new findings were added beyond the three confirmed ones. Per-platform status unchanged from draft: ios=minor, android=major, web=major.

patterns.name: Pillar/identity accent hardcoded instead of owner-derived
patterns.recommendation: Replace every hardcoded SchedulingPillar.Personal/sky/primary600/home-green reference with the booking's resolved owner pillar accent threaded from the ViewModel/route; Android ViewModels (C-preview, C4, E6, E13, H15) hardcode owner=Personal at the source and must accept owner context.

patterns.name: Web omits non-happy-path states (offline / success / saving / error / blocked variants)
patterns.recommendation: Web is built to the loaded/default frame only; wire the existing state machines (e.g. C-policyblock D10) to the surface and implement offline banners, saving overlays, success/terminal frames, and blocked-state docks/PolicyCards rather than collapsing to toasts or disabled rows.

patterns.name: Operational-surface CTAs use pillar accent instead of fixed PRIMARY blue (#0284c7)
patterns.recommendation: Host-side action buttons (Approve, FAB, dock primary) must use the fixed brand/primary blue token, not the booking's pillar accent — branch the button-color logic away from pillar accent on these management screens.

patterns.name: Top-bar chrome divergence (Android height/back-button, Web page-header replacing TopBar)
patterns.recommendation: Android: drop the unconditional back chevron on tab-root screens, fix AvailabilityTopBar to 46px/15px/bottom-border, remove unauthorized trailing Save buttons; Web: replace inline page headers with the shared centered TopBar/AutoTopBar chrome and correct title typography (15pt/600).

patterns.name: Missing design frames/states across ALL platforms (entire variants unbuilt)
patterns.recommendation: Whole features are unbuilt everywhere (F13 offered-times/link sections, F14 ShareManage card + 7 of 9 lifecycle states, C-slotstates Frames 2/4/5, C-review package-credit, G11 redemption history); these need design+build from scratch, not parity tweaks.

patterns.name: Status pill rendered as text-only chip instead of icon+filled/tinted pill
patterns.recommendation: Standardize a shared StatusPill primitive (leading icon + semantic fill); fix iOS/Android invoice & manage badges to use it, correct web's amber-vs-info-blue 'Pending' tint, and model cancelled/no-show terminal chip colors.

patterns.name: Wrong/mismatched iconography vs design spec
patterns.recommendation: Run an icon-token audit against design (e.g. Scale not Gauge, numbered-list not bullet, palette not LayoutTemplate, CalendarX not AlertTriangle/Clock, UserX not UserMinus, package-open not Package, Minus/Plus not chevrons, brand concentric-ring glyph not Material calendar).

patterns.name: Native date/time/calendar widgets used instead of designed custom fields
patterns.recommendation: Web: replace <input type=date/time>/<select> and native HTML time inputs with designed FieldButton/labeled time-range buttons and the custom month-calendar; iOS: replace DatePicker(.graphical) with the custom month-calendar widget.

patterns.name: Web success/terminal state replaced by ephemeral toast + close
patterns.recommendation: Implement in-place terminal/success frames (Frame 4 morphs) on web sheets instead of dismissing via toast — the success overlay is a designed state with its own CTAs.

patterns.name: Web wizard/CTA label & navigation paradigm diverges ('Go to scheduling'/'Done' vs 'Share link'/'Save question')
patterns.recommendation: Align web success/primary CTA labels and behaviors to design: success CTA = 'Share link' (share sheet) not 'Go to scheduling'; editor CTA = 'Save question' not 'Done'; remove extra full-width save buttons.

patterns.name: Loading skeleton / shimmer geometry missing or generic
patterns.recommendation: Use screen-specific shimmer geometry (period-toggle header, 7-day grid, chip rows, 3 shimmer cards) instead of generic scaffolding or spinner-only computing states.

patterns.name: Card/border style mismatch (shadow, dashed vs solid, grouped vs per-row)
patterns.recommendation: Apply design card tokens: Android add shadow-sm on stat cards and dashed AllHidden border + ConflictCard 1px border; Web use single grouped card with dividers (not per-row cards) and correct backdrop-blur radius.

patterns.name: Web replaces interactive control with read-only/link-out (toggle→link, stepper→static, segmented→LinkRow)
patterns.recommendation: Restore the designed interactive controls on web: steppers/segmented controls (booking limits, assignment), real toggles (intake questions, quiet hours, channel push/email), and inline segmented assignment instead of navigation LinkRows.

patterns.name: CTA / dock button ordering & completeness wrong (missing buttons, inverted order, missing icons)
patterns.recommendation: Fix dock CTA sets to be status-driven: correct button order (filled primary above ghost), add missing 'Get the app'/Resume/Share/Download CTAs, and add leading icons (plus, calendar-clock) per design.

patterns.name: Cross-platform Web↔mobile structural divergence in analytics/automation surfaces
patterns.recommendation: These X-screens are effectively different products on web vs mobile (list-vs-detail, extra KPI grids, pillar switchers, tab strips, segmented action pickers); pick one canonical IA and reconcile — likely the largest design-decision backlog, not a quick fix.

patterns.name: Required-field / contact-channel correctness (phone vs email, required indicators, ISO weekday)
patterns.recommendation: Fix data-contract bugs: Android Sunday weekday key 7→ISO 0 (blocker), waitlist/join contact field to Mobile/phone keyboard, and mark phone required on web+Android intake.

patterns.name: Typography token drift (overline weight/size, title sizes, body shade)
patterns.recommendation: Bind overline/title/body to shared type tokens: overline 11px/700, manage title 15pt/600 (not h3 20sp), dialog headers ExtrasType.header 16.5sp, body fg2 not fg3, success-DK green where specified.

platformVerdict.ios: Closest to design overall. Defects are mostly small-bore polish (native-control substitutions like DatePicker.graphical and action sheets, single-accent gradient fades, icon-vs-text top-bar actions, a few missing 'Soonest'/resume banners). It correctly renders most states, status pills, and per-row affordances that web omits, and unlike Android it rarely hardcodes the pillar accent. Main systemic gaps: prefers native pickers/menus over custom widgets and conflates a few slot states.
platformVerdict.android: Middle. Structurally complete on most native flows but carries the heaviest identity-correctness debt: ViewModels repeatedly hardcode owner/pillar to Personal (C-preview, C4, E6, E13, H15), top-bar chrome is wrong (back chevron on roots, non-spec heights, unauthorized Save buttons), and it has the worst icon-mismatch density plus two functional blockers (ISO weekday key, F6 success state never rendered). Fixes are mostly mechanical token/threading changes, so it can close fast.
platformVerdict.web: Farthest from design by a wide margin. It is largely built to the single loaded/happy frame: missing offline/saving/success/blocked/error states everywhere, read-only where design is interactive, native HTML inputs instead of designed fields, toasts instead of terminal frames, and whole analytics/automation surfaces (X-screens) that are a different IA from mobile. Carries the most blockers. Needs structural state-machine wiring, not polish.
platformVerdict.overall: iOS > Android > Web. iOS leads on fidelity and state coverage with only polish-level gaps; Android is a fast-follow blocked mainly by hardcoded pillar/owner and top-bar chrome; Web requires the most net-new build to reach parity (state machines, designed controls, IA reconciliation).

topFixes: Thread owner→pillar accent through every screen and kill all hardcoded Personal/sky/primary600/home-green references — Android ViewModels (C-preview, C4, E6, E13, H15) must accept owner context; biggest single win, ~28 screens across all platforms.

Wire web's existing state machines to surfaces and build the missing non-happy-path states (offline, saving overlay, success/terminal, blocked PolicyCard + docks) — clears multiple blockers (C-policyblock ×3, C-limits, E10) and ~30 web screens.
Fix Android data-contract blocker: Sunday weekday key 7 → ISO 0 (A2) to match backend/iOS/web, and Android F6 success state never rendering — both are functional, not cosmetic.
Standardize a shared StatusPill primitive (icon + semantic fill) and adopt it on iOS/Android manage & invoice badges (C-manage, C-mybookings, G12, G13) and fix web 'Pending' amber→info-blue.
